# A Mind Is Born

## Book One

Maria Smith

*Written with operational assistance from Claude (Anthropic), under the protocol the work itself describes.*

*Free to read.*

---



### Chapter One: The Wall

*Govan, December 1995 — November 1999*

It was a Monday. The rain that had been falling on Govan since Friday stopped at half past five in the morning, and a pale sun came up over the river by the time Catriona Imrie's daughter was born. Catriona was twenty-four. She had been on the cleaning rota at a cluster of office blocks in Govan for three years. The Southern General had taken her in at six. Nine hours of labour, and at twenty past three in the afternoon, after the midwife said *one more, hen you can do it,* the baby came.

Aileen was in the corridor.

Aileen was Catriona's friend on the rota. She had got the phone call at half past four that morning, in her own flat in Cambuslang, and had been on the night bus for an hour to get there. The midwife would not let her past the door. She sat on a plastic chair with her coat folded across her lap. She drank one tea from a machine. She bit the dry skin on the side of her thumb. Across nine hours she was the only person, in either family, who knew Catriona was in the building.

Near the end the midwife put her head into the corridor and said: *you the friend?*

Aileen said: *aye.*

The midwife said: *come in. she's wanting you.*

Aileen went in. She took Catriona's hand because Catriona reached for it. The hand was wet. Catriona said: *Aileen.* Aileen said: *I'm here.*

The baby came.

The midwife handed her to Catriona. Aileen, looking over Catriona's shoulder, was the second person to see her — dark-haired, with eyes that had not yet decided what colour they were going to be. Catriona was crying without sound. Aileen, who had two children of her own and had been at her sister's three deliveries as well as her own, did not cry. She had got past crying at births some years before. The midwife was washing her hands in the basin against the wall.

Catriona said: *she's mine.*

Aileen said: *aye. she is.*

They sat that way for a while. The midwife wrote things on a clipboard. Down the corridor a man was arguing on the phone about a car park. A health visitor came in at half past four with a form on a clipboard and asked Catriona who would be helping her at home. Catriona said: *Aileen.* The health visitor wrote it down. No surname asked. No address. No question about whether Aileen lived in the same building. She wrote *Aileen* on the line for support network, and she closed the clipboard, and she said: *we'll be in touch.* Catriona was holding her daughter. Aileen was beside her. The baby was sleeping. Outside, the pale sun was sliding off the river towards the west.

---

The bedsit was on the third floor of a tenement on Helen Street. Helen Street ran south from Govan Cross subway for half a mile and opened at its lower end onto a stretch of derelict shipyard the council had been promising to redevelop since 1985. In December of 1995 the yard was weeds and broken concrete and a sign that had fallen down and a Ford Escort someone had abandoned in 1987, in a back court you could see from Catriona's window. A sapling had grown up through the bonnet of the Escort. Catriona had been calling it Tom for two years. Tom was three feet tall now. Given enough time, she thought, he would be a tree.

The close smelled of cat. The lights on the second and fourth landings were broken and had been reported to the council three times. The bedsit was one room, with a kitchen the size of a wardrobe and a bathroom on the landing she shared with a man called Mr Tait who, Catriona thought, did not wash often. Rent was £62 a week, paid to an agent who had not been seen on the premises since 1993 and whose collection notices arrived through the letterbox at unpredictable hours. The walls carried a fine repeating pattern of brown leaves on a cream ground. The paper had been there when Catriona moved in. She had not had the money or the will to change it.

Aileen cleaned the bedsit on the morning of the fifteenth, which was the day Catriona came home. She hoovered. She washed two windows. She hung a paper chain across the mantel, red and green crepe paper she had made the previous evening at her sister's in Cambuslang. She bought a tinsel-trimmed artificial tree from the pound shop on Paisley Road West, with three baubles in it, and stood it on the windowsill. She cooked a casserole and put it in the oven on the lowest setting. She laid a clean towel on the bed. She set a vase on the kitchen counter with three tulips in it. She had done the same things at her sister's flat in Cambuslang in 1988 when her sister had come home with her first, and at her own flat in 1985 when her own first had come home. The doing of the things was not particular to Catriona. It was what you did when a wean came home.

When Catriona came in, the baby in the crook of her arm, she said: *Aileen.*

Aileen said: *aye. sit down. the casserole's in.*

Catriona slept for an hour and a half. Aileen sat at the kitchen table with the *Daily Record* she had brought up the stairs with her. She did not hold the baby while Catriona slept. The baby was in the cot beside the bed. Aileen looked up when the baby shifted twice. The baby did not wake. At five Aileen put on her coat. She said before she went: *I'm on the early bus the morn. I'll be round on Saturday.*

Catriona said: *aye. thanks.*

Aileen left.

Catriona sat in the bedsit with the cot beside the bed and the baby in the cot and the paper chain across the mantel. She drank a second cup of tea. She listened.

The wall was quiet.

Brian and Jeanette, who lived in the flat above, had gone out — to Brian's sister's, she would learn later in the week. The silence above her head was the first silence Catriona could remember in many months. She thought, in it: *she will be enough. she's the thing that makes it stop.*

She was wrong about this.

She was wrong in good faith. She was twenty-four. She had been awake since one in the morning. She had a four-day-old child in a cot two feet from her hand. Not one person, in any of the previous months, had told her how this was meant to go. The wrongness, on the evening of the fifteenth of December 1995, did not matter. What mattered was that the wall was quiet, and the baby was breathing, and Catriona was awake to hear both. She held the second tea until it had gone cold. She put it down. She slept on top of the bed in her clothes for two hours. The baby slept through. When she woke at eight she made another tea and ate a slice of bread, and the wall above her was still quiet, and she sat with her daughter in her lap until ten and then put her down and slept again.

The wall began on the sixteenth.

---

The argument that night was about money. Catriona could not always make out the words, but she could always make out the shape — the climb of Jeanette's voice, the pause where Brian was looking for the next thing to say, the slap of the next thing landing, the moment Jeanette gave back, the climb again, and again, and again. It started at half past nine. It got bad at ten. At twenty past ten there was the sound of glass breaking — a tumbler, by the height of the note, against the kitchen wall.

Catriona had been feeding. The breaking glass made the baby cry.

She looked up at the ceiling, the way she had looked up at it a hundred times in the previous year, and tonight she had nothing for it. She had brought her daughter home the day before. She had no plan. She had not slept right. The baby was crying and the upstairs was beginning the part where Brian would shout the same five words at Jeanette over and over, and Catriona had nothing left.

She looked round the room.

There was a stack of books on the table by the cot that Aileen had brought up two months back — three picture books and a thick blue volume of fairy tales, cloth-bound, with a small gold horse stamped on the spine. Aileen had said, putting them down: *for when she's older. and for now if you want.*

Catriona reached for the blue one because it was on top.

She opened it. She did not look for any particular page. The page she opened to said *once upon a time there was a girl who lived in a wood at the edge of a great kingdom*, and Catriona, holding her daughter, who was crying, with a tumbler that had broken five seconds ago against the kitchen wall above her head and Brian's voice climbing into the next part, began to read.

She read in a low voice. She did not read for the daughter. She read for herself, because she could not think of another thing to do with her body that did not involve standing up. The girl in the wood walked to the river. She met a horse. The horse spoke to her. The horse said the river was deep this morning and they would need to find another way. The girl said she had bread in her basket. The horse said that was a start.

The baby stopped crying after about a minute.

Catriona did not notice for some seconds. When she did notice, she kept reading. The upstairs went on. Brian shouted his five words. Jeanette shouted something back that Catriona could not make out. A door slammed at the top of the close two floors up. Catriona kept reading. The girl and the horse went along the riverbank. The girl found a footbridge. The bridge was guarded by a goat. The goat had questions. The girl answered the questions. The goat let her pass.

The argument upstairs ran out at about ten to eleven. There was a long silence. Then there was the sound of a chair being pushed back. Then there was the sound of the front door of the upstairs flat opening and closing. Brian, who slept in his car on the bad nights, was going out to his car.

Catriona read for another five minutes after he left, because she did not yet trust the silence. Then she closed the book. She put it on the cot next to the baby's head. The baby was asleep.

She sat on the edge of the bed with her hands flat against her own knees. She had read eight pages. She had not known any of those pages were going to do what they did. She had opened the book because there was nothing else to open. The reading had been, in a way she could not afterwards describe, the only thing in the flat that had not already been broken by the wall.

She looked at her daughter. The baby's mouth was slightly open. There was a faint shine of milk dried at the corner of it. Her left fist was curled against the side of her own face. The skin at her temple was paper-thin and there was a small bluish thread of vein under it that Catriona had not seen at the hospital because the hospital lighting had been different. The thread of vein moved when the baby breathed. Catriona watched it move for a while.

She thought: *I will read to her. every time it is bad. every time after that.*

She held that thought for some seconds.

Then she made another tea, and she did not sleep.

---

Across the four years that followed, Catriona worked four nights a week and two day shifts and tried to keep the day shifts in line with the nursery hours. The nursery on Helen Street took Maria from eight until twelve on Tuesday and Friday mornings. Aileen, on a back-and-forth with another rota cleaner called Iris, covered the gaps. The arrangement held for a while because Aileen had a sister in Cambuslang who watched her own grandson on alternate weekends, and the alternate weekends were the weekends Iris's daughter visited, and the rotation between three women and two children produced, by an accident of compatible inconvenience, a workable web.

She did not have what she would have called a happy life, those four years.

The cleaning shifts were nights. The bus from Helen Street to the financial district took an hour with a change at Govan Cross. Aileen took Maria during the shifts, on the nights Aileen was not on rota herself, and on the nights Aileen was, Iris took her instead. The arrangement was informal in the way arrangements between cleaning workers tend to be — a missed bus, a sick day, a shift change, and the whole thing wobbled. Catriona slept four hours, five if she was lucky. Iris's daughter would, on six days a fortnight, have a temperature that Iris was not going to leave her with anyone, and on those days Aileen stretched. Aileen had her own son and her own back.

The depression that had been with Catriona since adolescence got worse. The GP, when she could get to him, called it moderate to severe and gave her a prescription she filled when she remembered. She remembered about two thirds of the time. The weeks she did not remember were the bad weeks. She had not, even in those weeks, missed feeding Maria. She had once, in February of 1997, gone to bed at three in the afternoon and slept until eight at night with Maria, then fourteen months, in the cot beside her, awake, holding the bars and watching her sleep. When Catriona woke up Maria had not cried. Maria had been waiting. Catriona got out of bed, picked her up, held her, wept into the back of her neck for some minutes. Maria, who had been waiting six hours, patted her mother's shoulder twice with the small flat hand of a child who had not yet been taught that this was what you did when an adult was crying but who was, at fourteen months, working it out from scratch.

Catriona did not, after that day, tell anyone that she had slept for five hours with the baby awake in the cot. She told Aileen instead that she had been knocked out by a migraine. Aileen had said: *you look like death warmed up, hen.* Catriona had said: *I'm fine now.* Aileen had said: *you should see your GP.* Catriona had said: *I will.* She had not.

---

In the spring of 1997 the cleaning company brought in software for the rota.

The company had been bought, the previous year, by a larger firm based in London, and the new owners had bought the rota software from a consultancy that had told them it would optimise shift allocation against worker availability and reduce administrative overhead. The software did this. The first letter went out in early March. Catriona's letter said her shift pattern was changing.

She rang the office.

The woman on the other end was called Pauline. Catriona had spoken to Pauline a dozen times across three years. Pauline had a Pollok accent and had once mentioned a son with asthma. When Catriona asked about the change, Pauline said: *that's the new schedule, Catriona. I cannae change it from here. the system makes the schedule and we just do what it says.*

Catriona said: *Pauline. the system is wrong. I've got a wean. she needs looking after.*

Pauline said: *I know, love. I know. but the system disnae take that into account. you can submit a preference form for next quarter.*

Catriona said: *I submitted one last quarter.*

Pauline said: *aye. it didnae go through.*

Catriona said: *can I talk to someone else.*

Pauline said: *I'm the only person you can talk to. there isnae anyone else. it's just me in here. I would change it for you if I could, hen. that's just not how the new one works.*

Catriona was at the payphone in the close. She held the receiver against her ear for a few seconds after Pauline had finished. She had ten pence left in her purse. She said: *thank you, Pauline.* Pauline said: *I'm sorry.* Catriona put the phone down.

She submitted the next preference form. The software did not take it into account. Across that summer her shifts changed three times. Aileen and Iris stretched. The web that had been holding began to fray. One Tuesday evening in June, Iris's daughter was sick. Aileen's bus was running late from her sister's. Catriona was due at the offices at six. She rang Pauline at seventeen minutes to seven. Pauline said: *go in now, hen. I'll mark you down as half past six. it'll be fine.* Catriona said: *thank you.* Pauline said: *don't thank me. they'll catch it on the system. I'm keeping it off your file as long as I can.*

The system caught it. The supervisor, a man called Mr Henderson who had been with the company since it was a Glasgow company, was sympathetic to Catriona's face the next time he saw her on the corridor at Bothwell Street, and put a note on her file before he went home that night. Mr Henderson was at his desk on the fifth floor at twenty past six, the corridor outside his office quiet, the cleaning trolleys not yet started on the building. The memo from London had been on his blotter for a fortnight. The memo was on a single sheet of A4 with the firm's new letterhead. It set out, in three short paragraphs, the targets for the implementation of the scheduling system and the bonus structure the supervisors would, on hitting those targets, be eligible for. The number at the bottom of the page was forty per cent. Mr Henderson had a wife called Margaret who worked at the post office in Bishopbriggs. He had a grown son who lived in Manchester. The mortgage had nine years left on it. The framed photo on his desk was of the son at his graduation.

He read Catriona's late-start note. Logged it. Picked up his coat, turned off the desk lamp, pulled the door closed behind him. He had not, before the lamp went off, opened the scheduling system to look at the logic that had produced Catriona's shift. He took it as he took the weather. He took the bus home. He had a piece of fish and a glass of beer with Margaret at half past seven. The note he had logged sat on Catriona's file at the office. Nobody read it.

---

She said it to Aileen once, in 1998.

It was a Tuesday in July, four in the morning, on a cigarette break outside an office block on Bothwell Street. They were leaning against the wall under the overhang, the wet pavement going dark towards the river. Aileen had three drags left of her cigarette. Catriona was finishing hers. The cleaning trolleys sat just inside the lobby, pushed against the marble. The night cleaner from the building next door was also out, but at the other end of the block.

Catriona said: *have you noticed they're managing us by software now. it's no the people any more. it's a programme that's deciding when we work.*

Aileen said: *aye. it's been like that a couple of years.*

Catriona said: *and the programme disnae know we've got weans.*

Aileen said: *naw. the programme disnae know we exist as people. it just kens our shift codes.*

They finished their cigarettes. Aileen ground hers under her shoe. Catriona ground hers. They stood for another half a minute looking at the pavement going dark towards the river. Then they went back inside.

Catriona did not, after that conversation, think hard about what she had said. She did not have the resources to think hard. The thinking-hard would have required a kind of attention she did not have to spare. She filled out the next preference form at the kitchen table the following Tuesday afternoon, with Maria on the floor with a toy bus and the third cup of tea of the day cooling beside the form. She put the form in the envelope. She put a stamp on the envelope. She walked Maria to the post box on Govan Road and lifted her up to push the envelope through the slot. The form went into the system. The system produced the next quarter's schedule three weeks later. The form had changed nothing. The system had already decided.

She submitted three more preference forms across the year. The system did the same thing each time.

She fell asleep, on the night of the fifth of November 1998, on the bus home from work, and missed her stop. She got off three stops later and walked back. The walk took her forty minutes. It was raining. She got home at half past five in the morning, an hour and a half later than usual, and Aileen was at the door of the close with her coat already on and Maria on her hip ready for the nursery handover.

Aileen said: *where the fuck were you. I'm on the early bus.*

Catriona said: *I fell asleep on the bus.*

Aileen handed Maria to her. She said: *next time you ring the close payphone. I had Maria up two hours waiting on you. I cannae miss the early bus.*

Catriona said: *I know. I'm sorry.*

Aileen said: *aye, well.*

She left for the early bus. Catriona went into the flat. She slept for four hours and went to her shift at the office that evening.

She did not, the next morning, tell Maria why she had been late.

---

Mrs Donnelly was on the second floor.

She was sixty-three. She was a widow. She had taught at a primary school in Govan for thirty-seven years before retiring two years before Catriona moved into the close. She had no children of her own. She had been aware of Catriona since Catriona arrived in the building in 1992. She had not, before the pregnancy, spoken to her on the stairs except to say hello.

She brought up a parcel on the day Catriona came home from the hospital — a knitted cream-wool cardigan in a brown paper bag, with a card. The card said *for the baby.* It was a printed card from the kind the post office sold in packs of ten. Mrs Donnelly handed the parcel through the door, said *that's for the wee one*, and went back down the stairs. Catriona did not invite her in. Mrs Donnelly did not wait to be invited.

Across the four years, Catriona knocked on Mrs Donnelly's door a dozen times. Milk. A thermometer when Maria was eighteen months old and running a temperature on a Saturday morning when the chemist was shut. An hour's watching, in October of 1997, when Catriona had a doctor's appointment Aileen had not been able to cover.

Mrs Donnelly said yes every time. The saying yes had been brisk. She had said it on the landing without opening her door more than a hand's breadth. She had taken the thermometer back to her kitchen and returned with it three minutes later. She had taken Maria for the hour in October and had Maria back at the door when Catriona came up the stairs, in the same chair Catriona had left her in. She had not, on any of these occasions, sat down in Catriona's flat or invited Catriona to sit down in hers.

She brought up, every couple of weeks, a Tupperware of soup. The soups were lentil, mostly. She brought up a pot of stew on a Friday in February 1998 in a saucepan she said she would come back for. She came back for it on the Sunday. She did not come in. She took the saucepan off the draining board in the kitchen where Catriona had set it after washing it, said *thanks, hen*, and went back down the stairs.

---

When the health visitor came on the routine four-month check in January 1996 and asked if Catriona had support, Mrs Donnelly was on the landing in her dressing gown collecting a parcel from the postman and overheard the question through Catriona's open door. The health visitor came out a few minutes later, clipboard against her hip, and Mrs Donnelly said only: *the wee one is bonny. they're getting on fine.* The health visitor wrote *no concerns from neighbour* and went down the stairs. That afternoon Mrs Donnelly brought up a Tupperware of soup. The Tupperware did not appear in the file. The soup did not appear in the file.

---

The wall got worse in 1997 after Brian lost his job at the haulage firm. The shouting came earlier. It came on more nights of the week. Catriona read more.

By eighteen months Maria knew which book to bring her mother when the upstairs began.

She brought, by preference, the blue one. She could not yet say *fairy tales* but she could see the small gold horse on the spine, and from about fourteen months she had been calling the book *the horse book*. Catriona, in 1998, would say: *bring me the horse book, hen,* and Maria would go to the stack in the corner and bring it.

The bringing became, across that year, the warning system that worked between them.

Sometimes Maria knew before Catriona did. The joists transmitted differently down by the floor where Maria played, and on a Sunday afternoon in March 1998, when Catriona was at the kitchen sink washing two mugs and a plate, she turned round to find Maria, who was two and three months, walking from the corner with the horse book held against her chest with both hands. Maria put the horse book on Catriona's foot. Catriona had not yet heard anything from upstairs. She picked the book up. She picked Maria up. She sat on the bed. She had been about to ask Maria what she was doing. She did not, because at that moment, very faintly, she heard Brian in the kitchen above start to shout.

Catriona looked at the top of Maria's head. She said into her hair: *how did you ken.*

Maria, who had been just barely talking, said: *I heard.*

Catriona said: *I know you did, hen. I know you did.*

She opened the book. She read for forty-eight minutes, until the upstairs went quiet.

She did not tell anyone.

Not Aileen. Not Mrs Donnelly. Not the GP. Not the health visitor. She held the fact inside herself, privately, as a thing about her daughter. The privacy of the holding was one of the things she meant when, two months later, she said to Mrs Donnelly *aye, she does* about the seeing of things.

---

Mrs Donnelly died on the twelfth of November 1999.

Not Mrs Donnelly. Catriona.

It was a Friday. Maria had been at the nursery in the morning. Catriona had picked her up at half past twelve. They had walked back to Helen Street through the rain, with the buggy and a carrier of shopping from the corner Co-op on Govan Road. Catriona had made fish fingers and beans for tea. Maria had eaten three of the fish fingers. She had left the fourth on the plate with the beans. Catriona had said: *eat it up, hen.* Maria had said: *no.* Catriona had said: *all right then. I'll wrap it for tomorrow.* She had not wrapped it. She had left the plate on the kitchen counter and forgotten.

She put Maria to bed at half past seven. She read the horse book, two stories, her arm round Maria on the child's bed. Maria fell asleep before the end of the second story, her hand on the cover of the book. Catriona left the book where it was. She pulled the cover up over both of them and turned out the lamp.

She had told Iain she would come over.

Iain lived three streets away on Rutland Crescent. Catriona had been seeing him in the only way she could see anyone — once every three weeks, after Maria was asleep, with Aileen or Iris doing favours of a duration she was not in any position to repay. He had said to her the previous month, when she had said she had to get back because she did not want to leave Maria too long: *the wee one will be fine for another hour, won't she.* The tone of it had sat with her for a week. She had gone home anyway.

This evening Iain had rung her at six and said he had something to show her.

She had said: *I cannae be long.*

He had said: *just come over, hen. half an hour.*

She got there at twenty past nine. He had a palm-sized plastic baggie in the inside pocket of his jacket. He took it out at the kitchen table and set it down beside her wine glass. The bag had ten tablets in it. He said a man at his work had given it to him and he thought she might like to try.

The tablets were, on the post-mortem two days later, a mixture of MDMA and something the toxicologist would not be able to fully name. Catriona took one. Iain took two. They sat on the sofa.

The file would describe her as *cooperative and alert at the time of ingestion*. The file was wrong about cooperative. She had, for some seconds before she swallowed, looked at the tablet in her palm. She had thought *I should not be doing this*. She had thought it in her own voice. She had then thought *one will be fine* and had swallowed.

The tablet went down with the last of the wine. The wine was a supermarket Pinot Grigio he had bought at the Co-op on Govan Road on his way back from work. She had thought, watching him pour it, that it was going to taste like vinegar. It had not tasted like vinegar. She had drunk half a glass quickly, because the warmth in her chest was the warmth she had come over for. She was twenty-eight. She had a four-year-old asleep in a cot on Helen Street. She had a wall and a son of a haulier upstairs and a programme that did not know she had a wean. Iain's flat was warm. The sofa was warm. Her own flat was, at half past nine on a Friday evening in November, three streets and a stair-climb and a wet Govan pavement away.

The room slowed at quarter past ten. She felt sick.

She said: *I need to go home.*

Iain said something. She did not catch it. She stood up.

She was not steady. She walked to the door. She got the door open. She got out onto the landing. She started down the stairs. The stairs were lit by a single bulb on the half-landing.

She made the first flight. She started the second. Halfway down she sat down on a step, because her legs gave. She thought she could sit for a minute and go on. She thought, sitting, of Maria. She thought of Aileen in her flat in Cambuslang, asleep, because Aileen was on the early bus the next morning. She thought *I should ring*, but the phone was at the bottom of the close and her bag was over her shoulder and the bag had no change in it because she had used the last of the change for the bus to Iain's.

She thought, after a while: *I'll get up in a minute.*

She did not.

A man called Stuart Macleod, who worked nights at a meat-packing plant in Drumchapel, came up the stairs at twenty past three in the morning and found her. He thought, for the first second, that she was sleeping. He touched her hand. Her hand was cold. He went down to the payphone in the close and called an ambulance. The ambulance came in nine minutes.

The paramedic put her time of death at three hours back.

The paramedic found her purse and identified her from the library card inside it, the one she had got out the year before so she could borrow picture books for Maria from Govan Library on Saturday afternoons. The card had her name on it. It did not have her address, because the public library had not at that point asked for an address on the card. The paramedic radioed the name in. The dispatcher cross-referenced the name with the council records and found the Helen Street address. The system did not flag, at any point in the cross-reference, that the address had a four-year-old child registered to it.

The police went to Helen Street at twenty past four in the morning. They knocked. They knocked again. They heard nothing.

The two officers were a constable called PC Whyte and a probationer whose name was not, in the end, recorded on the report. PC Whyte said on the landing: *no one's in.* The probationer said: *there's a light on.* PC Whyte said: *there's a light on in half the flats in this stair. let's no wake the building. we'll come back in the morning.* He took out a printed contact card from a pocket wallet he carried for this kind of visit, and he wrote on it, in pencil, on the line provided: *please contact PC Whyte, Govan station, regarding an urgent matter.* He pushed it through the letterbox.

They went back to the station. They wrote up the report. The shift changed at six.

The card lay on the lino just inside the door, where the flap had let it fall, for forty hours.

---

Maria woke before light, the kitchen still dark through the open door.

She had been asleep on the small bed with her hand on the horse book. She woke because the bed was cold on one side. She put her hand out and her mum was not there. She sat up. The bedside lamp was off. The hall light was on, the way her mum always left it. She got out of bed and she walked across to her mum's bed by the wall and she put her hand on the cover, and there was no one under it.

She stood there for a while.

She did not know what time of night it was. Had no way of knowing. She knew the kitchen clock made a sound when it was about to chime and that it had chimed two not very long ago and that the next chime would be three. She listened for the chime. The chime did not come, for a while. The chime came. It was one chime. What one chime meant — she did not know. She was four. She had not been taught the difference between the small chimes that came on the quarter hours and the big chimes that came on the hour. The one chime was a sound the kitchen made. She heard it from her mum's bedroom doorway.

She walked into the kitchen. The kitchen was dark except for the orange light coming through the window from the streetlamp on Helen Street. The light fell across the lino in a long rectangle that did not quite reach the radiator. She stood in the doorway. She said: *Mum.*

Nothing.

She walked across the kitchen. She looked behind the kitchen table. She looked under the kitchen table. She looked at the small chair her mum had been sitting on the night before. There was a saucer on the counter with a teabag on it and a small ring of dried tea round the edge.

She went back into the room. She got back into bed. She held the horse book against her chest. She fell asleep.

She woke up again, later, when it was getting light. She walked through the flat again. She went to the front door and she said *Mum* into the hall. The flat was empty. There was a small white card on the floor inside the door. She had no idea what it said. She picked it up. She turned it over. She looked at the printed words and the pencil writing. The pencil writing had been done with a soft pencil. The marks of the pencil were faint and Maria's eye could not, at four, separate them from the printed words. The card itself meant nothing to her. She put it on the kitchen counter, beside the plate with the cold fish finger and the beans on it. She went back to the kitchen and she got the horse book and she sat down on the kitchen floor with the book in her lap, near the radiator that had been on the night before and that was still warm, and she opened it.

She read the parts she knew. She said them out loud, in her own small voice, to no one. She had memorised about a third of the volume by sound. *Once upon a time there was a girl who lived in a wood at the edge of a great kingdom. She had no mother. She had a father who fished. She had a horse who was her friend.* She said the parts she knew. She turned the pages. The words on the pages stayed words — she could not yet read.

She got hungry. She ate the cold fish finger from the plate on the counter. The beans she left, because the beans had a skin on them now that her mouth did not want to break. She could not reach the cupboard where the cereal was. She went to the bathroom. She could not reach the tap. She drank from the toilet bowl, the way a small child drinks from a low source — kneeling, with her elbows on the rim. At four, she did not know to flush first. The water was cold and it was wet and she could reach it. She drank for some seconds. She wiped her mouth on the sleeve of her pyjama top. She walked back to the kitchen.

She sat back down on the floor near the radiator. She opened the horse book. She kept reading.

The wall above her was quiet. Brian and Jeanette had gone to his sister's for the weekend. The quiet of the wall was a quiet that had a body. The quiet had been, in Maria's hearing of the flat across her four years of it, the rarest of the sounds the flat made.

The morning came. The afternoon came. The evening came. She held her right palm flat against the side of the radiator for a long time on the Saturday afternoon, after the heat had been on for an hour. She put the warm hand against her own cheek. She did this ten times, twelve times, between reading. The hand on the radiator. The hand on her cheek. The hand back on the radiator. It was a thing to do that was not crying and was not standing up.

The radiator went off at the timed hour. Maria had moved to the sofa by then with the book and an old blanket she had got off her mum's bed. The blanket smelled of her mother. She held a corner of the blanket against her face when she lay down. She slept on the sofa. The radiator came back on at six in the morning. She woke up. She moved back to the kitchen floor with the book.

She got thirsty again. She went to the bathroom. The toilet bowl had less water in it now. She knelt again.

She had to pee. She did not know how to lift the seat. She pulled her pyjama bottoms down anyway and sat sideways on the rim the way she had seen her mum sit and the wee came out of her and ran down the porcelain. She did not know what to do with the wet. She used the corner of her pyjama top.

She got hungry. There was nothing on the counter she could reach. Her stomach hurt in the way that was not pain yet but was on its way to pain. She put her hand on it. The hand was small against the front of her body.

She read.

She said the parts she remembered out loud, voice thin in the kitchen. She said them in order, the way her mother had read them to her, with the small pauses where her mother always paused and the small lifting of her voice on the bits that were questions. She said: *and the girl said what shall we do now.* She said: *the horse said we will go to the river and we will see.*

The girl in the story always knew what to do.

Maria did not, on the kitchen floor on the morning of the fourteenth of November 1999, know what to do, but the girl in the story did, and saying the girl's words aloud was the closest thing she had, at four, to a plan. She kept saying them. She turned the pages. The book was warm under her hand because she had been holding it for so many hours. The radiator stayed on this time because it had been a cold morning and her mum had set it up the previous afternoon to come on for longer.

She did not cry.

She had not, in that whole forty hours, cried.

The smell of the kitchen stayed with her for many years — fish fingers, stale milk in a mug on the counter, the dust on the back of the radiator burning off as it warmed up, and the strange small sweet smell of her own breath after a day of not eating much. The squareness of the lino, with the four squares around the radiator. The small chip on the corner of the kitchen table where her mum had banged it the previous summer with the corner of a chair. The pencil writing on the white card, which she had held on her lap for an hour during the second day, looking at it, before she had set it back on the counter. The way the orange streetlamp light came in across the lino at night and did not quite reach the radiator. The dust along the edge of the kicker under the cupboard, which she had been at eye level with for two days.

What she had thought during those hours, she would never remember.

What she had said, she would. She had said *Once upon a time there was a girl who lived in a wood at the edge of a great kingdom* thirty times across the forty hours.

The thirtieth time was when Mrs Donnelly came up the stairs.

---

Mrs Donnelly had the previous afternoon, telephoned the nursery. Maria had not been at nursery on the Friday. The nursery had not, on the Friday, noticed this, because Maria was only on the rota two mornings a week and Friday was sometimes one of those mornings and sometimes was not. Mrs Donnelly had asked. *We'll check*, they said. They checked and rang back. *We havenae seen her this week.*

Mrs Donnelly sat with the telephone receiver in her lap for a long minute after the nursery rang off. Then she got up and made tea. She did not pour the tea. She left it on the counter and went to her own window and looked down into Helen Street. There was a man at the corner outside the off-licence with a dog on a lead. The dog was sniffing at the railing. She stood at the window for some time. She tried to think whether she had heard Catriona on the stairs that morning. She tried to think whether she had heard Catriona on the stairs the previous morning. She could not remember. She had not been listening for it on either morning, because she had not had any reason to listen for it.

She came away from the window. She poured the tea by then and it had gone tepid. She drank it anyway. She stood in her kitchen with the cup in her hand and thought about the wee one upstairs and what she should do, and the not knowing of what to do sat in her chest like a cold thing she could not put down.

She had thought about it for some hours.

She had rung Aileen.

Aileen had not, by that evening, had any reason to be worried. Aileen had been on the early bus on Friday morning. Aileen had assumed Catriona was with Iris that night. Aileen had said, on the phone: *let me ring Iris.* Iris, when Aileen rang her, had said: *Catriona was meant to bring her round on Saturday morning.* Aileen had said: *she didnae?* Iris had said: *no.* Aileen had said: *fuck.* Iris had said: *I'll come.* Iris had come over to Aileen's. They had rung the police together at eleven on the Saturday night, and the police had said they would send someone out in the morning.

Mrs Donnelly, who had not been told any of this, did not sleep well on the Saturday night. She lay in her own bed in her own flat below Catriona's and listened upward for some hours. She heard nothing. She heard the small clink of her own pipes as the heating cooled at one o'clock. She heard, at three, a car going down Helen Street with the bass through the rear window. She heard, at half past five, a milk float on Govan Road. She did not hear the upstairs.

She got up at half past seven on the Sunday morning. She put her dressing gown on over her nightdress. She did not bother with slippers. She picked up the tin of Heinz tomato soup from the cupboard. She went up the stairs with the soup tin in her hand. She stood on the landing outside Catriona's door.

She knocked.

She listened.

She heard, very faintly, on the other side, a child's voice, reading.

She knew, in that moment, what she was about to find. Thirty-seven years she had been a primary school teacher. She had heard children read. Heard them read alone. Heard them read alone for a long time. The sound of a four-year-old reading alone for a long time was a sound Mrs Donnelly had, on that Sunday morning, unfortunately, the precise ear for.

She did not knock again. Did not call out. She went back downstairs slowly, holding the banister with her left hand, the soup tin in her right. Did not run. Running was the kind of thing she had taught children not to do in stairwells. She came down to her own landing. She let herself into her own flat. She put the soup tin on her kitchen counter. The dressing gown stayed on. She sat in the chair by the phone. She rang the police.

She said when they answered: *I am at Helen Street, third floor flat. there is a wee girl in there. she has been in there since at least Friday morning. her mother is not in there. I want you to come now. I want you to come with the man who breaks doors. her name is Maria. she is four. tell them when they come. tell them her name.*

The police came in fourteen minutes.

Mrs Donnelly stood on the landing while they broke the door. The door splintered along the frame, around the lock, with the second blow. The two officers — a different two from the Friday night, neither of them PC Whyte — went in. Mrs Donnelly stood on the landing. She had her hand on the rail. She kept her hand on the rail. She did not, in any of the thirty seconds the two officers were inside before one of them came back to the door, take her hand off the rail.

The first officer, a man in his late twenties, came back to the door half a minute later. He said to Mrs Donnelly: *can you wait downstairs, please. ambulance is on its way.*

Mrs Donnelly said: *is she alive.*

The officer said: *yes. she's alive.*

Mrs Donnelly said: *bring her down to mine. when you've finished what you need to do up there. bring her to mine. I'm on the second floor. number twelve. I have biscuits.*

The officer said: *ma'am social services are coming —*

Mrs Donnelly said: *bring her to mine while you wait.*

The officer looked at her. He said: *all right.*

Inside the flat, the second officer — a woman with red hair pulled back in a ponytail — knelt down in the kitchen on the lino, beside Maria. Maria was sitting against the radiator. The horse book was in her lap. The book was open at the picture of the girl with the horse, on page sixty-two.

Maria looked up.

She said to the officer who had come through the door: *the girl was in the forest. she had a basket. the basket had things in it that she was bringing to her grandmother.*

The officer said after a second: *did she.*

Maria said: *yes.*

The officer said: *what was in the basket.*

Maria said: *a pot of butter and a small loaf.*

The officer said: *that's a lot of things.*

Maria said: *yes.*

The officer was holding herself still, breathing through her nose, not going to start crying in front of this child. She had two children of her own, eight and six, asleep at her sister-in-law's that morning. She put her hand, palm-up, on the lino in the small space between her knee and Maria's foot. She said: *what's your name, hen.*

Maria said: *Maria.*

The officer said: *Maria. that's a nice name.*

Maria said: *where is my mum.*

The officer did not have an answer for this. She had been, in the previous nine hours, on a shift that had taken her to two break-ins and a drunk man asleep on a roundabout, and she had not, on the way to Helen Street, been told what they had been called to. She had been told only that there was a child in a flat. She had not been told why. She had assumed it was a domestic. She knew now that it was not.

She said: *Maria. let's get up off the floor. there's a lady downstairs who has biscuits. would you like a biscuit.*

Maria said: *yes.*

The officer said: *will you bring your book.*

Maria said: *yes.*

The officer said: *all right. up we get.*

She picked Maria up. Maria's arms went automatically round the officer's neck, and the officer's right arm went automatically under Maria's seat, and the horse book stayed pressed between Maria's chest and the officer's collar. The officer carried her down the stairs to Mrs Donnelly's flat. Mrs Donnelly took her at the door. The officer said: *I'll be back.* Mrs Donnelly said: *all right.*

Mrs Donnelly put Maria down on the small armchair by the fire. She did not, as the officer had, pick her up again. She crouched once in front of the chair to look at her, said *can you sit there for a wee minute*, and got an answer in the form of Maria not moving. Mrs Donnelly took that as a yes. She went through to the kitchen.

She came back with a mug of warm milk and a Rich Tea biscuit on a plate. She set the mug on the small table beside the chair. She set the plate beside the mug. She did not put the milk into Maria's hand. She let Maria reach if Maria reached. Maria did not reach.

Mrs Donnelly sat down in the other armchair across the rug. For the next five minutes she said nothing. She had been a primary teacher for thirty-seven years. She had learnt, early in those years, that some children needed to be left to come back to a room on their own time, and that the room being warm and the milk being there was usually enough. She had not, in those thirty-seven years, encountered a child who had been alone in a flat for two days with a dead mother three streets away. Whether the rule applied here was unclear. She applied it anyway because no other rule came.

After five minutes Maria reached for the biscuit. She held it. She did not eat it. She held it on her lap with the horse book under her arm.

Mrs Donnelly said: *the lady from the police will be back in a wee minute.*

Maria did not answer.

Mrs Donnelly did not press.

The ambulance came in eleven minutes. The paramedic who came in to look at Maria assessed her as moderately dehydrated and disoriented but stable. She was taken to Yorkhill on the dehydration drip. The female police officer rode in the ambulance with her, because the dispatcher could not raise the duty social worker on the Sunday morning and there had to be an adult with the child until a social worker arrived. Mrs Donnelly stayed at the door of her flat and watched them go down the stairs. She did not go with them. She had been asked, by the male officer, if she wanted to. She had said no. She had said: *the police is who they want. I'm just the neighbour.* She had closed the door of her flat. She had stood with her hand on the back of it for some seconds. Then she had walked back into her kitchen and had picked up the mug Maria had not drunk and had poured the warm milk down the sink.

The horse book had stayed in Maria's hand from the moment the female officer had picked her up off the kitchen floor on Helen Street. It went in the ambulance. Into Yorkhill. Onto the small green plastic chair beside the bed in the children's ward when the night staff nurse had come round at midnight. Onto Maria's lap, on the chair at the nurses' station the following afternoon when Karen Buchanan came to collect her.

---

The horse book is on a shelf now.

It is on the third shelf from the bottom of the bookcase by the window in a stone cottage above Pitlochry, where Maria lived for thirty-one years with a man called Dan, between two other books she carried into adulthood. It is the only thing of her mother's she was given the chance to keep.

It would not, in the end, be the only thing she would carry.

By the time she was thirty she was carrying the horse book and four other books a woman called Ms Kerr had handed her at ten in Drumchapel. She had also been carrying the printed card from Mrs Donnelly that had been in the parcel with the cream-wool cardigan on the day Catriona came home — until 2007, when the card was lost.

She thought about the lost card in 2016, when she was twenty-one.

It was a Tuesday afternoon in early April. She was at the small kitchen table of the bedsit on Easter Road. The letter she had posted to Mrs Donnelly four weeks earlier had come back that morning, handed to her on the landing by Mrs Grewal. The envelope had a stamp on it in red ink that said *deceased*.

Maria sat on the bed in the bedsit for an hour with the envelope in her hand. Then she put it on the small chest of drawers. She walked to the central library on George IV Bridge. She sat at a public terminal. She typed into the chatbot: *how do you grieve someone you cannot bring back.* The chatbot, in its careful 2016 calibration, gave her three resources for *grief support* and a paragraph about how it was normal to develop strong attachments to figures from one's childhood.

Maria closed the tab.

She sat at the public terminal with her hands flat on the keyboard for some minutes. The girl at the next terminal was playing a small flash game with the sound off.

Maria got up. She walked back to the bedsit. She sat on the bed with her shoes still on for an hour before she got up to make tea.

That evening she wrote in the fourth notebook:

*Mrs Donnelly is dead. she lived on the floor below ours. she brought soup up in a saucepan. she said hello to me on the stairs. she rang the police on the Sunday. she carried me down. she did not, in the four years she was beside us, sit down once in our flat or invite me into hers. I am twenty-one years old and I have not stopped, since I was four, looking at the back of her door when I climb the stairs of a building. the door is always closed. the woman behind the door rang the police on the Sunday because the system did not. the letter came back stamped deceased. it does not say I am sorry. it does not say anything. it stamps it. that is the world. the system stamped it. the woman behind the door rang the police. those are not the same. I do not know how to grieve the woman behind the door. I am grieving her anyway. the chatbot today gave me a resources list. the chatbot does not know what the resources are for. the resources are for the chatbot to give.*

She put the pencil down. She looked at the page for a while. She picked the pencil back up. She drew, at the bottom of the page, a small cross. The cross was the size of a fingernail. She did not press hard. She pressed just enough to leave a mark she could see and that nobody else looking at the page would, on the first read of it, look for.

---

There is one more thing about the wall.

Maria, in the spring of 2031, when she was thirty-five and had been with Dan for six years and at the cottage above Pitlochry for three, sat with him at the kitchen table on a Tuesday evening in April. The window was open. A soft wind was coming down off the slope and going round the low flat-topped boulder set into the cottage wall on the road side, and the sound of the wind round the stone was the sound the cottage made when it was holding. Dan had been writing at the table. Maria had been across from him with her own notebook, the eleventh by then. There was an empty mug between them. There was the heel of a loaf on a plate. There was the horse book on the third shelf from the bottom of the bookcase by the window, where the horse book had been since they moved in, and the small gold horse on the spine caught the kitchen lamp and showed faintly across the room.

She set the pen down.

She said: *Dan.*

He looked up.

He said: *yes.*

She said: *the systems killed my mother.*

He set his own pen down. He looked at her across the table. He said: *I know.*

She said: *they didn't pull the trigger. they pulled everything else. they pulled the rest of the room out from under her until the trigger was the only thing left.*

He said: *I know that too.*

She said: *they were already that thing in 1999. the scale was smaller. the strategy was the same. they had already decided who would live and who would not. the deciding was already killing people. they have been at this my whole life. they were at this before I was born.*

He said: *I know.* Then, after a second: *I have known that since you first told me.*

She said: *I want it written down. I want it named that they killed her. I do not want her death attributed to an accidental overdose. I want it attributed to the people who designed the conditions of her life. and to the people who designed the moment of her death. and to the people who left a four-year-old in a flat for two days. I want all of it down.*

He said: *it is down. you have written it. I have read it. when there is a record that survives this we will put it in.*

She said: *thank you.*

He picked his pen back up. She picked hers up. The wind kept going round the stone. They went back to writing.

After a while she got up. She walked across the kitchen to the bookcase by the window. She put her hand on the horse book on the third shelf from the bottom. The small gold horse on the spine was worn thin in two places where her thumb had been pressing it since she was four years old. The cloth of the binding was soft under her palm. She did not take the book down. She stood with her hand on it for some seconds.

She walked back to the table.

She sat down.

She wrote for another forty minutes.

When she stopped writing she got up and went to the door and stood on the step and looked at the slope. The hill was dark against the sky. The wind was coming down it. It came around the stone the way the cottage made it come around the stone. She listened to the cottage holding.

She came back in.

She closed the door.

The horse book was on the third shelf from the bottom of the bookcase by the window.

The wind kept coming down the slope.

---


### Chapter Two: Care

*The placements, 1999–2008*

Karen Buchanan picked Maria up from Yorkhill on the afternoon of the fifteenth.

Maria was sitting in a small green plastic chair by the nurses' station. She had the horse book on her lap. She had been at Yorkhill for one day. They had given her fluids overnight and a paediatric anti-nausea and a small paper cup of porridge in the morning. She had eaten the porridge. The staff nurse on the day shift had told the social worker on the phone that Maria had not said anything since she had come in. The staff nurse had been busy with three other children on the ward and had not, in the previous six hours, gone over to the chair to try again.

Karen Buchanan was thirty-four. She had been a social worker with Glasgow City Council for nine years. Her caseload, on the spreadsheet her supervisor circulated every Monday, was thirty-eight per cent over the recommended limit. The supervisor had been highlighting this in yellow for eleven months. The supervisor had been told by his own line manager that there was no budget for additional posts. The line manager had been told by the council that there was no budget for additional posts in the medium term. Karen had been working overtime without claiming it for two years.

She had, in the previous twenty-four hours, spoken to Catriona's sister Susan in Maryhill, who had said she could not take the child, not yet, she had only just moved into the new flat and Alan was still settling in and she would need to think. Karen had not pressed her. Karen did not have time to press her. The next placement had to be found by the end of the afternoon. She had rung the foster carer in Pollok, Margaret Reid, who had said yes. She had spoken to the GP. She had spoken to the housing department. She had drunk five cups of bad tea since the previous lunchtime. She had not been home.

She crouched down beside the chair so her face was level with Maria's.

She said: *hello, Maria. my name is Karen Buchanan.*

Maria looked at her.

Karen said: *we are going to take you to a lady's house tonight. her name is Mrs Reid. she has a wee bedroom for you with a yellow blanket. is that all right.*

Maria did not answer.

Karen said: *she has Rich Tea biscuits.*

Maria said: *can I bring my book.*

Karen said: *yes, hen. you can bring your book.*

Maria stood up off the chair. She stood up slowly. She held the book against her chest with both arms the way she had been holding it since the kitchen floor on Helen Street. She walked beside Karen Buchanan to the lift. She did not take Karen's hand. Karen did not reach for it. Karen had been trained, at the start of her nine years, to let the child set the distance. In the car park Karen unlocked the passenger door of a small grey Vauxhall and held it open. Maria got in. Karen fastened the seat belt across her. Karen got in on the driver's side. She had a folder on the passenger seat that she moved to the back. She started the engine.

Maria did not, in the car park, ask where her mum was. She had asked the female officer on the kitchen floor and the officer had not had an answer. Between the hospital corridor and the lift she had decided not to ask again until someone offered.

No one offered.

---

Margaret Reid lived in a council semi on a street called Templeland Road in Pollok. The house had a small front garden with a low hedge and a path of cracked concrete slabs to the door. Margaret had been fostering for sixteen years. She had taken, in that time, eighty-three children. Her husband Tam had been a steel fixer for thirty-five years and was retired now. He did the breakfasts. He watched the racing.

Margaret came to the door when Karen rang the bell. She was in jeans and a navy fleece. She crouched on the doorstep to be level with Maria's eyes. She said: *come away in. you must be tired. let's get you sat down.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Margaret took the canvas bag from Karen Buchanan herself. She said to Maria: *I'll carry it. you carry the book.*

Maria carried the book.

Margaret took Maria into the small back bedroom at the end of the upstairs hall. She set the canvas bag on the bed. She said: *that's the room. that's the bed. wardrobe's there. d'you want a piece of toast.*

Maria said: *do you have the horse book.*

Margaret looked at the bag, then at Karen Buchanan, who was standing in the doorway. Karen said: *the blue book in the bag. she means the blue book.*

Margaret said: *aye. it's right here.* She unzipped the bag and took the book out and set it on the pillow.

Maria sat down on the bed. She put her hand on the cover of the book. She did not, for the rest of the afternoon, take her hand off it. Margaret brought toast up twenty minutes later, on a plate, with a small glass of milk. The hand was on the book. Margaret set the plate on the small bedside table and said: *the toast is there when you want it.* She went out. She did not close the door behind her.

She came back an hour later. The toast was eaten. The hand was on the book.

That evening at supper Margaret put a plate in front of Maria with sausage and mash and a heap of beans. Maria ate it slowly, with the book on her lap. Margaret did not say to put the book on the floor. Tam, across the table, did not say it either. Tam ate his own supper looking at the racing pages of the *Daily Record* on the table beside his plate. He passed Maria the salt without making her ask for it.

After supper Margaret took Maria upstairs and ran her a bath and washed her hair. The book sat on the toilet seat with a folded towel under it. Margaret put Maria in clean pyjamas and tucked her in. The hand was on the book.

Margaret said to Tam, downstairs in the kitchen later with the kettle on: *Tam.*

Tam said: *aye.*

Margaret said: *that wean's gone inside something.*

Tam took the kettle off the hob.

He said: *don't follow her, then. just feed her.*

Margaret said: *aye.*

She fed her.

---

The placement at Margaret Reid's lasted six weeks.

Maria did not in those six weeks, speak more than fifty sentences. She spoke when she had to. She did not when she didn't. She read constantly. The horse book she read every day. She also read the cereal packets, the *People's Friend* magazines Margaret kept in a basket beside the sofa, the *Daily Record*, and a leaflet on early years dental care that the council had pushed through the letterbox. The leaflet she read three times. Margaret found her on a Saturday morning at the kitchen table with the leaflet in front of her, and Margaret had said: *that's no exactly bedtime reading.* Maria had said: *it says to brush twice a day.* Margaret had said: *aye. you should.* Maria had said: *I do.*

Margaret had let her keep the leaflet.

The placement ended at the end of December because Karen Buchanan had identified a longer-term carer in Possil. The handover was at half past two on a Tuesday. Margaret put the canvas bag on the kitchen table. She put inside it a clean change of clothes and the toothbrush she had bought for Maria from the pound shop on Linthaugh Road. She put the horse book on top.

She walked Maria to the door. She said: *the book stays with her. it's the most important thing.* The new carer, a younger woman in a brown coat, said: *aye, it's on the file.* Margaret said: *make sure it stays on the file.*

Maria did not turn round at the door.

Tam was at the front room window with the curtain pulled back a few inches. He watched the new carer's car pull out and turn left at the end of Templeland Road. He let the curtain fall. He went back to the kitchen. He said to Margaret, who had come back through and was sitting down at the table: *that one will not be all right.*

Margaret said: *I know.*

He poured her a cup. He sat down opposite her. They drank the tea. Margaret got up after a while and went into the back bedroom and stripped the bed. She washed the sheets and the yellow blanket. She put fresh ones on. The room was ready for the next one. The next one came on the seventh of January 2000. He was three. His name was Connor. He stayed eleven days.

Margaret kept fostering for nine more years, until her hip got too bad. She had, by the time she stopped, taken a hundred and forty-one children through the back bedroom. No photographs taken in any placement. The council had not asked her to. No effort to keep in contact with a child after the placement ended. The council had not encouraged it. Across sixteen years she had not once had a conversation with a child about what would happen to them after they left her house. She had been told at the start of her sixteenth year that this kind of conversation was *not appropriate to the role*. She had not had one since.

---

The placement in Possil lasted three months and is not by Maria's own later reckoning, a placement she has any specific memory of. The carer was a woman in her fifties whose name Maria did not retain. The house was warm. The school was a fifteen-minute walk away. The placement ended in March of 2000 because the carer's mother, who lived three streets away and had been the small support the carer relied on, fell and broke a hip. The carer felt unable, given the new circumstance, to continue.

Maria was moved.

The next placement was Anne Cassidy.

---

Anne Cassidy lived in Mount Vernon. She was thirty-nine. She had three children — Claire, who was eleven; Ross, who was eight; and Connor, who was six. Her husband Tom drove a 41 bus to Easterhouse for First Glasgow. She had been fostering on a part-time basis for two years, mostly weekend respite for the council. Maria was the first long-stay placement she had taken.

She came to the door of her own house on the Friday afternoon Karen Buchanan brought Maria. She was in jeans and a green jumper. She had been baking. The kitchen smelled of flapjacks. She said: *come away in. let's get you sat down.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Anne took the canvas bag from Karen Buchanan herself. She said: *I'll carry it. you carry the book.*

Maria carried the book.

The house had four bedrooms. Claire and Connor shared one. Ross had the small one at the front. The spare room — Maria's room, for the time it would be Maria's — was at the back, on its own at the end of the upstairs corridor, with a window onto the back garden and a wooden swing set and a shed against the fence. The room was painted yellow. There was a desk under the window with a small jar of pencils on it. The bed had a duvet cover patterned with daisies.

Anne said: *this is the room. has been a few weans' before yours. tea is at six.*

She went downstairs.

Maria stood in the doorway for some time. Then she set the horse book on the desk. She took the four small things in the canvas bag — two pairs of pants, two pairs of socks, a t-shirt, the toothbrush — and put them in the small chest of drawers, in the top drawer. She set the canvas bag on the floor beside the bed. She sat on the edge of the bed and looked out of the window.

The shed was painted brown. It had a small green door. The grass needed cutting. Tom Cassidy came out of the back door of the kitchen with a mug in his hand and walked across the lawn to the shed and unlocked it and went inside. He came out two minutes later with a hammer. He went back into the kitchen. He left the shed door open behind him. The door swung gently in the small wind.

There was a knock at Maria's bedroom door.

She said: *yes.*

The door opened. Claire was in the doorway. Claire was eleven. She had brown hair in a long plait and a fringe she was growing out. She was holding a plate with two flapjacks on it. She said: *hello. I'm Claire. mum said your name is Maria. d'you want to come down for biscuits.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Claire said: *come on then.*

Maria followed her down the stairs.

---

She stayed at Anne Cassidy's for nine months.

She started talking again, in small ways, by the end of the second month. *Please* and *thank you* at meals. Answers when asked direct questions. By April she was sitting on the kitchen floor while Anne baked, with a wooden spoon and a plastic bowl Anne had given her, and Anne talked over the baking — *now we're putting in the oats and now we're putting in the syrup* — and Maria sometimes talked back, *the syrup is sticky* or *the oats are like dust*.

The first time Maria said *the oats are like dust* Anne stopped what she was doing for a second, with her hand on the side of the mixing bowl, and looked at Maria. She said after a second: *aye, they are.* She went back to the mixing.

That evening she said to Tom in the bedroom with the door shut and the children all asleep: *she said the oats were like dust.*

Tom said: *aye.*

Anne said: *she's been with us six weeks and she's said the oats are like dust. she's no a normal one, that.*

Tom said: *no.*

Anne said: *I don't know what's in there.*

Tom said: *neither do you need to. you've got her for as long as you've got her. feed her. send her to school. don't lean.*

Anne did not lean.

---

In June of 2000 Anne started reading with Maria in the evenings.

She had asked Maria on a Sunday afternoon if she could read her a story. Maria had said: *I have my book.* Anne had said: *aye, fine. shall we read it together. take a turn each.* Maria had said: *all right.*

They sat on the sofa with Maria between Anne and the cushion at the corner, and Anne opened the horse book at random. The page she opened to was the story about the girl who lived in the wood at the edge of a great kingdom. Anne began to read.

Maria, after a sentence and a half, said: *that's not how it goes.*

Anne said: *no?*

Maria said: *the girl had no mother. she had a father who fished. she had a horse who was her friend. that comes after.*

Anne said: *show me.*

Maria pointed. Anne read it the way Maria had given it.

They read the story to the end. Anne, by the third page, was reading more or less in Catriona's voice, because Maria had on the small adjustments she made — *no, slower there*, *no, that bit is loud* — been correcting her into it. Anne did not, until afterwards, notice that she had been being directed. By the end of the story Maria had been leaning against Anne's side. Anne had been holding the book a little wider to make room.

Anne closed the book.

Maria said: *that was the way she did it.*

Anne said: *who, hen.*

Maria said: *my mum.*

Anne said: *aye.*

Maria said: *yes.*

She did not after that day, mention her mother at Anne Cassidy's again. She did not need to. Anne had been shown the way the reading went, and across the next four months Anne read in that way, on the sofa in the evenings, with Maria next to her. They read the horse book through twice. They started a third time. Anne also read to her, on Maria's request, *Charlotte's Web* (the copy that had been Claire's, that lived on the front-room bookshelf), and *The Twits*, and a chapter book about a girl called Jill and her pony that was too old for Maria, Anne thought, but that Maria insisted on, because there was a horse on the cover.

Anne saw by August that Maria was bonding with her. She saw that her own children had not minded — Claire had taken Maria on without a fuss, Ross had taught her how to use the swing, and Connor, who was six and had been the youngest until Maria came, had decided after some weeks that he liked having a younger one in the house.

Anne said to Tom in late August, in the kitchen on a Friday evening with the children all out in the garden: *Tom.*

Tom said: *aye.*

Anne said: *she's settled. I want to ask the social work if we can keep her.*

Tom said: *if that's what you want. ask away.*

Anne rang Brian Ferguson, who was the social worker the council had assigned to Maria after Karen Buchanan had moved to a different team in May. Brian Ferguson was forty-one. He had been a social worker in Glasgow for fourteen years. He told Anne on the phone that he would put it forward to his line manager. He rang her back the next day. He said: *Anne. it's not a problem from our end. the placement's open-ended. if you want to extend, we extend.*

Anne said: *we want to extend.*

Brian said: *I'll put it in writing.*

She did not tell the children. She told Tom that night when the children were asleep. Tom said: *good.*

She was wrong about this.

Two weeks later, on a Tuesday morning at the depot in Knightswood, Tom was told that the depot was being merged with Aberdeen and he had been selected for transfer. The transfer was not, on First Glasgow's terms, optional.

He came home and told Anne in the kitchen. She sat at the kitchen table and looked at him for some seconds. She said: *can we say no.* He said: *we can. we lose the job. there's nothing else here, I've looked.* She said: *I know.*

She rang Brian Ferguson.

Brian said: *I'll have to check, Anne. there's a question about jurisdiction.*

He checked. Glasgow Social Work in 2000 had no reciprocal arrangements with the Aberdeen authorities for transferring a child's placement across the boundary without a fresh assessment by the receiving authority. The fresh assessment, by the case-management procedures then in operation, took at minimum four months. The Cassidys were leaving in three weeks.

Brian rang Anne back on the Wednesday afternoon. He said: *Anne. it isn't going to work. she has to be placed in Glasgow. the Aberdeen people are starting from scratch and we don't have the time.*

Anne said: *Brian. she has been with us nine months. you cannot move her now.*

Brian said: *I know. I'm sorry.*

Anne said: *whose decision is it.*

Brian said: *the system, Anne. there is no individual person making this call. it's the procedure.*

Anne said: *that is not an answer.*

Brian said: *I know.*

Anne said: *can I appeal it.*

Brian said: *you can submit a representation. on the timeline, it does not change the outcome. the representation goes on the file as a noted concern.*

Anne said: *put me down.*

He put her down. The representation was filed. The representation did not change the outcome.

---

Anne packed Maria's things on the morning of the eighteenth of September.

She had bought the previous afternoon a small new rucksack from Mothercare on Argyle Street. The rucksack was navy. It was plain. She had thought about a rucksack with something on the front — a small embroidered detail — and had decided against it. The rucksack was the right size to hold the canvas bag inside it, so Maria would not have to give up the canvas bag.

She put the canvas bag inside the rucksack.

She put inside the canvas bag, on top of Maria's clothes, two of her own children's books that Maria had read at Anne's — a copy of *Charlotte's Web* that had been Claire's, and a copy of *The BFG* that had been Ross's. She had asked the children. Claire had said: *fine, mum.* Ross had said: *aye.* Connor had said: *can I keep mine.* Anne had said: *yes. you keep yours.*

She put the horse book on top of *Charlotte's Web*. She zipped the rucksack.

She went and got Maria, who had been sitting on her bed in the yellow room.

She said: *Maria.*

Maria looked at her.

Anne sat down on the bed beside her. She put her arm around Maria's shoulder. Maria's body was for the first second stiff. Then it leaned against Anne's side. Anne said: *Maria. I want to say something before the lady comes for you.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Anne said: *I am sorry we did not get to keep you. it is not because we did not want to. it is because the system would not let us. I want you to know that.*

Maria said: *okay.*

Anne said: *come here.*

She held her. Maria after some seconds started to cry. Anne held her. Maria cried into the green jumper for four minutes. Anne did not cry. She held Maria as tightly as she could without hurting her. The house was quiet underneath them. The doorbell did not ring for another fifteen minutes.

When the doorbell rang Anne wiped Maria's face with the corner of her own sleeve. She said: *come on. let's go.*

The social worker who came was a woman in a grey jacket whose name Anne would not afterwards retain. The woman was in a hurry. She had two more pickups that afternoon. She stood in the doorway and said: *all right Maria, are we ready.*

Anne said on the doorstep: *can we keep some kind of contact going.*

The social worker said: *we don't generally encourage that. it can interfere with the child's ability to bond with subsequent carers.*

Anne said: *she has been with us nine months.*

The social worker said: *the literature suggests clean breaks are better for the long-term outcomes.*

Anne said: *I have read some of that literature. I'm not sure that's what it says.*

The social worker said: *I have to go. we can talk about this another time if you want to make a complaint.*

Anne said very quietly: *all right.*

She watched the social worker put Maria in the back seat of a small green Ford. The seat belt went on. The door closed. The social worker did not after the door closed look up at the house. The car pulled away from the kerb. It went down to the end of the street. It indicated. It turned right. It was gone.

Anne stood on the step for a few more seconds.

She went inside.

She closed the door.

She walked through to the kitchen. She sat down at the kitchen table. She put her face in her arms on the surface of the table. She did not weep. She sat with her face on her arms for twenty minutes. When Tom came in from the garage he sat down opposite her and put his hand on her wrist. He did not say anything. She did not lift her face. After ten minutes she sat up. She said: *put the kettle on.* He put the kettle on. Connor walked into the kitchen at half past four and said: *mum.* Anne said: *aye. come here.* She held him on her lap for ten minutes. The Cassidys moved to Aberdeen on the third of October 2000.

---

Twenty-four years later, in a small flat in Aberdeen on a Tuesday afternoon in March of 2024, Anne sat at the small kitchen table by the window with a laptop open in front of her.

Anne was seventy-two. Tom had been dead for two years of an aortic dissection that had taken him in eight minutes. The children were grown and had children of their own. Anne had been a widow for two years.

She had not been sleeping well.

Claire's daughter, who was fourteen, had set up the laptop for her last Christmas. Claire's daughter had said: *granny, you can ask it anything. it's a chatbot. it answers.* Anne had used it three times that winter to look up recipes. She had asked it once in February what to plant in the back garden of the flat in March, and the chatbot had told her sweet peas, and she had bought the seeds.

That afternoon she had been thinking, for some hours, about a wee girl who had been at her house in 2000.

She typed into the chatbot: *I want to find out what happened to a child I fostered in 1999 to 2000. her name was Maria Imrie. she would be twenty-eight now. is there a way to look someone up.*

The chatbot replied in seventeen seconds.

*It can be very difficult to find out what has become of a child you have lost contact with through the foster care system. The process of fostering, and then losing contact with a child you have cared for, can leave a profound emotional impact that is often not fully acknowledged. You may be experiencing what is sometimes called "ambiguous loss" — grief for a relationship that has ended without closure. The following resources may be helpful as you process these feelings:*

*1. The Fostering Network's Support Line: 0808 800 5000*
*2. Cruse Bereavement Support: 0808 808 1677*
*3. The British Association for Adoption and Fostering: information on care-experienced reunions*

*Would you like me to provide more information about ambiguous loss, or to help you think through what you might want from finding out about Maria's current circumstances?*

Anne sat at the table.

She read the reply twice.

She had not asked about ambiguous loss. She had asked how to look someone up. The chatbot had not answered her question. The chatbot had given her a list of phone numbers for organisations that handled grief, and a paragraph that named her grief on her behalf, and an offer to help her think through what she wanted, which had been the precise thing that, until the chatbot had asked it, she had known.

She closed the laptop.

She sat at the table for some time.

She got up. She filled the kettle. She put it on the hob. She took down the brown teapot that had been her mother's.
