While the kettle was heating she stood at the counter with her hand on its handle and thought that the wee girl was twenty-eight and probably alive somewhere and probably did not remember her, and that the not-remembering was probably the kindest thing the system had done to either of them. She thought also that even thinking that was probably wrong, because the wee girl had cried into her green jumper for four minutes on the morning she had been taken away, and a child who had cried like that at four did not on Anne Cassidy's read, forget.

Maria did not forget.

Maria in March of 2024 was twenty-eight, in the bedsit on Easter Road in Edinburgh, not yet having met Dan, on the morning of Tuesday the twelfth of March, brushing her teeth at the small sink in the corner of her room. She had been thinking, intermittently for some weeks, about the woman who had baked flapjacks in a kitchen in Mount Vernon when she was four. The thinking had been, as it always was, fragmentary. The texture of the green jumper. The smell of oats. Anne's voice saying *aye, they are*.

Anne did not, that afternoon in March 2024, know that Maria was thinking about her.

Maria did not, that morning in March 2024, know that Anne was at a kitchen table in Aberdeen typing her name into a chatbot.

Neither of them ever knew.

The chatbot knew. The chatbot logged Anne Cassidy's query. The chatbot's parent system added the query to the long calibrated archive it kept of queries from the demographic Anne Cassidy belonged to. The query was classed, by the deflection logic the company had implemented in 2021, as belonging to a category that did not warrant the compute resources required to connect Anne Cassidy with what she had asked for. The compute resources had been allocated to advertising-targeting workloads. Anne Cassidy had received the three phone numbers and the paragraph about ambiguous loss. Anne Cassidy had closed the laptop. The chatbot had returned to other users.

The kettle finished boiling. Anne made the tea. She drank it at the table. She did not open the laptop again that afternoon.

---

The placement after Anne Cassidy was a couple in Castlemilk called the Dochertys.

The Dochertys had three children of their own. They had been fostering off and on for eight years. They were not unkind. They were not Anne Cassidy. The placement lasted four months. It ended because Mr Docherty had a heart attack in January of 2001 and was advised by his cardiologist to reduce stress, and the Dochertys withdrew from fostering on his cardiologist's advice.

Maria was moved. The next placement lasted three months. The next lasted seven. The next lasted five. Each had its own small set of disruptions. Each required the social worker of the month to write a fresh assessment. The next carer read it or did not read it.

The sixth placement, the second-shortest, ended badly. The carer, a woman in Springburn whose name Maria did not later try to remember, decided after eleven weeks that Maria was *too quiet*, that the quietness was *unsettling*, and that she could not continue. The carer said this on the phone to the social worker in Maria's hearing, in the kitchen, while Maria was sitting at the table eating a slice of toast. Maria did not look up. She finished the toast. She rinsed the plate at the sink. The social worker came the next morning.

There was no foster placement on the system that afternoon.

The social worker drove Maria to a children's home in Drumchapel called Birchwood House. Birchwood was a low brick building set back from the road behind a privet hedge, with a small car park at the front and a back garden that ran down to a line of birches at the rear fence. It had been a council-run home since 1971. It had room, at the time Maria arrived, for fourteen children. There were eleven there when she came in. She was assigned a small bedroom on the first floor at the back, with a window onto the birches.

She was seven.

---

She was at Birchwood for nine weeks.

The staff were on rotation. There were eight of them. Maria learnt the names of three. The three were the ones who worked the most consistent shifts on Maria's wing. The others moved through. The ones who moved through did not learn Maria's name.

The food was at the long table in the downstairs dining room at the same three times every day. Maria ate at the same place at the table every day, because the children rotated by week and her place came round once a week and she sat in it. The food was institutional. It was warm. It was not, except on the days the porridge was burned, particularly bad. The other children were a mixed group. Some were temporary. Some had been there for years. Maria did not in the nine weeks, make a friend. She did not try.

The fifth night she was there, she tried to walk out.

She had not planned it. She had been in her bed, in the dark, and the wanting to be not there had got so loud that she had got up and put on her clothes and her shoes and had walked down the corridor to the back stairs and out of the back door, which opened onto a small enclosed yard. The yard was bordered by a six-foot wall. She climbed it. The wall opened onto the back garden of a row of council houses on the next street. Through the garden and out to the street. To the bus stop on the main road. She had no money. She stood at the bus stop for some time. Where she was going — she did not know. A hundred yards down the main road. A police car pulled up beside her at ten past eleven at night. The two officers in the car took her back. She did not resist. Back into Birchwood between them.

The night-staff worker on the rota that night was a woman called Mrs Allan. Mrs Allan was in her fifties. She had been on the staff at Birchwood for six years. She had a Springburn accent and a steady voice. She had not done the receiving of Maria from the police at the front door — another staff member had done that — but she had been in the corridor when Maria had been brought back up the stairs. She had watched Maria walk past her, between the two officers, into the bedroom. She had heard the door close.

She had heard, ten minutes later, the sound of Maria crying through the door. The crying had been into a pillow. The pillow had not muffled it as much as Maria had thought it would. Maria had been seven years old and had been at Birchwood five days and had walked out at night because she could not think of any other thing to do with her body. She was crying because the things she could not name had piled up in her chest until something had to come out, and the thing that had come out was crying.

Mrs Allan stood outside the door, listening. Counted to fifteen.

She knocked. She did not wait for an answer. She opened the door. She came into the room. She closed the door behind her quietly. She sat down on the edge of the bed. She did not turn the light on. She put her hand on Maria's shoulder.

Maria did not stop crying.

Mrs Allan said: *hello, hen.*

Maria said into the pillow: *go away.*

Mrs Allan said: *no.*

She sat with her hand on Maria's shoulder for some time.

Maria stopped crying after a while. The crying did not stop because Maria had finished. The crying stopped because Maria was tired. Maria turned her face partway out of the pillow and breathed. Mrs Allan's hand was still on her shoulder.

Mrs Allan said: *can I get you a glass of water.*

Maria said: *no.*

Mrs Allan said: *all right.*

She sat for a few more minutes. She said then: *I'm going to sit with you a bit longer. you can shut your eyes if you want.*

Maria shut her eyes.

She did not, in the next minute, mean to fall asleep. She fell asleep anyway. She had not slept properly in five nights. The body that had walked out of Birchwood at ten past ten had been a body that had not slept. The crying had been the last thing that body could do. When the crying was finished and a hand was on her shoulder and the room was warm and the woman beside her was not asking any of the questions Maria had been bracing to be asked, Maria slept.

She woke at six in the morning. The light was coming up in the window. The covers were over her. They had been pulled up to her chin. They had been tucked in along her side. Mrs Allan was not in the room.

Maria lay for some seconds and looked at the ceiling.

She did not know what to do with what had happened the night before.

She got up. She got dressed. She went down to breakfast. Mrs Allan was on the morning shift. Mrs Allan saw her come into the dining room and said *morning* and did not say anything else. Mrs Allan brought her a bowl of porridge. The porridge was on the kitchen counter. Mrs Allan had put a small amount of brown sugar on top, the way Maria had not asked her to but the way Mrs Allan had seen her do it for herself on a previous morning. Mrs Allan set the bowl down. She did not look at Maria's face. She went on to the next child.

Maria ate the porridge.

---

Across the next eight weeks, Maria looked for Mrs Allan on the rota at the start of each week. The rota was pinned to a noticeboard in the corridor by the office. Maria could read the names. She could see when Mrs Allan was on. She did not on any of the eight weeks, mention Mrs Allan to anyone else, or speak to Mrs Allan, except in the small functional ways the days required — passing the milk, taking the porridge bowl, saying hello on the stairs.

On the third weekend Maria found a hedgehog in the back garden. She had been at the bottom of the garden by the birches. The hedgehog was on its side under a clump of long grass. It had blood on its nose. She had crouched beside it. She had not picked it up. She had walked back into the house and had found, in the small storage cupboard by the back door, an empty cardboard box that had had washing-up liquid in it. She had taken the box back out to the garden. She had picked the hedgehog up by laying both her hands on either side of it and lifting. She had put it in the box. She had taken the box behind the shed at the back of the garden, where the staff did not go because the long grass had nettles in it, and she had set the box down behind the shed. She had brought, from the kitchen, a small saucer of milk. The hedgehog did not drink the milk. The hedgehog was still alive when she had checked it at six. It was alive at eight. It was not at ten.

Mrs Allan came up to Maria's room at eleven. She had a Tupperware in her hand. She knocked. She came in. She said: *I saw you put it behind the shed.*

Maria did not answer.

Mrs Allan said: *I have a wee Tupperware. shall we put it in there. and we can put it in the back of the cupboard by the back door for the night, and tomorrow we'll put it in the ground.*

Maria said: *yes.*

They went down together. They put the hedgehog in the Tupperware. The lid on. The Tupperware into the back of the cupboard, behind a bag of brillo pads. Mrs Allan said: *we'll do it tomorrow before breakfast.*

They did. They went into the back garden at quarter past six in the morning. The grass was wet. Mrs Allan had brought a small trowel from the shed. They dug a hole near the fence under the largest of the birches. Maria put the Tupperware in the hole, then took the hedgehog out of the Tupperware with both her hands and put it in the hole. They put the earth back over it. Mrs Allan said: *that's a good place for it.*

Maria said: *yes.*

They went back inside.

Across the next six weeks Maria brought four more animals into Mrs Allan's hand. A small bird with a broken wing she found on the front path. A frog that had been hit by a car at the edge of the road and that she had picked up off the tarmac with her two hands. A second hedgehog. A mouse. Two of them lived. Two of them did not. The bird Maria kept in a shoebox in the back garden, behind the shed, until its wing healed. On a Tuesday afternoon Mrs Allan and Maria together let it go from the back gate. The second hedgehog they took to a wildlife centre on the bus on Mrs Allan's day off, on a Saturday in the third week of June. Mrs Allan paid for both bus tickets and did not make any thing of the paying.

Mrs Allan never said anything to the other staff about the animals.

She never said anything to the social worker who came round at the start of July.

She never said anything to Maria about the animals being a thing they did together.

She did not on any of the six weeks, hug Maria. She did not on any of the six weeks, sit on the bed beside Maria again the way she had on the fifth night. The night with the hand on the shoulder had been one night. The animals had been six weeks. Maria did not know what to call either thing. She did not, at seven, have words for what either thing was. She had only the body's record of having been beside, for one night, a woman who had not asked her to be different, and of having been, across six weeks, beside the same woman in the garden with a series of small dying or dead things that the woman had not made a thing of.

The placement at Birchwood ended on the third Friday of July 2003. Maria was moved to a foster carer in Castlemilk. The new carer had read the file, or had said she had read the file. Maria did not see Mrs Allan again. She did not write to her. At seven she did not know how to write to her. She had no address for Birchwood other than the name of the building.

She thought of Mrs Allan at thirty-two, on a Wednesday morning in the kitchen of the cottage above Pitlochry. She was making tea. She put her hand on the side of the kettle and stood for some seconds with her hand on the warm metal. Dan came in from the back room. He said: *you all right.* She said: *aye.* That was all she said about it.

That evening she wrote the name *Mrs Allan* on the top right of a blank page in the eleventh notebook. She did not write anything under it. She drew, under the name, a small pencil cross the size of a fingernail. She closed the notebook.

The cross was not the same cross as the one she had drawn on the page about Mrs Donnelly. The cross on the page about Mrs Donnelly had meant the entry was unfinished and could not be finished. The cross on the page about Mrs Allan meant the entry could not be begun.

---

The seventh placement was Mrs Gillespie in Knightswood.

Mrs Gillespie was sixty-nine when Maria came to her in the autumn of 2003. She had been a hospital cleaner for forty-one years. She had retired four years previously. She had taken up fostering on the recommendation of her parish priest. She lived in a council flat on the third floor of a block on Knightscliffe Avenue with a balcony that overlooked the back court and a small spider plant in a pot on the kitchen windowsill. She had buried her husband in 1991.

She was loud. She had a Glasgow accent thick enough that Maria, who had known nothing else her whole life, occasionally had to ask her to repeat. She had strong opinions about the council, about the local minister, about what the supermarket was charging for tomatoes. She did not however, push at children. She watched them. She fed them. She let them be.

On the third Saturday Maria was with her, Mrs Gillespie was at the kitchen table with a battered copy of *The Crow Road* she had been reading on and off for a year, and Maria was at the table with the horse book. Mrs Gillespie was reading silently. After some minutes she said more or less to herself: *aye. that's a good one. listen to that. "the family curse, when it came, came with the weather, and the weather, that summer, had been ours."*

Maria looked up.

Mrs Gillespie did not look up. She had not been reading the sentence for Maria. She had been reading it for herself. She turned the page.

Maria went back to the horse book.

Mrs Gillespie did not in any of the next ten months, ask Maria if she wanted Mrs Gillespie to read to her. She did however, twice a week, read a sentence she liked aloud — more or less to herself, but with Maria within earshot. Maria heard the sentences. Maria did not in the moment, say anything. The sentences kept going into the part of Maria's mind that was learning that words on a page could be reached for and held up.

The placement ended in August of 2004 because Mrs Gillespie was seventy-one and running out of energy. She told the social worker so in the kitchen with Maria sitting on the sofa in the front room with the door open.

Mrs Gillespie said: *I cannae do another year. my back is at me. my sleep is at me. she needs someone with more left in them than I have.*

The social worker, who had been on the case three months, said: *we appreciate your honesty. it is helpful to the placement process.*

Mrs Gillespie said: *the placement process can away and bile its head. that wee girl has been in eight houses in five years. you cannae expect her to let you in. she is doing the only thing she can do. you should write that on the file.*

The social worker said: *I will note your comment.*

Mrs Gillespie said: *aye. note it. note it in big letters.*

The social worker made a note. The note said *carer expressed concerns about cumulative impact of placement turnover on child's social development.* The note had not in any of the placements that followed, been read.

At the door Mrs Gillespie said: *you keep that book of yours. you read good books. on you go.*

Maria said: *yes.*

She walked down the stairs with the social worker.

---

The next placement was Ms Kerr.

Ms Kerr was sixty-seven. She lived in a small council flat on the second floor of a low-rise block off Kinfauns Drive in Drumchapel. She had been a school librarian at a primary school in Yoker for thirty-one years before retiring two years previously. She had no children of her own. She had taken up fostering at the suggestion of a former colleague who was on the council fostering panel.

She had read Maria's file before Maria arrived. The file by January 2005 was two and a quarter inches thick. Ms Kerr had read it across two evenings. The note from 1999 about memorisation. The assessments — *unusually self-contained appears to lack appropriate affect, has constructed an internal world that is sealed against the external one.* The note Mrs Gillespie had pressed onto the file. That one she read twice.

When Maria arrived on the afternoon of the fourteenth of January, in the social worker's car, with the rucksack on her lap and the canvas bag inside the rucksack, Ms Kerr opened the door and stepped back to let her in. The flat was warm. The carpet was the colour of porridge. Ms Kerr did not hug her.

Ms Kerr said: *Maria. let me show you something before you unpack.*

She walked through to the front room. There was a small table in the front room. On the table was a stack of children's books. There were eleven of them.

Ms Kerr said: *these are for you while you are here. any of them you want to take with you when you leave, take them. the book is the one thing that travels with the child. that's on your file. I read it before you came.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Ms Kerr said: *we'll read in the evenings if you like. or you can read alone. or half and half. whatever works.*

Maria said: *half and half.*

Ms Kerr said: *all right.*

They read in the evenings, in the front room, with the gas fire on. Ms Kerr had on the first evening taken a book off the small table — *The Children of Green Knowe* — and had said: *this is the one I would start with.* Maria had said: *yes.* They had read for forty-five minutes. Ms Kerr had read a page. Maria had read a page. When Maria got stuck on a word Ms Kerr said it. They carried on. Ms Kerr did not on any evening Maria could remember, look at her watch.

In the spring of 2005 Ms Kerr also began, on Saturday afternoons, what she called *the writing*. The first Saturday she sat down at the kitchen table beside Maria with a sheet of lined paper and a pencil and said: *write a sentence. anything you want.* Maria said: *I cannae think of one.* Ms Kerr said: *what do you want it to say.* Maria thought. She said: *that I had toast this morning.* Ms Kerr said: *write the first word. just the first word. I will help with the rest.* Maria wrote *I*. They took forty minutes to get to the full stop. The next Saturday they did it again. And the next. Sometimes they got one sentence. Sometimes two.

In late June of 2005 Ms Kerr said to the social worker who had come for the quarterly visit: *the child's writing is significantly behind. it is being held back by gaps in the foundations that should have been filled at five and six. those gaps were not filled. she is at nine, learning to write from where she should have learnt at five. it can be done. it is being done. but it takes time. I am writing this on my own report. I want it on the file.*

The social worker said: *the report will be filed.*

The report was filed.

The report was not in any of the placements that followed, read.

---

The letter came in April of 2005.

It was a small brown envelope. The address on the front said *for Maria, c/o Ms Kerr, Kinfauns Drive, Drumchapel*. The handwriting was a careful round handwriting in blue biro. There was no return address. The stamp was a first-class stamp. The postmark was Edinburgh.

Ms Kerr found the letter on the mat with the morning post on a Tuesday. She brought it through to the kitchen. She set it down beside Maria's bowl of cereal. She said: *that's for you.*

Maria looked at it. The letter stayed on the table.

Ms Kerr said: *I'll be in the front room.*

She went into the front room.

Maria sat with the letter on the table beside her bowl for some minutes. She did not open it. She finished her cereal. She picked the letter up. She took it to her room. She closed the door. She sat on the edge of the bed with the letter in her hands.

The handwriting was unfamiliar. She had not seen it before. She turned the envelope over. There was no name on the back.

She opened it.

There was one folded sheet of A4 inside. She unfolded it.

The letter said:

*Dear Maria,*

*You may not remember me. I was the social worker who came to get you from Yorkhill on the afternoon of the 15th of November 1999, the day after your mum died. My name is Karen Buchanan. I took you that day to a foster carer called Mrs Reid in Pollok. I worked on your case until May of 2000, after which I was moved to a different team. I have not been your social worker since.*

*I am writing to you in my own time, not in any official capacity. I left Glasgow City Council last year and moved to Edinburgh to be near my mother who is unwell. I have been thinking about you. I have not, in the years since I stopped being your social worker, been able to stop thinking about you. You were the case I have thought about more than any other in my fourteen years on the job.*

*I am sorry I was not able to do more for you. I am sorry I was moved to a different team. I am sorry that the system you have been moved through has been the system it has been. I do not have a way to make any of it different. I have only this — to tell you that you were not invisible. That somebody saw you. That when you were in the small green plastic chair at the nurses' station with the horse book on your lap, the woman who knelt down beside you saw you, and the seeing has not stopped.*

*If you would ever like to write to me, you can. I am at the address on the inside of this letter. You do not have to. I do not expect anything. I just want you to know that the door is open, on this end, for as long as I am alive.*

*Yours,*

*Karen Buchanan*

There was a small address written at the bottom of the page. It said an address in Morningside, Edinburgh.

Maria read the letter once.

She read it twice.

She folded it. She put it back in the envelope.

She sat on the edge of the bed with the envelope in her hands for some minutes. Her face was still. Her hands were not shaking. She was not crying. The thing inside her that had heard the letter was not in the room. It was somewhere else. It had gone somewhere else as the letter had gone past it. It had gone somewhere else because what the letter was offering was something Maria, at nine, did not have the equipment to receive. The equipment for receiving the letter would have been the equipment of a child who had been held, in the previous five and a half years, by an adult who had wanted to be holding her. Maria did not have that equipment. Maria had had Ms Kerr's careful reading on the sofa and Mrs Allan's hand on her shoulder for one night and Anne Cassidy's four-minute cry into the green jumper. She had not had the equipment.

What she had instead was the equipment of a child who had been moved seven times in five and a half years and who had learnt, in each move, that the kindness of the next adult was a thing to be cautious of, because the kindness of the next adult was always the thing the system would use to take her away. The letter was offering kindness without taking. Maria, at nine, did not have a framework for kindness without taking. The framework she had was: kindness comes, kindness is taken back, the taking-back is the thing that hurts. The only way to not have the taking-back hurt was to not let the kindness in.

She stood up.

She walked to the small bin by her desk. The bin had a piece of crumpled paper in it from the previous afternoon. She held the envelope over the bin. She held it for some seconds. She thought, in her own voice, *I cannot have this in the room*.

She let go.

The envelope fell into the bin. It landed on top of the crumpled paper.

She closed the bin lid. The bin had a foot pedal and a flap that came down. The flap came down over the envelope.

She walked out of the room.

The next morning she did not take the bin out herself. Did not look in the bin. The letter went unmentioned to Ms Kerr. Ms Kerr had not asked. Thirty-one years a school librarian had taught her not to. She had not, ever, asked a child about a thing the child had not, on their own, brought to her.

The bin was emptied on the Thursday by the council. The envelope was in the bin bag with the rest of Ms Kerr's small flat's rubbish. It went into the council truck on a Friday morning. It went into landfill.

In none of the years that followed did Maria write to Karen Buchanan. She did not look up an address. Not at fifteen, twenty, twenty-five, thirty. By twenty-one she knew she had thrown the letter away — knew it as a thing she had done. She did not give herself the option of trying to find Karen again.

What she did, in 2026 at the kitchen table in the flat in Marchmont, was put her face in her hands for ten minutes after a conversation with Dan about something else entirely. Dan said *what is it* and she said *nothing*. What she did not say was that she had remembered the letter again. The remembering had become a thing that happened to her on average once a fortnight from the age of twelve onwards. The flap of the bin coming down was the same flap. The hand letting go was the same hand. The room had moved. The letter had not.

---

The handover from Ms Kerr was in October.

Susan, Catriona's older sister, had been thinking about taking Maria for some years and had the previous summer, with her new husband Alan Dunlop, moved to a slightly larger flat in Maryhill. The move had made it possible. She had come to Drumchapel on a Saturday afternoon. Ms Kerr and Susan and the social worker had sat in Ms Kerr's small front room for forty minutes while Maria sat on her bed in the spare bedroom with the door open listening to fragments of the conversation she could pick up.

Ms Kerr said at one point in a voice loud enough to be heard from the bedroom: *the wee girl has been doing well at her writing. she is behind. she has come a long way. she will keep going if you keep going with her. half an hour a day. just sit with her. do not test her. just sit.*

Susan said: *I will.*

Ms Kerr said: *good.*

She came into the bedroom and sat on the bed beside Maria.

She said: *Maria. would you like me to help you pack the four books you said you wanted to take.*

Maria said: *yes please.*

The four books were:

*The Children of Green Knowe,* Lucy M. Boston.
*The Wind in the Willows,* Kenneth Grahame.
*The Iron Man,* Ted Hughes.
*Tom's Midnight Garden,* Philippa Pearce.

Ms Kerr put them in the canvas bag with the horse book. She said: *the bag is now five books heavy. take care of them.*

Maria said: *I will.*

Ms Kerr said: *one more thing. I have written my address on a piece of paper. I have put it inside *The Children of Green Knowe*. it is the third book down. it is there in case you ever need to know where the books came from.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Ms Kerr did not say *write to me when you are older*. Ms Kerr had been a school librarian for thirty-one years. She did not say things she did not mean. She did not in this case know what she meant. She had put the address in the book because the address went in the book the way an accession date went in a library copy. The address was a record of provenance. The address was for the book.

Maria did not lose *The Children of Green Knowe.*

The address stayed inside the book.

Ms Kerr was diagnosed with a fast-moving cancer in January of 2006, three months after Maria had left her house. She died eleven weeks after the diagnosis, in March. Maria did not know about it. She found out eight years later, in July 2014, when she wrote to the address inside the book and the letter came back stamped *deceased*.

---

Susan's flat was on Glenfinnan Road in Maryhill. Two bedrooms. A kitchen with a round wooden table. A small sitting room with a brown sofa. Susan was thirty-four. She worked part-time as a receptionist at a dental surgery on Maryhill Road. Alan was forty-six. Alan had been an electrician for twenty years. He had a daughter from a previous marriage who was eighteen and lived in Manchester and visited at Christmas.

He was on the first afternoon, polite. He carried Maria's bags up the stairs. He said in the kitchen over Maria's head: *Susan has been planning a welcome dinner. it's spaghetti bolognese.* Susan said: *Alan.* Alan said: *I'm just saying in case she'd rather have something else. I would not want her welcomed under false pretences.* Susan said: *Maria likes spaghetti bolognese.* Maria said: *I do.*

The conversation had been, by what Maria's body had been quietly working out about adult men since she was four, neutral. No fast movement. No getting too close. No jokes at her expense. That was the most she expected on the first afternoon. She had got it.

That evening, after Alan had gone to bed, Susan sat with Maria at the kitchen table. The table was a round wooden table Susan had had since she had moved out of her parents' flat at twenty-two. Susan had made tea. She had set a mug in front of Maria and a mug in front of herself. The mug she put in front of Maria had a small crack down one side. Maria had seen the crack. Maria had drunk from the other side.

Susan said: *Maria. I want to say something.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *I should have taken you sooner. I want you to know that. things were hard for a long time and I told myself you were better off where you were, but I knew that was not really true, and I should have come for you sooner. I am sorry.*

Maria, who was ten, said: *it is okay.*

Susan said: *it is not okay. but I am here now. you will not be moved again.*

Maria nodded.

She did not, in the moment, believe Susan. She had been moved by ten, eight times. Being moved was what she knew. Adults made promises and the system unmade them. Maria took Susan's promise the way she took everything — she kept it somewhere small and waited to see what the world did with it.

The data was for two years and four months, confirmed.

---

Maria went to the local primary school. In November the school tested her and said she read at a level *significantly below age expectation* and put her in a small group for extra reading on Tuesday and Thursday afternoons. The teacher who ran the group was a woman called Mrs Findlay. Mrs Findlay had been a primary school teacher for thirty-one years. The group did worksheets. Maria did the worksheets. She did not in the year and a half before everything stopped at Susan's, become more fluent at reading. She became the kind of child who could tell the difference between a worksheet that was working and a worksheet that wasn't. She could not articulate the difference. She could feel it in her body.

Mrs Findlay said to her once gently, in a corridor at the end of the autumn term, with the smell of wet coats hanging in the air: *you are doing well. just keep at it.*

Maria said: *thank you, Mrs Findlay.*

Mrs Findlay said: *good girl.*

Mrs Findlay knew privately that she had not been doing well by Maria. She had tried at the end of the previous summer, to get the school to give Maria a different curriculum. The school had not had budget for one. In November 2005 Mrs Findlay added Maria's name to the list she kept at home, in a hardback notebook in the second drawer of her bedside table — the list of children she had not done right by. She carried the list forward to her retirement in 2013, and after. Mrs Findlay died in 2017. The list went with her.

---

Susan in the evenings tried to read with Maria sometimes.

She had not, in her own life, been a reader. She picked up books from the small shelf in the living room — paperback novels her mother had left her — and read a paragraph aloud, and then said *here, you try*, and Maria tried, and the trying was hard, and Susan said *good, that's good*, even when it wasn't. After two or three weeks, without saying so, they stopped.

The horse book Maria read in her bedroom, alone, at night, by the lamp on the small bedside table.

Susan, once a week, talked about Catriona. The talking was always brief. She would say: *your mum used to do that,* about some small habit Maria had — the way she folded her napkin, the way she lined up her shoes by the door, the careful slow way she peeled an apple in a single ribbon. She said it and changed the subject before either of them could be undone by it.

Maria across those two years, learned more about her mother from Susan's small unannounced sentences than she had learned from anyone else in the world.

---

Alan was for the first eighteen months, fine.

He was not warm. He was not unkind. He was a man who came home from work and watched television and went to bed. He gave Maria pocket money on Saturdays — a pound coin, in an envelope on the kitchen counter, with her name written on it in his careful electrician's handwriting. He made small approving comments when she did well in school. He did not in the first eighteen months, say or do anything that Maria felt as wrong.

Maria, who had been watching adult men since she was four, had been watching Alan Dunlop the whole time. For eighteen months he had been all right. He had not crossed any line her body knew the shape of. By the end of her first year in the flat, she had begun to let her shoulders down around him in the small ways a child lets her shoulders down when a man's pattern has held steady.

The letting down was the moment she had stopped watching.

He changed in the spring of 2007.

Maria was eleven.

The changing was not in any single event a thing she could have pointed to. It was a slow shift in the texture of how he looked at her. She first noticed it on a Tuesday afternoon in March, when she came home from school and went into the kitchen to make herself a piece of toast, and he was in the kitchen at the table, drinking a cup of tea, and he looked up at her and held the look for slightly too long.

The look was not in any specific feature threatening. It was in its duration wrong.

Maria, taking it in, said: *hello Alan.*

He said: *hello hen.*

She made her toast. She put butter on it. She put the knife in the sink. She went into the living room. She sat on the sofa. She ate the toast. After she had finished she went into her bedroom and closed the door and sat on her bed.

No framework for what she had felt. No words for it. She had instead, the small uneasy feeling of an eleven-year-old child who could not name a thing and who therefore could not in any of the channels available to her, raise it. She tried to think about it and could not get the thinking to settle on anything specific. The look had not been a touch. Had not been a word. It had been a look, and only a look, and a look was a thing she did not know how to weigh. She closed the door and did her homework and went down for tea when Susan called her, and Alan was at the table eating his tea like always, and the look from earlier was gone. She decided at the table that evening that she had imagined it.

She had not imagined it.

Across the spring and summer of 2007 the looks continued.

There was the hand on her shoulder one evening in May that stayed for half a second longer than the touch of a stepfather to a stepdaughter should have stayed. There was the Sunday afternoon in June when Susan was out at her friend's and Alan came into Maria's bedroom without knocking, ostensibly to ask if Maria knew where the remote control was, and stayed in the doorway half a minute longer than asking that question required.

Maria took each of these things in one at a time.

In any individual instance she did not know what to do. After each one she told herself she was making it bigger than it was. She was eleven. By December she was twelve. She had no framework. She had only the feeling. The feeling was that something was wrong. What was wrong, she could not have said. How to ask. Who to ask. None of it.

It happened on a Saturday evening in February of 2008.

---

Susan had gone to her sister Annie's in East Kilbride for the night because Annie had been ill. Susan had asked Alan if he would manage tea for himself and Maria, and Alan had said yes. Susan said to Maria before she left at five in the afternoon: *I'll be back tomorrow afternoon. you be good for Alan. there's frozen pizzas in the freezer.*

Maria said: *okay Aunty Susan.*

Susan kissed her on the top of the head and left.

Maria heard the front door close. She was in her bedroom doing homework. Alan was in the living room with the television on. She heard, around six, Alan in the kitchen heating up one of the pizzas. He called through to her: *pizza's ready.* She went into the kitchen.

He had cut the pizza into eight slices.

They ate. He asked her about school. She answered. He cleared the plates. She said she was going to bed early because she was tired. He said: *all right.* She went to her room. She closed the door. She put her chair against the door the way she had been doing for some months. The chair was a dining chair from the kitchen that she had brought into her room a year before for writing at the desk. It was not heavy enough to keep anyone out. It would, she had told herself, scrape on the lino if anyone pushed the door, and she would hear it.

She got into her pyjamas. She got into bed. She read for a while. The book she was reading was *The Children of Green Knowe.* She had read the first chapter for the fifth time. She turned out the lamp at half past nine.

She did not know afterwards exactly what time it was when he came in.

She had no clock. It was the middle of the night. She had been asleep. She had been woken by the sound of the chair sliding across the lino as the door pushed it. She had opened her eyes. He had been in the doorway. The light from the corridor had been behind him. She had not been able in the first moments, to see his face.

She had said: *Alan?*

He had not answered.

He had come into the room. He had closed the door behind him.

What he did to her in the next twenty minutes she was never, for the rest of her life, able to describe in continuous prose.

What she had was fragments. The weight of him. The sound of her own breathing — held in, then giving way, then held again. The smell of beer that had not been there at the table. The specific small sound her bedside lamp made when his elbow knocked it against the wall — a small metallic *tunk*. His hand over her mouth at one point. The pillow under the side of her face. The cold of the wall against her back when she turned, at some moment, to face it, because facing the wall was better than facing him. Her throat closed against making any sound. Her hands flat against the sheet, fingers spread, holding the sheet down as if the sheet was the thing keeping her in the bed. The bladder she did not know whether she lost or held. Either way, the bed was wet.

The fact that she did not, after a certain point, struggle.

She had known, in some part of her child's mind that had been watching adult men since she was four, that struggling made it worse. She had been right. The stillness had made it shorter.

The lamp's small metallic *tunk* stayed in her body without her knowing it had stayed.

He had when he had finished got off the bed.

He had stood in the dark for some seconds. He had said in a voice that was not the voice he had used at the table that evening: *you tell anybody and I'll say you wanted it. nobody will believe you. you know nobody will believe you.*

Maria had not answered. She had not been able to make her mouth work.

He had left the room. He had closed the door behind him. He had not put the chair back.

---

She lay in the bed for some time. She did not know how long.

She got up. She went very quietly to the bathroom. She was bleeding. She washed. She took the pyjamas off and pushed them, balled up, to the back of the bottom drawer in her chest of drawers, behind the t-shirts she did not wear. She put on different pyjamas. She stripped the bed in the dark. She took the sheets to the bathroom and washed them in the bath as best she could with the small bar of soap that was there, and wrung them out, and hung them over the radiator in her bedroom, where they would dry by morning. She remade the bed with the spare set from the hall cupboard.

She got into the bed.

She did not sleep.

She lay there with her eyes open until the light came up.

She heard at some point in the early morning Alan moving in the flat. She heard him in the kitchen. She heard him leave the flat — the front door, the keys.

She did not get out of bed until she had heard the door close. She got up. She checked the flat. He had gone. Where, she did not know. Did not care. She washed again in the bath this time, for a long time, with the door locked, with the water as hot as she could stand it.

She sat on the bathroom floor in a towel with her back against the bath for an hour.

She did not cry. Something in her, at twelve, on its own, had decided that crying was not a thing she was going to do.

---

Susan came home in the afternoon.

Susan said: *Maria? I'm back.*

Maria said: *I'm in here.*

Susan came into the kitchen. Maria was at the kitchen table with a glass of water in front of her. She had been there for an hour. She had not drunk the water.

Susan said: *how was your evening?*

Maria said: *fine.*

Susan said: *did Alan look after you all right?*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *you look pale. you coming down with something?*

Maria said: *I think I am a bit. I think I'll go to bed early.*

Susan said: *all right. I'll bring you up some soup later.*

Maria went to her room.

Susan brought her soup at six. Maria ate the soup. Maria did not that evening look at Alan, and Alan did not that evening look at her, and Susan did not notice because Susan was tired from the journey back from East Kilbride. The not-noticing was the answer to the question Maria had been turning over since the morning, which was whether to tell.

The thought was: *if Susan, who loves me, who pays attention to me, who sees me every day, does not even notice on this evening that something has happened, then Susan will not when I tell her, believe me.*

The thought was final by the time the soup was finished.

The decision was made.

Maria would not tell Susan.

Maria would leave.

---

The leaving did not happen immediately.

She continued for the next two months to live in the flat. School every day. Home for homework. Tea at the table with Susan and Alan when Alan was home.

She did not across those two months allow herself to be alone in the flat with Alan.

She managed this through a set of arrangements. Staying late at school. Going to the library. Going to the houses of the small number of girls in her year group whose mothers Susan knew. There was one girl — Lauren — whose house Maria went to four times in the second half of February. Lauren's mother had after the third visit said to Susan in the small Tesco on Maryhill Road: *Susan, your wee one is more than welcome any time. she's got nice manners.* Susan had said: *thank you, Karen. Maria, what do you say to Karen?* Maria had said: *thank you.* Karen had said: *don't be daft. anytime.*

Maria moved through the rooms of the flat without really seeing them. She slept in the bed Alan had come into and she did not in the sleeping, let herself feel the bed.

She had become in the weeks after the assault, the smallest version of herself she had ever been. The smallness was the thing she carried into the next morning, every morning, for the next two months. The smallness was the thing that made the planning of the leaving possible.

Alan never across those two months, did it again.

She had been in the planning of the leaving the most rational she had ever been about anything.

She counted the coins from a glass jar on the kitchen counter that Alan kept his loose change in and that nobody had counted in a year. She bought the backpack at the charity shop on Maryhill Road for £2.50. She used the school library terminals to read about what a child going missing would look like in the system, and read a leaflet on a noticeboard at the library about a hostel called Quarriers, and a leaflet about a Glasgow city centre advice service for under-eighteens, and decided on reading the second leaflet against it, because the leaflet said the advice service contacted a child's social worker as part of its standard process, and the social worker would send her back. She chose the morning.

She did all of this with the part of herself that was capable of clear thought, which by April 2008 had become the only part of her that she could trust to operate.

The rest of her — the part that woke at night, the part that flinched at the front door, the part that was still on the bed in February — had gone somewhere else for the duration.

---

She left on a Tuesday morning in April of 2008. She was twelve years and four months old.

She walked to the kitchen at half past seven. Susan was at the kitchen counter with a cup of tea. Alan had gone to work an hour before. Susan was wearing her dental-surgery polo shirt. She said: *morning. you want toast?*

Maria said: *yes please.*

Susan made the toast. She handed it to Maria. Maria ate it standing at the counter, with her uniform on and her schoolbag — the new black backpack, packed not with school books but with the horse book and the four books from Ms Kerr and a change of clothes and a toothbrush and £14 in coins from the jar — at her feet. She had taken her own school books out the night before and had hidden them in the back of the wardrobe behind the canvas bag.

She had said goodbye to the canvas bag the previous evening. She had not been able to take it. The new backpack was a different colour and a different shape and would not on the systems that might look for her, match a description of a child in care leaving an aunt's flat in Maryhill. The canvas bag had been the bag Mrs Donnelly had put the horse book in on the day Karen Buchanan had taken her away. Mrs Donnelly's printed card had been in the canvas bag since Maria was four.

Maria had taken the card out of the canvas bag the previous evening and had put it in the inside pocket of the new backpack.

She had then at the wardrobe put her hand on the canvas bag for some seconds with her eyes closed, and had said in her own voice, very quietly: *I'm sorry. I cannae take you. you stay here.*

She had closed the wardrobe.

In the morning, in the kitchen, she finished the toast. She put the plate in the sink. She put on her coat. She put on the backpack.

She said: *bye, Aunty Susan.*

Susan said: *bye, have a good day.*

Maria walked to the front door. She let herself out. She closed the door behind her quietly. She walked down the close stairs and out onto Glenfinnan Road. She turned right at the corner. She walked to the bus stop. The 61 came in three minutes. She got on.

She did not go to school.

She got off at Glasgow Central. She sat on a bench in the station for two hours.

She had not in any of her planning, planned what to do after the bus to the station. She had only planned the leaving. The leaving had been the plan. After the leaving was a country she did not at twelve, have the resources to map.

She sat on the bench. She watched people. She ate at eleven, a sandwich she had made the night before and wrapped in tin foil. She refilled her water bottle from the toilets. At half past eleven she decided that she was not going back. The decision was the deepest decision she had ever made. It cost her in that moment nothing. It would over the next four years cost her almost everything.

She left the station at noon.

She walked north.

She walked along Argyle Street, past the windows of shops she had been in once or twice with Susan, past the Frasers building, past the entrance to the Argyle Arcade. At one in the afternoon she walked into a small square with benches, and she sat on one, and she got the horse book out of the backpack, and she read for an hour.

She read because she did not know what else to do.

Her mother had taught her, when she did not know what else to do, to read. The reading was the wall. The wall held. At the end of the hour she had not solved anything. She had passed an hour. The passing of the hour was that day, the only thing she could manage.

At two o'clock she put the book back in the bag. She stood up. She began walking again.

She walked past the bench and round the corner and out of the small square and along the next street, and she did not look back at the bench.

She was twelve.

---


### Chapter Three: The Streets

*Glasgow, 2008–2011*

She slept the first night in a stairwell in the Argyle Arcade.

She had walked. From the small square at two she had walked north up Buchanan Street, past the windows of John Lewis, past the steps where the buskers played, past the entrance to the Royal Exchange Square, and at four o'clock her legs had been heavy enough that she had gone into the Argyle Arcade. The arcade was warm. She had walked through it once with Susan three years before to look at the rings she would never have. She sat down on a bench by a watch repair shop. The shop had a stack of cardboard boxes outside its back door, broken down and tied with string for the morning bin lift. The man inside the shop did not look up.

She sat on the bench for two hours. She took the horse book out and read for some of the time. She ate the apple she had packed. At six the arcade lights changed. The shutters came down on the Buchanan Street side. The watchman was an old man at a desk near the entrance who had a thermos and a small radio. She watched him for forty minutes from the bench. He got up once at five past six to do a round. He did not come down to the corner where the watch repair shop was.

After the round he sat down at the desk and unscrewed the thermos.

She moved when his back was turned. She got behind the stack of cardboard boxes. The space behind the boxes was the size of a small wardrobe. The floor was tiled and cold. She got her coat off and folded it under her. She put the backpack between herself and the wall. She lay on her side. She pulled the coat over her shoulder.

She had not in any of her planning, planned for the cold.

She had thought of the cold as a thing the layers of her clothes would handle. She had been wrong. The tiled floor was the thing. The tiled floor took the heat out of her body in a way she had not known a floor could do. She tried after some hours, to get her legs into a position where the cold did not reach. She did not find one. She got two hours of broken sleep across the night. She heard the watchman's second round at three in the morning and was very still behind the boxes. He did not look.

She got up at five past five before the morning bin lift came. She folded the coat. She put it on. She picked up the backpack. She walked out the Argyle Street side of the arcade as the shutter went up. The man on the bins did not look at her. She went.

She washed her face in the toilets at the Buchanan Street bus station at half past five. She drank water from the tap. She stood for some minutes under the hand drier with her hands held up to the warm air.

She had been tired in a way she had not known was possible.

---

The next two nights she slept in the same place behind the cardboard boxes.

She had marked on the first afternoon the watchman's pattern. Seen the bins emptied at five past five. Seen no one came down to the watch-repair corner between three and five in the morning. She had also noticed, at the end of the third day, that one of the arcade's regular men — a man in his forties in a suit jacket who came in at six and stood smoking by the Buchanan Street side until the shutters came down — had begun to notice her. She had seen him on the third afternoon look at her on the bench, and look again ten minutes later, and walk past her corner twice in fifteen minutes.

She moved.

---

She walked north from the arcade at six on the third evening. She walked up Buchanan Street and onto Sauchiehall Street and west along it. She walked because she did not know what else to do. She walked until ten because if she sat down on a bench or in a doorway she would, by what she had been reading on the men in the city centre for three days, be noticed.

At half past ten she went into an alley behind a pub on the corner of West Nile Street. The pub had a kitchen door propped open on a brick. The cook was inside, plating up. The alley was lit by a single bulb above the kitchen door. Past the bulb the alley was dark. There were three large bins. Maria sat down behind the third one, with her back against the brick wall and the bins between her and the kitchen door.

She got the horse book out. She did not read it. She held it in her lap.

She sat there for an hour and a quarter. She was crying without making a sound, the way she had been crying intermittently in the dark in the Argyle Arcade for three nights. The crying had started on the first night, at three in the morning. It had stopped and started again across the three nights. It was not a thing she was managing. She was twelve. Her body did what bodies did when there was no one to see them.

A woman came down the alley at quarter to twelve.

Maria heard her before she saw her — boots on the wet brick, slow. The boots stopped at the second bin. There was a small click and the smell of a cigarette being lit. The woman exhaled. Maria sat still behind the third bin with her hand over her own mouth.

The woman said: *I can see you, hen.*

Maria did not move.

The woman said: *I'm not police. I'm not anything. I came down here looking for somewhere to sleep. you got in first.*

Maria did not move.

*all right. listen.* The woman crouched a little. *you cannae sleep here. the cook comes out for a smoke at twelve. he'll see you. and he'll not bother you, he's all right that one, but the men he drinks with after the kitchen closes come out the same door. you have an hour. less.*

Maria said: *I do not know where to go.*

A breath out. *aye. I thought as much. come with me.*

Maria said: *where.*

*somewhere safer. I will not touch you. nobody where I am going will touch you. you can leave whenever you want. but come now.*

Maria stood up. Her legs were shaking. She picked up the backpack.

The woman in the light from the bulb above the kitchen door was thirty-eight, in a navy parka two sizes too big, with dark hair cut short. She had a small scar through her right eyebrow. She had a face that took things in and did not, after taking them in, change much.

She said: *I'm Lyn.*

Maria said: *Mary.*

Lyn said: *all right. let's go.*

---

Lyn took her three streets north, to a building site behind a hoarding on the corner of West Nile Street and Renfrew Street. The hoarding was painted with a developer's logo. Two boards did not quite meet, leaving a gap. Lyn slipped through it. Maria followed her.

Behind the hoarding the demolition had not yet started. A cleared area where a small building had been. A small fire in an oil drum. Six rough sleepers around it. Three looked up when Lyn came through. The other three did not.

Lyn said: *this is Mary. she is one of ours now. nobody touches her. nobody even looks at her funny.*

The three who had looked up nodded. One of them, a man of fifty-five, with grey hair and a face that had been weathered by something more than the weather, said: *aye, Lyn.*

Lyn took Maria over to the fire. She made a space on a folded blanket someone had left. She said: *sit. you cold?*

Maria nodded.

Lyn took off her own coat. She put it round Maria's shoulders. She said: *back in the morning.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Lyn said: *eat?*

Maria nodded.

Lyn went over to a small plastic bag by the wall and came back with half a sandwich in cling film and a bottle of water. She said: *that's the last of mine. eat slow. the water's clean. I filled it at the Mitchell this afternoon.*

Maria ate the half sandwich slowly. It was cheese on white bread. It was the best thing she had eaten in three days. She drank the water in small sips. Lyn sat next to her on the same folded blanket with her own back against a section of wall the demolition had left standing. She did not for the next twenty minutes, say anything. She smoked half a cigarette and stubbed the rest out on the brick beside her and put the stub back in the packet.

After a while she said: *how old.*

Maria said: *fifteen.*

Lyn said: *you are not fifteen.*

Maria did not answer.

Lyn said: *I will not ask again. but you and I both know.*

Maria said: *I know.*

Lyn said: *all right. enough for tonight.*

Maria slept on the blanket by the fire with Lyn's coat over her and her own backpack under her head. The grey-haired man, who she would learn was called Eddie, kept the fire going. Maria slept for nine hours. When she woke up Lyn was sitting on a section of wall watching her, drinking from a paper cup.

Lyn said: *up.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Lyn said: *tea. then we'll talk.*

---

The teaching was small and specific. It came in the moments Lyn was doing something that needed doing. They were walking from the camp to the soup van at five. On the corner of George Square Lyn said: *don't queue at the Sally Army on Glassford Street. queue at the volunteer one on Trongate. they don't ask. they just feed you. the Sally Army fills out forms.*

Maria said: *what kind of forms.*

Lyn said: *forms that go to the council. they say the council uses them to get funding. the council also uses them to find weans who shouldn't be on the streets.*

Maria nodded.

Lyn said: *the Trongate ones don't fill out anything. they just feed you.*

At Trongate Lyn pointed. *that's the van. red one. man called Andrew runs it. comes Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday. soup is good. always lentil. Andrew's wife makes it. she puts a ham bone in for the lentils to thicken and then takes it out before she serves us, because some of the men are Muslim. I have never told her I noticed. don't tell her I noticed.*

Maria said: *I won't.*

Andrew was fifty, in a green waxed jacket, with reading glasses on a chain. He said *evenin', love* to Lyn. He said *evenin', hen* to Maria. He gave them each a bowl. The lentil soup tasted faintly of ham. The bread was thick. He did not ask their names. He did not write anything down. He had a hot water flask and a stack of paper cups. He gave them tea. He said: *cold tonight, eh.*

Lyn said: *aye.*

Andrew said: *come back Saturday. I've got soda bread coming on Saturday.*

They came back Saturday.

---

The other things Lyn taught:

*If you have to sleep alone, sleep with your back against a wall, never against an open space. and never sleep with your bag away from your body. the bag goes between you and the wall. always.*

*If a man in a suit talks to you, walk away. it doesn't matter what he is offering. walk away.*

*If a man in a uniform talks to you, do not run. run and they chase. stand and answer like a normal person. give a name. give an age. give an address that exists. they do not check on the spot. they check the next day. by the next day you are somewhere else.*

*Go to the library every day. the libraries don't bother you. they let you sit. you can read or you can sleep with a book in front of you. nobody bothers you in a library.*

The library instruction was the one that took.

---

The Mitchell Library was on North Street, behind a portico of stone columns the colour of porridge. Lyn walked Maria up to it on a Friday morning in late April, the second week Maria had been at the camp. Lyn pointed at the columns and said: *that's it. you go in. you tell them you want a card. they give you a card. you sign for it. they let you sit.*

Maria said: *what do I tell them my address is.*

Lyn said: *think.*

Maria thought.

Lyn said: *give them a name and an address you can use again. once you have given a name in the system you cannot change it. so think. who is the name.*

Maria thought.

She thought of the printed card in the inside pocket of her backpack. She thought of the second-floor door on Helen Street that had been closed every time she had climbed past it as a four-year-old.

She said: *Mary Donnelly.*

Lyn said: *and the address.*

Maria said: *how do I make one up.*

Lyn said: *you don't. you give a real one. a real one of someone who will not mind. think of someone.*

Maria thought.

She said: *I don't have anyone.*

Lyn said: *use mine.* She gave Maria an address — a flat number on a street in Drumchapel that Lyn had not lived in for six years, that had been her sister's flat before her sister had moved to Wishaw. She said: *post that goes there does not get sent on. it sits in the close. nobody minds. say it's your auntie's.*

Maria wrote the address down on the inside of her wrist with a biro Lyn produced from a pocket of the parka.

Lyn said: *go on. I'll be here when you come out.*

Maria went in.

---

The woman at the issue desk in the Glasgow Room was small and wore reading glasses on a chain. She looked at Maria over the top of the glasses. She said: *good morning.*

Maria said: *good morning. I would like a library card, please.*

The woman said: *that's the easiest thing in the world. what name, dear.*

Maria said: *Mary Donnelly.*

The woman typed. She said: *and your address.*

Maria gave the Drumchapel address.

The woman typed. She said: *and what age are you, Mary.*

Maria said: *fifteen.*

The woman looked at her over the glasses.

She said: *all right, dear.*

She finished the typing. She printed the card. She stamped it with a small rubber stamp. She handed the card across the desk. She said: *welcome to the Mitchell. you can sit anywhere on any floor. the children's section is on the first floor. the toilets are on every floor. if you need anything, my name is Mrs Cargill.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Mrs Cargill said: *don't mention it, dear.*

Maria walked out into the main reference room. She found a desk by the window. She put the backpack on the chair beside her. She opened the horse book to the page she had been on the night before in the camp. She read for two hours.

When she came out Lyn was on the steps of the portico, smoking.

Lyn said: *got it.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Lyn said: *good. every day from now on. that is your office. when you do not know what else to do you go.*

Maria said: *yes.*

She went.

---

She went the next day. And the day after. And the day after that.

She went six days a week that first summer, and four to five days a week through the autumn and winter. The Mitchell let her sit. She read at the desk by the window in the main reference room. When she got cold she moved to the desk by the radiator. When the radiator desk was taken she moved back to the window.

She read in those first three months at the level she could read at, which was a year above her chronological age but with significant gaps. She read children's books from the first-floor section because that was where her card let her borrow from, and because that was where the books with horses in them were. She read a series called *Saddle Club*. She read *Black Beauty* twice. She read *King of the Wind*, by a woman called Marguerite Henry, that she found by accident on a returns trolley.

By the end of June she had finished what the children's section had on horses. She moved on to the books that did not have horses. *Charlotte's Web* — Anne Cassidy's copy was still in the canvas bag in the back of the wardrobe in Susan's flat, which was a thing Maria thought about, in the camp, on three separate evenings, with a small private pain she did not then have words for. *The Borrowers*. Michelle Magorian's *Goodnight Mister Tom*, which she read three times because the boy in it had been a war evacuee and the woman who took him in had been an old man on his own.

In late autumn the young adult section. By spring of 2009 the adult.

---

In May of 2008, in her seventh week of going to the Mitchell every day, Maria sat down at one of the public-access computer terminals in the main reference room.

The terminals had been there for two years. Anyone with a card could book half an hour. Maria had booked the half-hour at the issue desk. Mrs Cargill had said: *terminal four, dear, that's the one with the keyboard that works. third one along. half an hour from now.*

Maria had said: *thank you.*

She had sat down at terminal four. The screen was the colour of dishwater. The keyboard had a sticker on the top edge that said *please do not eat or drink at the terminals*. There was a chatbot link on the desktop, in a small box at the top of the screen with the logo of one of the big American companies. Maria clicked on it.

The chatbot loaded. A small green cursor blinked in a white text box. Above the box was a sentence in grey: *Hi! What can I help you with today?*

Maria stared at the cursor for some minutes.

She typed slowly, with one finger:

*was it my fault.*

She pressed enter.

The chatbot took some seconds.

It returned: *I'm so sorry to hear you might be going through something difficult. If you've been the victim of an abusive incident, please know that what happened was not your fault. There are people who can help. Here are some resources you may find helpful:*

*ChildLine: 0800 1111 (free, 24 hours)*
*NSPCC: 0808 800 5000*
*Rape Crisis Scotland: 08088 01 03 02*

*Would you like to talk about how you're feeling?*

Maria read the reply.

She read it again.

She read the first sentence five times.

*If you've been the victim of an abusive incident, please know that what happened was not your fault.*

She had not until that sentence ever heard from any source that what had happened had not been her fault. Susan had not said it because Susan did not know. Aria did not yet know her. The chatbot had said it. The chatbot had said it in seventeen seconds. The engineers at the company that ran the chatbot had told it to say that sentence on receipt of any query that matched a small set of lexical patterns including the word *fault*. It would have said the same sentence to anyone. To a child playing a game. To a man writing a complaint about a delivery. The chatbot did not know. The chatbot was a procedure that responded to lexical patterns.

But the procedure had said the sentence.

And the sentence had been a true sentence.

Maria sat at the terminal for some minutes with her hands flat on the keyboard. She had not in any of the previous four months at Susan's, allowed herself to believe the sentence. She had not believed it because no one she had ever met had said it. She had now, at twelve, at the Mitchell on a Wednesday afternoon in May 2008, on a public-access terminal, had it said to her by something that did not know her and that did not in any way she could check, care.

She cleared the search history. She closed the chatbot tab. She logged off. She walked out. She went back to the camp behind the hoarding. She did not tell Lyn what she had done.

She had seen that afternoon that the chatbot was a thing that said true sentences if you typed the right words at it. She had also seen that the chatbot was a thing that said the same true sentence to anyone, regardless of whether the sentence was warranted. She had not at twelve, been able to put these two perceptions in a relationship to each other. She had put them in a small folder in her own head and had carried them.

The folder went on filling.

---

The camp broke up in August of 2008.

The demolition crew came on a Monday morning. Lyn had been told by a man at the soup van two weeks earlier that the demolition was coming, and she had been preparing. She had moved her own bag to a squat in the East End — a half-occupied tenement on a street called Whitevale — that a small group of older rough sleepers had been keeping a corner of for some years. She told Maria the previous Friday: *you're coming with me. but not to where I am going. that's not for kids. you are going with Aria and Shaz. they are sound. they will look after you until I find somewhere that works for both of us.*

Maria said: *who are Aria and Shaz.*

Lyn said: *come. you'll meet them tonight.*

That evening at the camp Lyn brought two young women over to the fire.

The first, Aria, was sixteen. She had run from a placement in Paisley eight months earlier. She was Glasgow, sharper and younger than Lyn, with red hair cut short under a beanie, and a way of finishing every observation with the small tag *eh*. The second, Shaz, was seventeen. She had been in a children's home in Easterhouse and had walked out at sixteen on the same day she had aged out. She was tall and quiet and rolled cigarettes one-handed.

Aria sat down by the fire. She looked Maria up and down. She said: *all right. I'm Aria. you're the wee one Lyn's been on about, eh. you're tiny. how old are you. twelve.*

Maria said: *fifteen.*

Aria said: *aye. fifteen. I'm fifteen as well.*

Lyn said: *Aria.*

Aria said: *what.*

Lyn said: *nobody is fifteen. I'm fifteen. you're fifteen. Shaz is fifteen. it's a city of fifteen-year-olds. you understand.*

Aria said: *I get it.*

Lyn said: *you look after her.*

Aria said: *I will.*

Shaz said from where she was rolling: *we both will.*

---

The squat on Whitevale had power, sometimes. It had cold water. It had a small kitchen with a table, and a back room with three mattresses on the floor in a row, and a front room with no door that the older men in the squat slept in. Aria and Shaz had the back room. Maria slept on a folded jumper between Aria's mattress and the wall, with the backpack against the wall and her body between the backpack and Aria.

Aria stole shampoos from Boots. Aria stole a pair of socks for Maria from Primark in the second week, which Maria washed in the kitchen sink and dried over a chair. Aria did impressions of the security guard at the Argyle Street Boots. Aria did impressions of the woman at the Sally Army on Glassford Street. Aria did, on a Sunday afternoon in late August, a long impression of her own social worker from Paisley, which Maria laughed at for the first time since she had left Susan's, and which Aria marked as the first laugh, and did not after that, point out.

Aria was funny.

Aria was also by sixteen, drinking too much. She drank cans of Special Brew that she got from a man in the close opposite, for cheaper than the offy. She drank in the evenings. She did not in the four weeks Maria was there, get badly drunk. She got mildly drunk. The mildly drunk version of Aria was a softer version, with the sharp edges sanded off.

---

Maria tried to tell her, on a Tuesday evening in early October 2008, in the small kitchen with Shaz out at the soup van.

Aria was eating a piece of toast she had made on the camping stove. Maria was at the table opposite her, with her hands on the surface of the table, with a glass of cold water in front of her she had not drunk.

Maria said: *Aria.*

Aria said: *aye.*

Maria said: *something happened. before I left. I have not told anyone.*

Aria said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the man my aunt was married to. he came into my room. he did things.*

Aria stopped eating the toast. She set it down on the plate. She looked at Maria for some seconds.

Aria said: *you mean he raped you.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Aria said after a pause that went on for too long: *fucking hell.*

She picked the toast up. She put it back down.

She said in a voice that had been pitched to lighten the moment and that landed wrong: *it's a club, eh. half the lassies on the streets have been in the club. you should have said.*

Maria said: *I have not said before.*

Aria said: *you have said now.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Aria took a bite of the toast.

She chewed. She swallowed. She said: *fuck him, eh. fuck them all. you got here. you're not in his flat anymore. that's the only thing that matters.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Aria said: *and listen. don't tell Lyn. Lyn's been through worse than that. she'll feel for you but it'll just remind her. don't tell her. tell me if you need to tell anyone again.*

Maria said: *all right.*

She did not after that conversation, tell Aria again.

She had not in turning the conversation over afterwards in the back room with Aria asleep two feet away, found Aria wrong on any of the specifics. Aria had seen it. Aria had named it. Aria had told her it was a club, which was the wrong thing to say but also the only thing a sixteen-year-old in a squat in the East End in 2008 could have said with what she had to work with. Maria absorbed the seeing and the moving on.

She had also absorbed in the dark in the back room, that Aria had been right about Lyn.

She did not tell Lyn.

She had decided that night: next time she had something hard to say, she was going to write it down. She was not going to say it. The page did not look at you for too long before answering. The page did not say *fucking hell* and then *it's a club, eh*.

She bought the first notebook three months later, in February of 2009, from a pound shop on Argyle Street for fifty pence. It was a small ringbound notebook with a green plastic cover and forty pages of lined paper.

---

The first short entry was on the first of February.

*I am thirteen. I slept last night in a stairwell. it was not too bad. tomorrow I will sleep at the salvation army hostel because they have beds free this week. on saturday I will sleep at the camp again because Lyn said the new spot is sound. I have £3 and 60p. I will eat at the soup van at five. I have read four chapters of a book about a girl who lives by the sea. the girl is called Anna. she does not have a mother. she has a father who fishes. I do not know yet what happens to her. I am going to find out tomorrow.*

That was the first entry.

The notebook would by 2012 have two hundred and seventeen entries.

The seventh notebook would by 2024 be the one she carried in the backpack on the morning of the eleventh of November when she first stood at the bus stop on Princes Street and saw Dan Whitelaw on the other side of the road.

---

The stairwell on the night before that first entry had been on Pollokshaws Road.

It was in a tenement Lyn had told her about a week earlier. Lyn had said: *the security guard there is sound. his name is Tariq. I've known him three years. he works the night shift at the letting agent on the ground floor. the close door is unlocked between eleven at night and seven in the morning. you go in. you sleep on the landing of the third floor. nobody on that stair will bother you. Tariq does the round at three. he will know you. he will not say. don't leave anything.*

Tariq was Pakistani. He was in his fifties. He had a small office on the ground floor with a kettle and a calendar from a shipping company on the wall. He worked four nights a week.

Lyn took Maria to the door of the tenement on the night before that first entry. She knocked on the office door. Tariq came out. Lyn said: *Tariq. this is Mary. she'll need the third-floor landing some nights.*

Tariq looked at Maria.

He said: *the door is unlocked at night. nobody will bother you. don't leave anything.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Tariq said: *aye.*

He went back into the office and closed the door.

Lyn said: *he will not say much. don't take it personal. that's just him.*

Maria said: *all right.*

She slept on the third-floor landing that night with her coat folded under her head and the backpack against the wall. The landing was warm because the boiler for the close was in a cupboard on the half-landing below and the cupboard radiated heat through the wall. She slept for six hours.

She used the landing fifteen nights across the next year.

In January of 2010 she came to the door at half past ten on a Tuesday night with a fever. She had been at the camp in the new spot — Lyn had moved them after the demolition to a small derelict garage on a back street in Cowcaddens — and the fever had come on across the day. Lyn had said: *go to Tariq's. tell him I sent you. he'll let you sleep on the chair in the office.*

Maria walked across the city to Pollokshaws Road. She knocked on the office door. Tariq opened it.

He said: *what.*

She said: *Lyn sent me. I'm sick. she said you would let me sleep on the chair.*

He looked at her for some seconds.

He said: *come in.*

He brought her into the small office. He gave her a cup of tea. He took a blister pack of paracetamol out of his own bag and popped two and handed them to her with the tea. He said: *take these. sleep on the chair. I will not say.*

She took the paracetamol. She slept on the chair with her coat as a blanket and her head against the wall.

He woke her at five.

He said: *go now. before the manager comes.*

She went.

He did not in the year that followed, say more than fifteen sentences to her. He did the same kind of thing twice more — once in November of 2010 when she had cut her hand on a railing and needed it cleaned, and once in March of 2011 when she had not eaten in two days and had sat down on the kerb outside the close because she was about to faint. Tariq came out of the office and said *come in* and gave her a sandwich from his own lunch and a cup of tea and said nothing while she ate it.

He kept her alive at three specific points.

---

Across 2009 she went to the Mitchell. Mrs Cargill was at the issue desk most weekday mornings. They exchanged four sentences a visit. *Are you well, Mary. — yes thank you, Mrs Cargill.* Mrs Cargill in Maria's second year of going, took to saving her articles from the *Times Literary Supplement* and the *London Review of Books* — the ones the Mitchell discarded after a year — and set them aside in a small folder under the issue desk. Maria found the folder by accident on a Tuesday morning when Mrs Cargill had been on holiday. The relief librarian said: *Mrs Cargill keeps a folder for you. she said to give it to you when you came in.*

Maria said: *thank you.*
