The folder had thirty articles in it. Maria read all thirty across the next two weeks. Most she did not understand. Some she understood in fragments. She learned that fortnight, that there were ways of writing about books in which the sentences were as long and as hedged as the things they were trying to describe, and that the hedging was sometimes, the point.

She brought the folder back. She said to Mrs Cargill: *thank you.*

Mrs Cargill said: *did you read them.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Mrs Cargill said: *did you understand them.*

Maria said: *some.*

Mrs Cargill said: *that is plenty. understanding some of the *London Review* at thirteen is a respectable place to be.*

Maria said: *I liked the long sentences.*

Mrs Cargill said: *so did I, when I started.*

She set up another folder.

In March of 2010 Mrs Cargill retired. She told Maria three weeks before. She said on the morning: *I have been here thirty-one years. it is time. they have got me a younger one to take my desk. her name is Joyce. she is fine. she will not save you the magazines. that is not her thing. you will need to ask the cataloguers directly.*

Maria said: *all right.*

On Mrs Cargill's last day she was at the desk at the usual time. Maria came in at half past ten. Mrs Cargill had a small paper bag beside her on the desk. She pushed it across.

She said: *for you. don't say anything. just take it.*

Maria took it.

Inside was a paperback copy of *Far From the Madding Crowd*. The cover was a little yellowed. The spine was creased in two places. On the inside cover Mrs Cargill had written, in blue biro, *for Mary, who reads*.

Maria said: *thank you.*

Mrs Cargill said: *good luck.*

Maria walked over to the desk by the window. She sat down. She read *Far From the Madding Crowd* across the next two weeks. It was, by some margin, the most difficult novel she had read at fourteen. She got through it because she had decided, before she had opened it, that she would.

---

The brothel was on a street off London Road in Bridgeton, on the second floor of a tenement with a black close and a single bulb on the half-landing that had not been changed in two years. The flat was at the end of the landing, second on the right.

Maria did not in November of 2009, know it was a brothel.

She knew Carly Bryce. Carly was fifteen. Carly had come up to the soup van one Thursday evening in September with her hair in a French plait and a Tesco bag with a packet of crisps and a can of Lilt in it. Carly had been at the back of the queue. Maria, by then thirteen and at the camp on Kennedy Street with Lyn, had been at the front. Maria had got her bowl from Andrew and had stood to one side to drink it. Carly had got hers and had come and stood beside her.

Carly had said: *first time at one of these.*

Maria said: *no.*

Carly said: *first time for me. I'm Carly.*

Maria said: *Mary.*

They had drunk the soup. Carly had said her mum's place was full this week — her brother on her mum's couch and Carly herself at her gran's but her gran had a man over and Carly had been told to go for the night. The soup was good. Maria said *aye*. *Want a crisp.* The packet held out. Maria took one.

The next Thursday Carly came back. The Thursday after that. By the end of October they were at the van at the same time most Thursdays.

On a Thursday evening in early November the temperature dropped. Maria had been at the camp in a thin coat. She had been to the Mitchell that afternoon and had been cold the whole walk back. Carly came up to her at the soup van and said: *Mary. you're shivering.*

Maria said: *I'm all right.*

Carly said: *no you're not. where are you sleeping the night.*

Maria said: *the camp.*

Carly said: *no. you're coming with me.*

Maria said: *I'm all right.*

Carly said: *come on. you cannae stay out the night. my mum will not mind. she's got that flat. she's always got a sofa for somebody. come on.*

Maria said: *I don't know.*

Carly said: *Mary. it's three degrees. just for the night. you can go in the morning.*

Maria looked at Lyn. Lyn was at the end of the queue.

Lyn caught her eye. Lyn gave a small nod.

Maria said: *all right.*

Carly said: *good. come on, then. it's a bus to Bridgeton.*

They got the bus.

---

The close was dark and smelled of old chip-paper. The light on the half-landing had a brown patch where damp had got in. Carly went up the stairs in front of Maria, knocking the snow off her boots on the second step. She knocked on the door at the end of the landing.

A woman opened it.

The woman was late forties. She had hair dyed a flat blonde and tied back. She was in jeans and a navy jumper. She had a face that looked at Maria the way Lyn looked at things — taking in, not changing much.

Carly said: *mum. this is Mary. she was at the soup van. it's three degrees out. she's got no place.*

The woman looked at Maria.

She said: *how old.*

Maria said: *fifteen.*

The woman said: *aye. come away in.*

She stepped back from the door.

---

The flat had a hallway with three doors off it, and a kitchen at the end of the hallway, and a small living room to the left of the front door. The hallway carpet was a dark red. There was a print of a stag on the wall. There was a small table by the front door with a phone on it. The phone was an old grey landline phone with a coiled cord. The cord was tangled.

The living room had a brown three-piece suite, a coffee table with a glass top, and a television in the corner. The television was on with the sound down. A Channel Five film was playing.

There were two other women in the living room.

The first was mid-thirties. She was in a dressing gown with her feet up on the sofa. She had a magazine on her lap. She looked up when Carly's mother and Maria came in. She said: *all right, Carly.*

The second was about twenty-two. She was at the small table by the window painting her nails a dark red. She did not look up.

Carly's mother said: *this is Mary. she's staying the night. Mary, this is Maggie and Theresa. Maggie's on the sofa. Theresa's at the table. you'll meet Donna later.*

Maggie said: *all right, hen. plenty of room.*

Theresa lifted her free hand from the nail polish and waved without looking up.

Carly's mother said: *I'm Susan. Carly's mum. that's the kitchen down the hall. there's bread in the breadbin and tea. help yourself.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Susan said: *Carly. shower the lassie. then we'll get her a bowl of something.*

Carly said: *come on.*

Maria followed her down the hall.

---

The bathroom was small and warm. There was a heat-lamp above the mirror. The towels on the rail were white. The shampoo on the bath edge was the kind you got at Boots, in a green bottle.

Carly said: *fresh towel's there. take as long as you want. I'll wait in the kitchen.*

She closed the door.

Maria stood for some seconds with her hand on the rail. The lamp made a small humming sound. The water from the tap when she turned it on was hot at once. There had not been hot water at the camp or the squat. There had not been a heat-lamp at any time in her life. She got into the shower and stood under it for ten minutes and did not move.

When she came out she was wearing the clean t-shirt and the spare jumper Carly had left on the lid of the toilet, with the same jeans. The clothes were Carly's and they were a size too big.

The kitchen had a small Formica table and three chairs. Susan was at the kettle. There was a bowl of soup on the table. The soup was vegetable, the kind that comes from a tin. There was a slice of toast on a plate. Susan had buttered the toast.

Susan said: *sit.*

Maria sat.

Susan said: *eat first. then we'll talk.*

Maria ate. She ate slowly. She ate the toast in three bites. She finished the soup. Susan watched her from the worktop while the kettle boiled. She did not say anything until Maria had finished.

When the kettle clicked off Susan made two cups of tea, one for herself and one for Maria, and sat down across the table. She pushed the second cup over.

She said: *Mary.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *how long have you been on your own.*

Maria said: *a while.*

Susan said: *no social work.*

Maria said: *no.*

Susan said: *no school.*

Maria said: *no.*

Susan said: *aye, that figures. all right. I am going to say one thing. I am not going to ask you any of the questions I want to ask you. you came in with my daughter. you ate at my table. you can sleep on the sofa in the front room tonight and you can sleep on the sofa in the front room tomorrow night if you want and you can sleep on the sofa in the front room for as long as that suits both of us. there is one rule. when the bell goes in the night, you do not come out of the front room. you stay where you are. you put the telly on if you like. you read if you like. you do not come out. you do not open the door of the front room. you understand me.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *good. there are other things you will work out. you do not need to work them out tonight. you understand me.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *all right. one more thing. you are fifteen.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *no you are not. but you are fifteen here. that's how it goes. you understand me.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *good.*

She drank her tea. She got up. She rinsed the cups in the sink.

She said: *bed for you, hen. Maggie will find you a blanket. you sleep all right.*

Maria said: *I will.*

---

The bell went at twelve.

Maria was on the sofa in the front room with the lamp on and a copy of a *Take a Break* magazine Theresa had handed her on the way past. She had been reading an article about a woman in Kirkcaldy who had lost five stone on a slimming plan. She heard the front door open and a man's voice in the hall. She heard Susan's voice in the hall, soft, walking the man past the door of the front room. She heard a bedroom door close down the hall.

She did not move from the sofa.

She turned the lamp off after a while.

She lay on her side on the sofa with the blanket Maggie had given her — a pink fleece with a small piece missing at the corner — pulled up to her shoulder. She looked at the orange light from the streetlight on the close coming through the gap in the curtain. The streetlight was at the angle it was at because of the position of the lamppost outside the window, which she had noted on the way in. She counted the small flowers in the wallpaper pattern beside the sofa for a while. There were thirty-two before the pattern repeated.

She did not sleep.

The bell again at one. At three. The front door close, soft, three times.

At four-thirty Susan came out of one of the bedrooms and walked through to the kitchen. The kettle. Footsteps in the hall, the door of the front room opening a few inches.

Susan said: *you up.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *come and have a tea.*

Maria went through to the kitchen. Susan was at the table in her dressing gown. The tea was on the table. Susan pushed it across.

Susan said: *all right.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *you know.*

Maria said: *I think so.*

Susan said: *good. that means you've got eyes. you stay until you don't want to stay. you do not go back out there. not yet. not at thirteen. not when it's that cold.*

Maria did not say anything to the *thirteen*.

Susan said: *I will not ask. you can tell me when you want to. you can not tell me. you are safe here. nobody who comes through that door will come into the front room. that is the rule and it is enforced. if a man tries the door he will not be back. that has happened twice. it will not happen with you.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Susan said: *don't.*

She drank her tea. She said: *go and sleep now. you'll sleep better with the daylight coming in. I'll be at mine until eleven.*

Maria went back to the sofa. She slept until ten.

---

She stayed at Susan Bryce's for fifteen months.

The flat had four working women in it on a normal week — Susan, Maggie, Theresa, Donna. Donna she met on the second evening. Donna was twenty-six and from Coatbridge. Donna was quiet on the surface and sharp underneath. Donna had a five-year-old son who lived with Donna's mother in Coatbridge. Donna sent her mother money every Thursday afternoon, in cash, in an envelope, with the postman.

Maggie was thirty-five. Maggie had been working out of the flat for six years. Maggie had a way of being in the kitchen in the morning with the kettle on and a piece of toast in her hand that was the way of a woman who had been doing the same thing in the same kitchen for six years.

Theresa was twenty-two. Theresa had a child of her own — a daughter Megan, four — who lived with Theresa's sister in Easterhouse. Theresa saw Megan every Sunday afternoon. Theresa came back from those afternoons quiet and went straight to her room for an hour.

The fifth woman in the flat, sometimes, was Carly. Carly stayed two or three nights a week. Carly did not work in the flat. Carly was Susan's daughter. Carly was at school three days a week and at her gran's on a Saturday and at the flat on the other nights. Carly was the only one of them, by Susan's rule, who did not.

Maria slept on the sofa in the front room every night for fifteen months.

The bell went on average four times a night. On some nights it did not go at all. On a Saturday night it might go six times. The men came in through the front door. They were walked past the front-room door, soft. They were taken to one of the back two bedrooms. They left through the front door, sometimes after twenty minutes, sometimes after an hour. Maria did not see them. Maria did not, in fifteen months, lay eyes on a single one of the men who came through the door.

The other women looked after her in small ways.

Maggie cooked. Maggie cooked twice a day — a breakfast, sometimes for one, sometimes for four; an evening meal, always for all of them at six unless somebody had a booking. Maggie made tattie scones on Sunday mornings. Maggie taught Maria how to peel a potato in one ribbon, which Maria had seen Susan Imrie do once at four and had not been able to do herself. Maggie's hand on Maria's, on the small kitchen knife, was the first hand on hers since the female police officer had picked her up off the kitchen floor at the bedsit on Helen Street. Maggie did not make any thing of it. Maggie taught her the peel and went back to the bacon.

Theresa was the one who took her to the shops. Tuesday was her day off. On a Tuesday afternoon she took Maria into town on the bus from London Road, to Marks and Spencer's on Sauchiehall Street, where she had a small list of things to get for Susan. A pair of socks or a pack of pants for Maria, or, on a Tuesday in February of 2010, a winter jacket from the BHS sale rail for £18. Theresa paid for the jacket out of her own money. She told Maria, in the bus on the way back: *Susan would have got it for you. I just got it first.*

Donna gave Maria the second-hand laptop she had stopped using. The laptop was a Dell that had a sticker on the front from Donna's son's school. Donna had a newer one. The Dell had a wifi connection to the BT line in the flat. Maria, on the laptop in the front room in the early afternoons when the flat was quiet, taught herself the BBC News website and the Wikipedia search box and the way to find a book in the Mitchell catalogue from the laptop. She read on the laptop in the afternoons, before the bell started at seven or eight in the evening, and she switched the laptop off and put it under the sofa cushion before the first booking of the night.

Susan ran the rota. She answered the phone in the hallway. The small notebook by the phone, in pencil, with the bookings written in. She was at the door for every arrival. The cash in a tin under her own bed, and on a Friday morning to the post office with the takings of the week, to put what could be put through the bank in.

In the fifteen months Maria was there Susan did not, once, ask Maria where she had come from.

She also did not, in the fifteen months Maria was there, take a penny from anyone for Maria's keep. Maria asked her once, in the kitchen on a Wednesday afternoon at the end of February 2010, about three months in.

Maria said: *Susan.*

Susan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *I should be paying you something. for the food. for the room.*

Susan said: *no.*

Maria said: *I should be.*

Susan said: *Mary. listen. the food in this flat is not the cost of the flat. the cost of the flat is the four bedrooms and the front room nobody comes into. you in the front room is fine. you in the front room is not a cost. the food you eat is what the bin would otherwise take. you do not pay me. that is the rule. you can stay until you want to go.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Susan said: *one more thing.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Susan said: *you keep going to the library. you read the papers. you read what they are saying. you read what the kind of people who come up these stairs say about the kind of people who answer the door. you are going to need to know what they say about us. you do not need to know it tonight. you will need to know it. that's the work.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Susan said: *good. peel that potato.*

She handed her the small knife.

---

The flat was not what Maria had thought it was for the first three days.

She had thought, on the first morning, that Susan and her three friends shared the flat for the rent. She had heard the bell at twelve and one and three the first night and had assumed the bell was for one of them at a time, and that each of them had a boyfriend who came over at unsociable hours, and that none of the boyfriends came into the front room out of politeness. She had thought this on the first morning because the alternative had not yet occurred to her. The alternative was a thing she had read about in a *Daily Record* article at the library at twelve, but had not put together with a flat in Bridgeton at thirteen.

She put it together on the third night.

She had been on the sofa with the lamp on. The bell had gone at midnight. The door opened. Susan's voice in the hall, light. The man's voice — *which one.* Susan's voice — *Theresa's free.*

Maria had heard the words.

She sat with the magazine in her lap for some minutes without seeing the magazine.

She had at some point in the next ten minutes, worked out that the words she had heard were the words she had heard. The four women in the flat were not housemates. The bell at twelve and one and three was the same bell. The three men were three different men. The men were not boyfriends. The flat was the kind of flat she had read about in the *Daily Record* article at the library at twelve.

She had also worked out, in the same ten minutes, that the flat was the safest place she had slept since the cot in the bedsit on Helen Street.

She did not understand at thirteen, how those two things could be true at the same time. No framework for it. She had only what Susan had said to her in the kitchen on the first morning: *you stay until you don't want to stay. you do not go back out there.*

She decided to stay.

The next morning she said nothing to Susan about having worked it out. Susan did not ask. The not-saying was on both sides, the right way to play it.

She stayed.

---

The fifteen months at Susan Bryce's were the months in which Maria slept.

She slept on the sofa six to nine hours a night. Through the bell. Through the door opening and closing. She slept because the body that had been on the alert for fourteen months knew, by the end of the first week at Susan's, that the door of the front room was not going to open at three in the morning. The body could let down. It had not been able to at Susan Dunlop's because of Alan. Not at the camp or the squat because of the men in the close who would step on you if they wanted to. Not at Tariq's because Tariq's was an office on a Tuesday night and not a sofa with a pink fleece blanket.

The body let down at Susan Bryce's.

That was what kept Maria alive across the next two years.

She also, in the fifteen months, started to talk.

She talked to Maggie in the kitchen. To Theresa on the bus to town on Tuesday afternoons. To Donna about the laptop and the Wikipedia search box and what Donna's son liked at school. With Carly it was books and television and a boy at Carly's school called Connor who Carly fancied. Susan in the kitchen on Wednesday afternoons when the flat was quiet and Susan was doing the accounts.

What she did not talk about: Alan Dunlop. Helen Street. The canvas bag in the back of the wardrobe at Susan Dunlop's. Karen Buchanan, the letter she had thrown in the bin at Ms Kerr's. Everything else was talked about.

The talking she did at Susan Bryce's was the most talking she had done in her life.

The reading she did at Susan Bryce's was on the laptop in the afternoons and at the Mitchell on the days she went into town. She went into town three to four days a week. She came back on the bus to Bridgeton at five, in time for Maggie's evening meal. She ate at the table with the four women and Carly on the nights Carly was there. She put the laptop away by seven. She got on the sofa with the lamp on. The bell went at eight or nine. She turned the lamp off at eleven. She slept.

---

She left Susan Bryce's on a morning in early March of 2011.

The leaving was not Maria's choice. Donna had been arrested on a Tuesday night the previous week. The arrest had been at the door — two men in plain clothes had come up the stairs at nine in the evening and had asked for Donna by name. Donna went to the door. They said they were from the police. Susan, who had been in the kitchen, came down the hall fast and stood between Donna and the door. The men told her Donna had been seen in a flat on Cumbernauld Road the previous month and had been recognised on a CCTV they had pulled. Susan said *Donna lives here. you can speak to her here.* The men said they needed to take Donna to the station. They took her.

Two nights later Donna came back. No charges. The CCTV had been from a different flat. The men had been on a fishing trip.

But they had seen the inside of the hall of the flat. The front door, the table with the phone, the stag print on the wall, the closed door of the front room.

Susan came into the front room on the Friday morning after Donna had been brought back. She was at the door of the front room with two cups of tea. She handed one to Maria.

She sat down on the chair beside the sofa.

She said: *Mary.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *the two men on Tuesday night looked at the door of this room.*

Maria said: *I know.*

Susan said: *one of them was a sergeant called Boyle. I know Boyle. Boyle is fine. Boyle was on a CCTV chase. Boyle did not look at the door of this room the way Boyle would have looked at it if he had been told to. but Boyle had been at the door. Boyle had seen you go to the toilet at the end of the hall on the way in. Boyle saw a girl who looked thirteen. Boyle's a father. Boyle is fine. but Boyle is also a man on a job and Boyle's job is what Boyle's job is. you understand me.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *if Boyle is asked, Boyle will tell. and somebody is going to ask Boyle.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *I am not putting you out of the door tonight. but I am telling you that you are going to need somewhere else, and the somewhere else is not going to be in this flat. you understand me.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *Lyn.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *I'll ring her this afternoon. you go and pack your things.*

Maria said: *all right.*

She packed her things. The things were what they had been when she had come in: the horse book, the four books from Ms Kerr, the two notebooks (now full, third one started), the printed card from Mrs Donnelly in the inside pocket. She added: the *Far From the Madding Crowd* paperback Mrs Cargill had given her in March of 2010, and a small zip-up wallet with £42 in it that Theresa had been giving her £3 a week from her own money across the fifteen months in case Maria ever needed to go.

She said goodbye in the kitchen on Sunday evening. Maggie hugged her at the kitchen door. Theresa gave her a fresh tin of polo mints out of the cupboard for the bus. Donna said *take care, hen*. Carly cried.

Susan walked her to the close door.

Susan said at the door: *Mary.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *you do not write to me. you do not come back. you walk out this door now and the door of this flat is closed to you. it is closed because if you come back the day will come when they come for me and you with me. you understand me.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Susan said: *I am sorry. it is not what I want.*

Maria said: *I know.*

Susan said: *if you have a need, you ring Lyn. you do not ring me. Lyn knows where to send you. all right.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Susan said: *go.*

Maria went.

---

She walked back to the camp at Kennedy Street. Lyn was at the fire. Lyn stood up when she came through the gap in the hoarding.

Lyn said: *Mary.*

Maria said: *Lyn.*

Lyn said: *Susan rang me.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Lyn said: *come and sit.*

Maria sat.

Lyn said: *you all right.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Lyn said: *good. we'll get you a tea. then we'll work it out.*

Maria said: *yes.*

She got the tea.

---

She slept that night at the camp, on the folded blanket she had slept on the night Lyn had first brought her in. The blanket was the same blanket. Eddie was still keeping the fire going. Eddie said *welcome back, hen* and did not say anything else. The camp was different from the one she had left in November 2009. The faces around the fire were partly different. Two of the men she had known were dead. One of them, Tommy, had died of exposure in a stairwell in Govan two months earlier. Maria heard about it from a woman called Pat that first evening.

Pat said: *Tommy went in January. cold one.*

Maria said: *I had not known.*

Pat said: *aye. you'd been away.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Pat said: *you back for good.*

Maria said: *I don't know yet.*

Pat said: *aye. take a day.*

She took a day. She took a week. She took the rest of March.

By the start of April she had been back at the camp three weeks and had slept on the folded blanket every night and had stopped, every morning, expecting the smell of Maggie's bacon at the kitchen end of the hall. The soup van at five again. The Mitchell three days a week. The third-floor landing on Pollokshaws Road on the nights Lyn said go.

The fifteen months had been a thing that was over.

She would, in the weeks after, learn the way the body learns what it has lost when what it has lost has been the only thing that had let it sleep.

---

Lyn was found in the doorway of a closed shop on Trongate at half past four in the morning on the eighteenth of April 2011.

Maria heard about it from Eddie that evening at the soup van.

She had come up to the van for her bowl of lentil soup, with the bowl in her hand, and Eddie was at the back of the queue, and Eddie's face was the face of a man who had been waiting to tell her something he had not wanted to tell her.

He said: *Mary. come here.*

She came over.

He said: *it's Lyn. she's gone.*

Maria said: *what do you mean.*

He said: *she's dead. last night. they found her in a doorway on Trongate. it was the gear. the bad gear that's been going round. you'd heard.*

Maria said: *I'd heard.*

He said: *I'm sorry, hen.*

Maria stood with the bowl of soup in her hand. The soup was too hot to drink. The smell of the lentils rose in front of her. The bowl was warm against her palms.

She said: *where is she now.*

Eddie said: *the morgue. they'll take her tomorrow. council burial. you cannae go, hen. you go to the council and they will know where to find you.*

Maria said: *I know.*

Eddie said: *sit down. you've gone white.*

Maria sat down on the kerb beside the soup van. The bowl was still warm. She did not in that first ten minutes drink any of it. Eddie sat down beside her. Andrew, the man at the van, brought over two paper cups of tea and put them on the pavement beside them and went back to the van. The queue was shorter that evening because Lyn had not been in it.

Maria said after some time: *Eddie.*

Eddie said: *aye.*

She said: *she said she would teach me how to ride a bus from the Carlisle station. she had a sister in Carlisle once. she said we'd go one day. just go and come back.*

Eddie said: *aye. she'd told me.*

Maria said: *we never went.*

Eddie said: *no.*

Maria drank some of the soup. She drank it because Lyn would have said *drink the fucking soup, I'm gone, you cannae help me by not drinking the soup.*

She drank it.

She sat on the kerb with Eddie for an hour and a half. They did not for most of the hour, say anything. Eddie smoked three cigarettes. Maria did not cry. She had not cried since the assault at twelve. Crying had closed in her then and had not opened back up. Lyn dying did not on its own, reach in past the closing.

She walked back, after the hour and a half, to the spot in the camp she had been sleeping in that week — a corner under a tarpaulin in a yard behind a builder's merchant on Kennedy Street.

She sat on the folded blanket. She got the horse book out. She read aloud, in her own voice, in the dark, until the morning, the story about the girl who lived in a wood at the edge of a great kingdom. The girl in the story had a horse who was her friend. The horse never died on any of the pages of the book.

Maria read the story aloud all night.

---

The council buried Lyn at Riddrie Park in a public grave that did not have a marker.

In 2018, at twenty-two, Maria went to the cemetery on a Tuesday afternoon. She walked through the public-grave section. She could not find which one was Lyn's. After forty minutes of walking up and down the paths she sat down on a bench and read aloud, in her own voice, the story about the girl who lived in a wood at the edge of a great kingdom, in case Lyn was somewhere where she could hear it.

It was the only thing she had to give to a person who had not, in life, asked anything of her.

---

Eddie died in March of 2012 of a heart condition that had not been treated because Eddie had not had a GP. He had been fifty-eight. He had collapsed on the pavement on Argyle Street on a Tuesday afternoon. The ambulance had got there in eleven minutes. He had been pronounced dead at the Royal at three. Maria heard about it the next day from a man called Martin who had been in the camp since the autumn, in the queue at the soup van. Martin said: *Eddie's gone, Mary.* Maria said: *I know who you mean.* Martin said: *this morning the news came.* Maria said: *thank you, Martin.*

A woman called Pat, who had been at the camp for two years and who had given Maria a small pair of woollen gloves the previous winter, was found in the river in November of 2011 and was on the post-mortem, ruled a probable suicide.

There were others.

By the summer of 2012 the small community Maria had been moving through for four years had taken eight deaths.

---

By the autumn of 2011 Maria had begun on her own, to teach.

There were always new arrivals. A thirteen-year-old girl from Easterhouse came into the camp on a wet Thursday evening in October, with a black eye and a small carrier bag of clothes and a damp envelope of papers. The girl stood at the gap in the hoarding for some minutes before stepping through, watching the fire, watching the bodies around it, watching to see whether the bodies around it were watching her back. Maria had been at the fire with a man called Davie. Maria stood up. Maria walked over to the gap.

She said: *come on in. it's all right. who are you.*

The girl said: *Lauren.*

Maria said: *come and sit by the fire, Lauren. I'm Mary. how old are you.*

Lauren said: *thirteen.*

Maria said: *aye. fifteen here. come.*

Lauren came.

Maria gave her in the first hour, what Lyn had given her in 2008.

She said: *don't queue at the Sally Army on Glassford Street. queue at the volunteer one on Trongate. they don't ask. they just feed you. the man who runs it is called Andrew. he comes Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday.*

Lauren said: *Trongate.*

Maria said: *aye. if you have to sleep alone, sleep with your back against a wall, never against an open space. and never sleep with your bag away from your body. the bag goes between you and the wall. always. and never with anyone who has been drinking. the man with the long coat that Davie was talking to over there earlier, you do not talk to him. he is not sound.*

Lauren said: *aye, all right.*

Maria said: *go to the library every day. the Mitchell on North Street. you can sit anywhere on any floor. when you do not know what else to do you go there. ask for a card. the woman on the desk now is called Joyce. she is fine. say your name is whatever you want it to be. tell her your address is —* she gave Lyn's old address in Drumchapel — *— that's mine as well. it works. they do not check.*

Lauren said: *the Mitchell.*

Maria said: *aye. one more thing. you read.*

Lauren said: *a bit.*

Maria said: *good. they'll let you read in the children's section even if you've got an adult card, if you ask. find a book with a horse in it.*

Lauren said: *aye, all right. ta.*

Maria said: *you'll be all right.*

Lauren said: *aye.*

Maria walked her over to a folded blanket. She gave her her own coat for the night. She took her water bottle off the bag and gave it to her.

She said: *you sleep there. I'll be at the fire.*

Lauren said: *thanks.*

Maria said: *don't.*

She sat at the fire that night with Davie. Davie had been a man around forty who had been on the streets four years. He had been quiet. He said to her after some hours: *you sounded like Lyn just then, hen.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Davie said: *she would have been pleased.*

Maria said: *I hope so.*

---

She left Glasgow in July of 2012, when she was sixteen, after a man at the camp she had been moving through that summer began to look at her in the way Alan Dunlop had looked at her at twelve, and she knew, without anyone having to tell her, that the only thing she could do was go.

The man's name was Brian. He came into the camp in May. He was thirty-five. He was by his own account a former roadie. He was not all right. Maria marked him on the third day. She waited two weeks to see if she had got it wrong. She had not. It got worse. He began by the end of June, to find reasons to be near her. He twice sat down next to her at the fire when there were other places to sit. He once on a Tuesday afternoon, walked behind her down Buchanan Street, far enough back that she was not certain it was him, and close enough that when she stopped to look in a shop window he stopped too.

She did not tell anyone.

Lyn was dead. Eddie was dead. Davie was kind but Davie was by 2012, drinking too much himself. Maria thought it through alone. She decided.

She told Tariq, on the night of the third of July, in the small office on Pollokshaws Road, with the kettle on. She said: *Tariq. I am going to leave Glasgow.*

Tariq said: *where to.*

She said: *Edinburgh.*

He said: *all right.*

She said: *I wanted to tell you.*

He said: *good. when.*

She said: *Tuesday morning.*

He said: *do you have money for the bus.*

She said: *eighteen pounds.*

He said: *take this.*

He reached into the inside pocket of his jacket and took out a twenty-pound note. He handed it to her across the desk.

She said: *I cannot.*

He said: *take it.*

She said: *Tariq. I cannot.*

He said: *Mary. take it. send it back if you want to. when you have it. to this address.* He wrote an address on a small piece of paper. It was the address of the letting agent on Pollokshaws Road.

Maria said: *I will send it back.*

Tariq said: *send it back when you can. not before.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Tariq said: *do not get on the wrong bus.*

Maria said: *I will not.*

Tariq said: *the seven-fifteen Citylink to Edinburgh from Buchanan bus station. platform eight. it goes every hour. take the early one. you'll be at Waverley before ten.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Tariq said: *go well.*

Maria said: *thank you, Tariq.*

Three years later, in 2015, when she had been at the Counter for a year and was able for the first time to put money aside, she sent the twenty pounds back. She sent it in cash inside a card that said *thank you for everything. Mary.* She did not sign her real name. She did not put a return address.

---

She got on the seven-fifteen Citylink to Edinburgh on the morning of Tuesday the tenth of July 2012.

She had said goodbye the night before to Davie at the camp, and to Andrew at the soup van on the Saturday before that. Davie said: *take care of yourself, hen.* Andrew said: *if Edinburgh disnae work out, come back. soup is here.*

She thanked them.

She took nothing extra. She had in the backpack: the horse book, the four books from Ms Kerr, the third notebook with the first two folded in the bottom under everything, Mrs Donnelly's printed card in the inside pocket, a change of clothes, a toothbrush, a water bottle, the *Far From the Madding Crowd* paperback. She had £38 in pound coins and notes. £20 of it was Tariq's. The rest was what was left of the £42 Theresa had given her in three-pound instalments across the fifteen months at Susan Bryce's.

The bus station was warm in the early-morning July light. There was a woman selling rolls and bacon at a stand by platform six. Maria did not buy one. She had a tin foil parcel in the side pocket of the backpack with a slice of bread and butter and a hard-boiled egg in it that she had made the night before in the kitchen of the squat on the camping stove.

Platform eight. The seven-fifteen Citylink. Twenty seats taken. Maria took a window seat halfway down on the left. She put the backpack on the seat beside her with the strap over her arm. The bus pulled out at ten past seven. She watched through the window as Glasgow went by. She watched the M8 west, the slow climb up to Harthill, the descent into Lanarkshire, the cooling towers at Westfield, the long flat run east through Bathgate. She watched the eastern half of central Scotland she had not in her life seen.

She arrived at Waverley at twenty past nine.

She got off the bus on platform two. She walked up the long slope of the platform and through the ticket hall and out the side exit onto Waverley Bridge.

She looked up.

The castle was on the rock to the west. The Old Town was above her, on the bridge. Princes Street was to the north, with the gardens running down below it, with the Walter Scott monument black against the sky.

She had never in her life, seen a city like this.

She thought, standing there with the backpack on her shoulder and the morning light coming down through the bridge:

*this is a different city. I am sixteen. I am going to live here for the rest of my life.*

She was, in the thinking, correct.

She turned left.

She went looking for the Cowgate.

---


### Chapter Four: Easter Road

*Edinburgh, 2012–2014*

The Cowgate Centre was three streets south of the Royal Mile, in the basement of a stone building with a wooden door painted dark green. The door had a small brass sign on it that said *Salvation Army — Women's Hostel — Ring Bell*. Maria rang the bell at twenty past ten on the morning of Tuesday the tenth of July 2012, with the backpack on her shoulder and her hair stuck to the side of her face from the heat coming off the road. She had walked from Waverley. She had stopped twice on the way — once on Cockburn Street to drink water from the bottle, and once on the Royal Mile to stand for a few seconds outside a shop window with the morning light coming down through the buildings the way it had not, in any city she had been in before, come down.

The man who opened the door was in his fifties, in a grey jumper and corduroys, with a small white moustache and a build that had been a runner's some decades back. He looked at her for a second.

He said: *come in, hen.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

She stepped down into the basement. The corridor smelled of bleach and toast.

The man said: *I'm Donald. you'll be wanting a bed.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Donald said: *come and sit at the desk. I'll do the form.*

He took her round to a small office at the end of the corridor. The office had a desk, a filing cabinet, a kettle, and a window onto the back court. He sat on the swivel chair behind the desk and opened a drawer and pulled out a printed form and a biro.

He said: *what's your name.*

Maria said: *Mary Donnelly.*

Donald wrote it down.

He said: *date of birth.*

Maria said: *eleventh of July nineteen ninety-four.*

She had practised the date for two months. She had aged herself by a year and a half — she was sixteen years and seven months, and the date she had given made her eighteen the day before yesterday. Eighteen was the threshold. Below eighteen the hostel was required to refer to social work. At eighteen she was an adult and they could, on the duty worker's discretion, give her a bed.

Donald wrote the date down.

He said without looking up: *long way from your birthday.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Donald said: *previous address.*

Maria said: *Glasgow.*

Donald said: *I need a bit more than that, hen.*

Maria gave Lyn's old Drumchapel address. Donald wrote it down. He looked up.

He said: *Mary. I'll put down what you've told me. I have not believed the date of birth. I'll put it down anyway because you have given it to me straight and that is what the form needs. you follow.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Donald said: *I will not call anyone. that is not what we do here. I am telling you so you know what I am doing. you are eighteen on the form. that is what I will tell anyone who asks. that's the deal.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Donald said: *no thanks needed. it's the form.*

He finished the form. He put it in the filing cabinet. He stood up.

He said: *bed seventeen. dinner's at six. towels are in the cupboard at the end of the corridor.*

He left her to find the dormitory.

---

The dormitory was a long room with thirty beds in two rows along the walls. Each bed had a thin metal frame and a flat blue mattress and a folded grey blanket. Bed seventeen was on the left-hand row, in the middle. The bed beside it on one side was empty. The bed beside it on the other side had a woman asleep on it, on her front, with her face turned to the wall and a coat pulled up over her shoulder.

Maria put the backpack on the floor at the foot of bed seventeen. She sat on the edge of the bed. The mattress took her weight in a way that told her the springs were old. The room was quiet. She could hear water running through pipes in the wall above her.

She sat there for some minutes.

She got up. She took the backpack. She walked down the corridor to the cupboard. She took out a towel. She walked across to the shower room. She locked the cubicle door. She undressed.

The shower was a metal head on a flexible hose, set in tile. The water came on warm. She stepped under it.

She had not been properly washed in three days.

She had, since leaving Susan's, washed her hair when she could, in sinks at the Mitchell or in the kitchen at the squat on Whitevale, or on the better weeks at the Salvation Army hostel on Glassford Street when there were beds free. She had washed her body in the same places. Her skin had a layer of grime on it that was, by 2012, the texture of her own skin to her — the way her skin felt when she touched it was the way her skin had felt for four years. The shower at the Cowgate took the layer off in five minutes.

She stood under the water with her face turned up to it.

She started crying.

She had not cried at Lyn. Not at Eddie. Not at Mrs Cargill leaving. Not, on any night she could remember in those four years, in any way that would have shown to another person. She had cried intermittently in the dark in the Argyle Arcade in April 2008, and she had cried in the alley before Lyn came, and that had been the last time her body had let crying happen at length.

The shower at the Cowgate let it.

She stood under the water for thirty-five minutes. She cried the whole time. She cried with the shower head running so the sound did not carry into the corridor. Her face was against the wet tile of the wall.

She cried for her mother, although she did not in the moment name it as for her mother. For Lyn. For Mrs Cargill leaving. Anne Cassidy. Mrs Donnelly with the printed card. Ms Kerr. Mrs Allan in the garden with the small dead things. The canvas bag in the back of the wardrobe in Susan Dunlop's flat. Eddie. Pat. Tommy who had died in the Govan stairwell on the cold night.

She cried for Susan Bryce at the kitchen table at four-thirty in the morning with the tea between them.

For Maggie at the worktop with her hand over Maria's on the small kitchen knife.

Theresa on the bus to town on a Tuesday. Donna at the door of the front room with the laptop. The thirteen-year-old Lauren whose surname Maria did not know how to find out the next year. The four-year-old on the kitchen floor who had not, since the kitchen floor, been able to cry properly because she had been moved before she could.

She cried like that for thirty-five minutes.

She washed her hair. Her body. Her face. She turned the water off.

She wrapped herself in the towel. She walked back to the dormitory. She put on clean clothes from the backpack. She lay down on bed seventeen.

She slept for nine hours.

She slept through dinner. She slept until half past nine in the evening.

She woke because Donald was standing at the foot of the bed with a small paper plate in his hand. There was a sandwich on the plate. He set the plate on the floor at the foot of the bed.

He said: *tea in the kitchen if you want it. corned beef on white. you slept through dinner.*

He went back out.

Maria sat up. She picked up the plate. She ate the sandwich. It was the standard sandwich the kitchen made when a resident slept through dinner. Donald had cut it in half. He had not said it was for her in particular. He had set it on the floor and gone. The doing-and-going was the shape of how the hostel worked. Maria registered the shape. She ate slowly.

She lay back down at half past ten and slept through to seven in the morning.

---

She stayed at the Cowgate Centre for eleven weeks.

The hostel had a maximum stay of ninety days, after which residents were expected to have moved on into longer-term accommodation. Maria used the ninety days. She used them to do three things: find work, find a place to stay, and, in quiet practical ways, become Mary Donnelly in the records of any institution she could persuade to record her under that name.

The work she found was at a coffee shop on Leith Walk called the Counter.

---

The Counter had one wide window onto Leith Walk, a counter at the back, six tables in between, a pastry case at the front, and a board with the day's specials written in chalk in a slanting handwriting. It was four streets south of the bottom of Leith Walk on the right-hand side as you walked from the city centre. It had been there for six years. The handwriting on the board was the handwriting of the woman who owned it.

Maria walked in on a Wednesday morning at half past ten. She had been at the Cowgate two and a half weeks. She had washed her clothes the previous afternoon at the launderette on Leith Walk, paying with two pound coins from a small canvas pouch she now kept her money in. She had brushed her hair flat. She had looked at her own face in the bathroom mirror at the hostel and had thought: *that will do.*

There was a card in the window of the Counter. The card said *staff wanted full or part-time, apply within*. The card was in the same slanting handwriting as the board.

The bell above the door rang when she pushed it open.

The woman behind the counter was in her mid-forties, in jeans and a black t-shirt and a black apron. She had brown hair tied back. She had reading glasses pushed up on her head. She was halfway through writing something on a clipboard. She looked up.

She said: *yes?*

Maria said: *I saw your card in the window. I'm looking for work.*

The woman set the clipboard down.

She said: *what is your name.*

Maria said: *Mary Donnelly.*

The woman said: *I'm Fiona. Fiona Pratt. I own the shop. how old are you, Mary.*

Maria said: *eighteen.*

Fiona looked at her for two seconds.

She said: *all right. eighteen on whatever you've given the hostel.*

Maria did not answer.

Fiona said: *I'm not going to argue. have you worked in a café before.*

Maria said: *no.*

Fiona said: *can you make tea.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Fiona said: *can you be here at seven tomorrow morning.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Fiona said: *all right. start tomorrow. the trial is two weeks. if it works it works. if it doesn't I'll tell you and you'll go.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Fiona said: *no thanks. show up.*

Maria said: *I will.*

Fiona had already picked up the clipboard.

---

Maria was there at seven the next morning.

Fiona was already there. The lights were on. The till was open. The chalkboard was being rewritten for the day. Fiona handed her the apron over the counter. The apron was a black canvas one with a pocket in the front. Fiona said: *you'll learn the till in a week. for now you wipe tables, restock the case, and when somebody orders you take the order to me. you watch how I make a flat white. by Friday you will be making them. don't put the steamer wand into the milk before the milk is in the jug. that's the only thing not to do. everything else we'll fix.*

Maria said: *all right.*

The first customer came in at twelve past seven.

He was a man in his thirties, in a navy suit, who walked in with a small rolling case and a folded *Scotsman* under his arm. He came up to the counter. He said: *morning, Fiona.*

Fiona said: *morning, Charlie. flat white?*

Charlie said: *aye.*

Fiona said: *Charlie, this is Mary. Mary, Charlie. comes in every weekday at twelve past seven for four years.*

Charlie said: *near enough.*

Fiona said: *Charlie's in property. flat in Trinity. half-seven meeting on Leith Walk Wednesdays. you'll see him.*

Maria said: *good morning.*

Charlie paid. Fiona made the flat white. Maria took the cup over to the small table by the window where Charlie was reading the *Scotsman*. She set it down. Charlie said: *thank you, Mary.* Maria said: *you are welcome.*

She walked back behind the counter.

Fiona said quietly, with her back to the espresso machine: *good. that's the bit a lot of them get wrong in the first week.*

Maria said: *what bit.*

Fiona said: *the bit where you say *you are welcome* and not nothing.*

Maria said: *I learnt that at primary school.*

Fiona said: *you'd be surprised.*

---

She worked the early shift Tuesday to Saturday — opening at seven, the morning rush from half past seven to half past nine, the quieter mid-morning until eleven, then handing over to whoever Fiona had on for lunch.

Across the first fortnight she learnt the regulars.

Charlie at twelve past seven on weekdays.

A woman in her sixties called Rita came in at twenty to eight on a Tuesday and Thursday for a pot of tea and a piece of fruit toast. Rita had been the sub-postmistress in Pilrig for twenty-two years until the post office had been closed in 2007. She brought her own newspaper.

A pair of nurses from the Royal came in at seven on a Friday at the end of their night shift. Two flat whites, one chocolate croissant between them, paid by the older one. They sat at the table furthest from the door.

A man called George, in his seventies, came in at half past eight every weekday and had a black coffee at the small table at the back. He read a different book every week. He said *good morning, dear* to Maria the first time he saw her and said it every day after.

Maria took the orders. Wiped the tables. Carried cups to the people. Took the money, gave the change. By the first week she could make a flat white that Fiona, on tasting, would say *aye, that's it*. By the second she was on the till. By the third she was the one closing up at eleven before the lunch person came.

On the Saturday at the end of the third week, Fiona said after she had locked up the till at eleven: *Mary. sit down.*

Maria sat at the small table by the window.

Fiona made two cups of tea. She brought them over. She sat down opposite. She had a folder on her lap. She put it on the table.

She said: *paperwork.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Fiona said: *you haven't given me a National Insurance number. that's fine for the first month because we're inside the trial. by the end of next month I need to put you on the books or I cannae keep paying you. tell me what's going on.*

Maria said: *I do not have one.*

Fiona said: *why.*

Maria said: *I am sixteen. I am from Glasgow. I left in April of 2008. I have been on the streets. I came up here to start over. the hostel wrote down a name that is not the name on my birth certificate. I do not know how to get a National Insurance number without the system finding me.*

Fiona said: *what is on your birth certificate.*

Maria said: *Maria Imrie. Imrie was my mother's name. she's dead.*

Fiona said: *right.*

Maria said: *I am also not eighteen.*

Fiona said: *I know.*

Maria said: *thank you for —*

Fiona said: *Mary, listen. I'm not interested in the speech. I'm interested in whether you can keep showing up at seven in the morning and making the coffee. you can. that's all I'm running. I'll pay you cash until you turn eighteen for real. the council will not look at a casual on cash. when you're eighteen we'll sort the paperwork properly. you can stay on. that's the deal.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Fiona said: *don't thank me. just keep doing the job. you're good at it. that's all I need.*

She picked up her tea. She drank it. She put the cup down.

She said: *one more thing.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Fiona said: *I do not need to know the rest of it. do not tell me. if you tell me, the next time the council comes round about something, I have heard it. I do not want to have heard it. you and I are running a coffee shop. that's the shape of this.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Fiona said: *good. go home. Tuesday.*

Maria said: *Tuesday.*

She walked out. The bell above the door rang behind her.

She walked back to the Cowgate. She walked through Princes Street Gardens and across the Mound and down the Cowgate. She got to the hostel at twenty to twelve. She lay down on bed seventeen.

She thought lying on the bed: *what was agreed is what was agreed. she does not want it to be more than that.*

She thought: *that is what I wanted too.*

She thought: *I will hold up my end.*

She did. The arrangement Fiona made on a Saturday morning at the small table by the window of the Counter in late July of 2012 held for thirteen years. Maria was still working the early shift on the morning of the eleventh of November 2024 when she stood at the bus stop on Princes Street and saw Dan Whitelaw on the other side of the road. Fiona was still the owner. The board with the day's specials was still in Fiona's slanting handwriting. The arrangement, across those thirteen years, did not in any direction need renegotiating. It continued. The continuation was the steadiest economic fact of Maria's adult life.

Fiona did not, in any of those years, ask about the rest of it. Not in 2013 when Maria began the Tuesday work. Not in 2016, when the evening shifts at the City Chambers started. Not in 2019 when Mrs Grewal died and Maria did not come in for three days. Not in 2024 when Maria came in on the morning after the bus stop with her shoulders set differently. Fiona watched. Fiona did not ask.

The not-asking was, on Fiona's part, the running of a coffee shop on Fiona's terms. It read on Maria, as the years went on, as the cleanest professional relationship of her adult life.

---

The card on the noticeboard at the Cowgate said *single room, third floor, working tenants only, £180 per month including bills, references not required if hostel-vouched*, and underneath, a phone number and a name: *Mrs Grewal*.

Maria took the card down to Donald at the front desk. Donald said: *Mrs Grewal's been doing rooms out of that building twelve years. she takes a few from us a year. you'll be all right.*

Mrs Grewal was free.

Maria took the bus to Easter Road at three. She got off at the stop opposite the Hibs ground. The street was wide. The tenements ran in a long sandstone row up the eastern side. The flat was on the third floor of one of the tenements, with a black iron close door that had an entry panel beside it. Maria pressed the buzzer for *G — Grewal*. A voice came through the grille. The voice said: *come up, hen. third floor on the right.*

The voice was soft. It was not Glasgow. It was not Edinburgh. It had a lilt at the end of *third floor* Maria had not heard before.

She climbed the stairs.

Mrs Grewal was at the door of the flat opposite the rented one. She was a slight woman of sixty-six, in a long cardigan over a dark blue salwar kameez, with grey hair pulled back into a low bun. She had a gold stud in her left nostril. She had reading glasses on a chain.

She said: *Mary, is it? come and see the room.*

She took Maria across the landing.

The flat opposite was the rented one. Mrs Grewal had three bedrooms in it, each let separately, a shared bathroom on the landing inside, a kitchen alcove off the corridor. The room she had available was the tightest of the three. A single bed. A wardrobe. A desk under the window. A sink in the corner. The window faced onto the back court. The court had a patch of grass and three bins.

Mrs Grewal said: *one hundred and eighty per month, paid on the first, cash if you prefer. heating is included. bathroom is shared with the other two. kitchen alcove is shared. there is a key for the front door and a key for the room. the rules are: pay on time, keep the room clean, no trouble in the building. that's all of it.*

Maria said: *I can do those.*

Mrs Grewal said: *Donald said you're sixteen.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Mrs Grewal said: *the council's age for renting is sixteen if you are working. you are working?*

Maria said: *yes. at the Counter on Leith Walk.*

Mrs Grewal said: *all right then. the name on the lease will be Mary Donnelly. I do not need to know what is on your birth certificate.*

Maria said: *thank you, Mrs Grewal.*

Mrs Grewal said: *no thanks. I am charging you rent.*

She handed Maria the key to the room.

She said: *I'll hold it for two weeks. you can move in when you have your first month's rent.*

---

Maria paid the first month's rent on the morning of the seventy-eighth day at the Cowgate, in cash, in a brown envelope with *September* written on it in pencil. She had saved the £180 from the cash Fiona had been paying her. She had also, the morning before, gone into a hardware shop on Leith Walk and had bought a single pillow, a thin duvet, and a set of cheap bed sheets from the bedding rack at the back, with the £32 she had set aside for that purpose.

Mrs Grewal took the envelope at the door across the corridor. She counted the notes once. She said: *September. all right. I'll write you a receipt.*

She went and got the receipt book. She wrote out the receipt. She handed it to Maria.

She said: *the spider plant on the windowsill in your room came with the room. I take cuttings off it now and then for the other rooms. it will keep if you remember to water it. once a week, a half cup. that is all.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Mrs Grewal said: *that's it, hen. settle in.*

She closed her door.

Maria walked back across the landing into the rented flat. She let herself into her room. She set the backpack on the bed. She took out the horse book and the four Ms Kerr books and *Far From the Madding Crowd* that Mrs Cargill had given her in 2009, and she set them on the desk in the order she had carried them. She unpacked the change of clothes into the chest of drawers. The new pillow on the bed. The thin duvet over it. The cheap sheets on top.

She sat on the bed.

She looked at the spider plant on the windowsill. The plant had two long thin shoots coming off the main rosette, each with a small offset growing on the end. The plant was not new. The pot was a small terracotta one with a chip in the rim.

She got up. She went to the sink in the corner. She filled the tooth-mug a quarter of the way with cold water. She brought it to the windowsill. She poured the water around the base of the plant.

She sat back down.

She was in a flat.

The flat was hers for as long as she paid the rent.

She did not have a framework for what that meant. The framework would build itself over the months that followed, in the small ways the body learns what it can let down and what it cannot. For the moment she sat on the bed in the small room with the back court three floors down and the smell of cumin coming from the kitchen across the corridor and listened to the sound of a bus going past on Easter Road.

She slept that night for ten hours.

---

She lived in the room on Easter Road from September of 2012 until May of 2025. The room did not change in any large way across those years. The cheap sheets were replaced once in 2014 with better cotton ones she paid for out of the second year's Tuesday cash. The bed was replaced once in 2019 when the springs gave out. The desk, by 2024, had a small archive of seven notebooks under it. The wardrobe held a slowly improving sequence of clothes. The window had, by 2018, two spider plants on it that were both clippings from the one that had come with the room, of which the first had become large and lush and the second smaller but greener.

Mrs Grewal died on the twelfth of October 2019 of pancreatic cancer. She was seventy-three. Her son Sandeep inherited the Easter Road flat and kept the tenants on the same terms his mother had set. The rent did not go up. Maria stayed in the room until May 2025.

But that was thirteen years away.

In the autumn of 2012, Maria sat in the room on Easter Road with the spider plant on the windowsill and the cheap sheets on the bed and read.

---

She had, on the inside of the wardrobe door, set a list.

The list was on a piece of A4 paper, Sellotaped at the four corners. She had written it across an evening in late September with one of the pencils Lyn had given her for her thirteenth birthday, of which she still had two left.

The list said:

*1. history. read general histories first then specific.*
*2. politics. read about how things are decided.*
*3. economics. read about money. ask Fiona for her old textbooks.*
*4. psychology. read about minds.*
*5. computer science. read about how the chatbots work.*
*6. write better. write every day. read good writers and try to learn how they do it.*

She had a card for the central library on George IV Bridge by the second week of September. She had asked Fiona, on the Tuesday before that, about economics textbooks. Fiona had said: *the library will have them. I have not read one since 1992. you don't want what I would have.* Maria had said: *all right.* Fiona had said: *Wilkinson and Pickett. *The Spirit Level*. start there. it's not a textbook. it's better.* Maria had got *The Spirit Level* out of the library that Saturday and had read it across the autumn.

She read across the autumn and winter thirty books. Schama's *A History of Britain*, volumes one to three. *The Spirit Level*. A pamphlet on the 2011-12 welfare reforms that she picked up from a cardboard box outside a community centre on Easter Road. A short book on the 2008 financial crisis aimed at general readers, that the library had on the new-arrivals shelf. How the Westminster system worked. The Scottish Parliament. The history of the post office — that one she read because of Rita at the Counter. The cleaning industry's labour market in Britain since 1980, because of her mother.

She read the books slowly. She wrote in the third notebook in the evenings — the third because the second had run out on the bus from Glasgow to Edinburgh in July. She wrote what she had read. She wrote questions she had not understood. She wrote the names of things she would need to look up.

She also wrote, on the back pages of the third notebook, a separate list. The list was of words and phrases she had heard at the Counter from people she could see in the city centre. *Compliance officer. Beneficial ownership. Pre-tax. Marginal rate. Drawdown. Trust structure. Capital event. Cross-border. Family office. Sailing weekend. Domiciled. Resident but not domiciled. Settlor-interested.* She did not yet know what most of them meant. She wrote them down. She looked them up later, one at a time, at the library terminal.

By the end of January 2013 she had looked up forty of them. By the end of March, sixty.

She did not, at seventeen, know she was building a vocabulary. She thought she was making a list of words she had heard and did not know. By the summer of 2013 the list was the beginning of the vocabulary she would need for the Tuesday work.

---

She walked the city in the autumn evenings and in the winter weekends. The walking was free, and she did not yet know the place.

The New Town first. She had the names of streets from the list at the back of the third notebook and from the bus routes she had committed to memory in the first month. Queen Street and George Street and Princes Street and the lanes between them. Charlotte Square. Heriot Row. Drummond Place. The small streets between Dundas Street and Howe Street where the basements still had bootscrapers set into the stone beside the steps.

The West End next. Atholl Crescent and Coates Crescent and Manor Place. Charlotte Lane behind the offices. Stafford Street.

The Grange in November. Three miles south of the centre and the houses were detached and the gardens had high stone walls. She walked the streets — Dick Place, Lauder Road, Findhorn Place, Strathfillan Road — and looked at the houses in the dusk through the gaps in the walls, and counted them, and noticed which had cars on the gravel and which had two cars and which had three.

Murrayfield in December. The houses were set back from the road behind clipped hedges. The streets — Murrayfield Drive, Garscube Terrace, Ravelston Dykes — and the same kinds of things on the drives.

She walked Morningside in January. She walked Stockbridge in February. She walked Trinity in March because Charlie at the Counter had a flat in Trinity and she had wondered what Trinity looked like.

She did not yet know what she was doing with the walking. She was learning the city. She was also, in a way she did not yet have language for, mapping where the money was. The mapping became the second layer of the vocabulary list.

By May of 2013 she could have walked any of the streets in the West End or the Grange or Murrayfield with her eyes closed.

---

In the late spring of 2013 she also began, in measured ways, to edit her body.

She had been on the floor of the Counter five mornings a week for nine months. Her shoulders had set differently. Her hands had stopped looking like the hands of a child. She had grown an inch and a half across the previous year and was now five foot seven. The skin under her eyes had cleared. The hair on her arms had come in.

She bought, with cash from the Counter, a small kit of things from a chemist on Princes Street. She bought, separately, a pair of tweezers from a different chemist. She bought, separately, a hair conditioner that had silk in it from a third. She had read at the library, across April, an article in *Marie Claire* and an article in a magazine called *Tatler* and a pamphlet on personal grooming the library had on a free-publications rack. She had also watched, at the till at the Counter, the women who came in to buy coffee at half past eight on a weekday and who were the kind of women whose work involved being looked at — and she had noticed the small particular things they did with their hair and their nails and the fold of their collars.

She practised at the small sink in the corner of her room. She practised the way she did everything — slowly, without rushing, and without showing anyone she was practising.

She also began, in May, to walk differently. The walk she had was a working-class Glasgow girl's walk, done without thinking. She watched the women in the New Town and she practised, on Easter Road in the morning on the way to the Counter, an adjustment. The adjustment was small. The angle of the head. The looseness of the shoulders. The way the heels hit the ground first.

She also stopped, in May, the small habit of looking down when an adult man came past her on the pavement. She had been doing that since she was about ten. She had been doing it for the reasons she had been doing it. By May 2013 she had decided that the next stage of the work she was beginning to plan needed it stopped. She stopped. The stopping took her three weeks. It cost her, in those three weeks, two hundred adjustments at the level of the body.

Mrs Grewal said to her at the round table by the window of the kitchen across the corridor, on a Sunday afternoon in the second week of June 2013: *Mary. you are walking differently.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Mrs Grewal said: *all right.*

She did not press.

She did not press across the next eleven years.

---

In August of 2013 Maria began the practical preparation for what she had been building toward.

She had decided, on the Sunday morning at the round table when Mrs Grewal had told her she was walking differently, that the practical work started by autumn. She had eight months of Counter wages saved by then — about £1,200 in cash in the canvas pouch under a floorboard in the wardrobe. She did not, on her budget, need the pouch money for rent or food. The pouch was the working capital for the next stage.

The preparation, across August, was three things.

The first was clothes.

She bought, in the last week of August, a dress from a charity shop in Stockbridge that had a Vivienne Westwood label on the inside neckline. The dress had been £18. It had been a size 12. Maria was an 8. The dress was the right cut and the wrong size. She took it to a tailor on Henderson Row called Wojciech who did alterations for the New Town. Wojciech took it in across the bust and the waist for £22. Maria collected it the following Tuesday. The dress, when she tried it on at the tailor's, read on her body in the mirror as a dress that had been bought new for £450 and worn three times.

She bought, the same week, a pair of black low-heeled shoes from a charity shop on Comely Bank Road, £6. She bought a strand of costume pearls passing for real from a small jeweller on Hanover Street, £45. She bought a black cardigan from a charity shop in Stockbridge for £4 and a small black clutch bag from a charity shop on Causewayside for £3.

She washed the dress on the cool cycle at the launderette on Leith Walk. She steamed it over the bath in Mrs Grewal's bathroom while Mrs Grewal was at the temple on a Sunday morning. She hung the dress on a wooden hanger on the back of her wardrobe door.

The second was the reading.

She had read across August three of the books Wilkinson and Pickett had cited, and four of the books the central library had on Edinburgh's New Town and on the kind of people who lived in it. The names of two streets in the West End, three streets in the Grange, four streets in Murrayfield — committed. The rates at the Caledonian, the Balmoral, and the Roxburghe — committed. Three restaurants where men of the kind she was looking for took women — the Witchery by the Castle, Number One at the Balmoral, Restaurant Martin Wishart in Leith.

She had not memorised these things from any source the men she was looking for could spot. The reading had been done in such a way that the knowledge had become her own — the way a woman who had grown up with such things knew them, casually, without effort, without performance.

The third was the practising.

She practised at the small sink in the corner of her room. How to order a drink. How to say *thank you* to a barman. How to look at a man across a bar without it being a look she had to make. How to read a book at a table in such a way that a man read her as reading the book and not as waiting for him. The book she chose, by the third week of August, was Roth's *American Pastoral*. She had not yet read it. Bought second-hand from a charity shop on Stockbridge for £2. She practised holding it open at page seventy-three.

She also did, across the four weeks of August, the practical work of safety. On a piece of paper folded into the inside pocket of the small black clutch, the rules Susan Bryce had given her in 2010. On a second piece of paper, a list of phone numbers — a black taxi rank in Stockbridge, a black taxi rank at Waverley, the front desk of the Caledonian, the front desk of the Balmoral, the front desk of the Roxburghe, the police non-emergency line, the Counter (Fiona). On a third, the address on Easter Road. All three in the clutch.

She set, in the wardrobe, three small things on the shelf above the hanging dress. A small bottle of Chanel No 5 she had bought second-hand for £6. A small lipstick in a colour the women in the New Town wore. A small compact of pressed powder.

She looked at the shelf on the evening of the last Friday in August.

She thought: *Tuesday.*

---

The first Tuesday was the seventeenth of September 2013.

She had told Fiona, on the previous Saturday, that she would not be working evenings on Tuesdays from now on. Fiona had said: *I do not run evenings.* Maria had said: *I know. I am telling you.* Fiona had said: *all right.*

She had told Mrs Grewal, on the Sunday morning, that she would be out late on Tuesdays. Mrs Grewal had said: *all right. the front door is on the latch from eleven. you have your key.*

Maria had said: *yes.*

Mrs Grewal had said: *that is all I am asking.*

She had not asked.

---

The Caledonian was on the western end of Princes Street, in a red sandstone building beside the railway line, with a doorman in a top hat at the entrance.

Maria walked up the steps at twenty past seven in the evening on the seventeenth of September 2013. She was in the Vivienne dress, the pearls, the black low-heeled shoes, the cardigan over her shoulders, the small clutch. She had on a touch of the lipstick. The doorman opened the door. He said: *good evening, madam.*

Maria said: *good evening.*

She walked through the lobby. The carpet was a dark green with a small gold pattern. The walls were panelled in oak. She walked to the bar at the end of the lobby.

The bar was a long room with a polished oak bar across the back, a row of small tables along the windows facing onto Princes Street, and a row of leather armchairs in the middle. There were eight people in the bar. Six men, two women. The two women were sitting at a table by the window with two men, in conversation. The four other men were at the bar.

Maria walked to one of the small tables along the windows. She set the clutch on the table. She sat down. She got *American Pastoral* out of the clutch. She opened it at page seventy-three.

The barman came over.

He said: *good evening, madam. what can I get you.*

Maria said: *a Hendrick's with cucumber, please.*

The barman said: *with tonic?*

Maria said: *Fever-Tree, if you have it. yes.*

The barman said: *we have it.*

He went back to the bar.

Maria read page seventy-three. She was reading the book the way a woman who had read it before and was reading it for the second time would, on a Tuesday evening, waiting for somebody. She was also reading the room. The four men at the bar. The two couples at the other tables. The barman. The doorman she had passed on the way in. The lighting. The exits.

The barman brought the drink over. Maria thanked him. He went back to the bar.

She drank a sip. She turned the page.

She waited.

A man sat down at the table next to hers at twenty past eight.

He was in his late fifties. He was in a grey suit cut by a tailor she could not name but whose work had cost the kind of money a tailor's name implies. He had a watch that was either a Patek Philippe or a very good imitation. He had silver hair cut close and a face that had been tanned by a recent trip somewhere south. He carried himself like a man who had never in his life been asked to wait for anything.

He had on his table a glass of whisky.

He looked, across the small distance between their tables, at Maria.

He said: *what are you reading.*

Maria looked up. She set the book down. She held his gaze for a count of two before she answered.

She said: *Roth. *American Pastoral*.*

He said: *I have not read it in years.*

Maria said: *it holds up.*

He said: *what bit are you at.*

Maria said: *the swimming pool.*

He said: *aye. that's the bit.*

He paused. He took a sip of his whisky.

He said: *I am Robert. Robert Henderson. may I join you.*

Maria said: *Mary. yes.*

He stood up from his table. He came across to hers. He sat down opposite her. The barman, who had been watching, came over with Robert's whisky. He set it on the table.

Robert said to Maria: *can I get you another.*

Maria said: *yes please. the same.*

Robert said to the barman: *and another Macallan eighteen.*

The barman went.

Robert said: *now. I have not seen you in here before.*

Maria said: *no.*

Robert said: *but you knew the cucumber.*

Maria said: *I read about it.*

Robert said: *aye.*

He looked at her for a moment.

He said: *what brings you to the Caledonian on a Tuesday.*

Maria said: *I wanted a drink in a nice room.*

Robert said: *fair enough.*

She did the listening across the next ninety minutes. Robert talked about a deal he had done that day, about a sailing weekend he had coming up in Argyll, about a daughter who had moved to London in the spring. Maria asked the questions of a woman who had not heard any of it before. She asked them in such a way that Robert filled in the answer without realising he had been asked. She had been reading, since Susan Bryce had told her to in 2010, the kind of writing the kind of men Robert was wrote about themselves. She had been watching the women at the till at the Counter for nine months. She had worked out, across those readings and those mornings, what the work was. The work was the listening. She did it.

At ten past ten Robert said: *Mary. I have a room here. care to come up for a nightcap.*

Maria said: *what does the room cost me.*

Robert paused. He looked at her again. His face shifted a quarter of an inch. He understood.

He said: *what is the room worth, Mary.*

Maria said: *four hundred.*

Robert said: *that is reasonable.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Robert said: *cash.*

Maria said: *cash.*

Robert said: *all right.*

They finished their drinks. They went upstairs.

She did the work in his room on the third floor of the Caledonian Hotel, with her eyes on his face. The listening was the work. She had worked that out on her own, from the *Daily Record* articles at the Mitchell at twelve and from the *London Review* articles Mrs Cargill had left her at thirteen and from the *Take a Break* magazines on the front-room sofa at Susan Bryce's at fourteen. What these men did not get at home and would not get from anyone else who knew them. He was a child of seven hungry for somebody to listen. He had talked for ninety minutes before he had touched her.
