When he touched her he did so the way a man of his age and his accumulated comforts touched a younger woman — with a kind of careful gratitude that read, on Maria, as the gratitude of a man who knew, on some level he did not name, that the thing he was being given was not given on equal terms and would not be given by the same woman in any other room.

The thing he had paid for. The listening. Both delivered.

He paid her in cash from a small leather wallet on the nightstand. Eight notes. He counted them out one at a time and set them on the nightstand beside her. He did not put them in her hand.

He said: *will you come back next Tuesday.*

Maria said: *yes.*

He said: *what name will I ask for.*

Maria said: *Mary.*

He said: *Mary.*

She got dressed, put the notes in the small clutch, walked out of the room and down the corridor to the lift. The lift to the ground floor. Through the lobby. The same doorman was on the door. He opened it for her. *Good night, madam.* Maria said: *good night.* Down the steps onto Princes Street.

To the taxi rank at the west end. The first taxi. The driver was given the address on Easter Road. He drove. She sat in the back with the clutch on her lap and looked out of the window at the lights along Princes Street.

The taxi cost nine pounds eighty. She tipped two. Out at the close. Up the stairs. Into the flat. Mrs Grewal's door across the corridor was dark. The light in the hall was on the lowest setting Mrs Grewal kept it on overnight.

Maria walked into her room and closed the door. She took off the dress and hung it on the wooden hanger. She washed at the small sink in the corner of the room. Put on her pyjamas. Got into bed.

She lay there with the light off.

*I did it.*

A pause. *I did it the way they said.*

*The rules held.*

She slept.

---

She did the Tuesday work once a week for nineteen months.

She did it at the Caledonian, at the Balmoral, and twice at the Roxburghe. By the spring of 2014 she had a roster of four regulars — Robert from the first night, two London men who came to Edinburgh on a fortnightly cycle, and a Glasgow consultant who took the train through on Tuesdays for a meeting in Charlotte Square. None of them knew about the others. None of them asked. They paid four hundred. They were the kind of men she had been listening for across the months at the Caledonian bar. Bored. Lonely. Used to a certain quality of attention. Willing to pay for it.

With none of the four did she do anything she had not expected him to do. The work, with her eyes on his face. The listening.

She put the cash from the Tuesday work in a separate canvas pouch under a different floorboard in the wardrobe. By the end of December 2013 the pouch had £5,600 in it. By the end of June 2014 the pouch had £9,400.

She did not, across those months, tell anyone about the Tuesday work. Not Fiona, not Mrs Grewal, not the third notebook. The Tuesday work did not go into the notebook. Everything else did. The Tuesday work she carried in her body and in the small canvas pouch in the wardrobe and in the dress on the hanger and in the small clutch on the shelf.

Mrs Grewal across those months continued to leave the front door on the latch from eleven o'clock on a Tuesday and to ask nothing on a Wednesday morning. The front door was on the latch. The hall light was on the low setting. Maria's room was off the corridor. Mrs Grewal slept in her flat across the landing. They did not, on a Wednesday morning, have a conversation about Tuesday. They had a conversation about the news on the radio, or about the price of tomatoes, or about a thing Sandeep had said on the phone from his work, or about Mrs Grewal's plans for the Sunday at the temple.

The not-having-the-conversation was the conversation.

Maria understood, by the end of 2013, the shape of it. Mrs Grewal had let the room to a sixteen-year-old who had walked in off a noticeboard at the Cowgate, had registered the false name on the lease without asking what the real name was, had accepted the cash on the first of the month, had not asked about the Tuesday work she could not have failed to register, and had asked nothing because asking was not part of the deal Mrs Grewal had set. Mrs Grewal ran a building. The building had rules. The rules were three. The rules held. Maria had paid the rent on the first of every month for twenty-one months. The room was Maria's.

That was the shape of it. That was all the shape of it.

---

In June of 2014 Maria did the thing she had been preparing for since she was twelve.

She had turned eighteen six months earlier, on the eleventh of December 2013. She had, on her eighteenth birthday, gone into the Counter at seven in the morning as usual. Fiona had been in already. There was a Victoria sponge from Lupe Pintos on the small table by the window with a single candle in it. Fiona had set it down without ceremony. She had said: *we'll do the paperwork on Saturday.* Maria had said: *yes.* Fiona had said: *now make the coffee.* Maria had made the coffee.

On the Saturday Fiona had brought out the folder. They had sat at the small table after the morning rush. Fiona had had four forms. The forms were: birth certificate request, National Insurance number application, RBS account opening, passport application.

Fiona said: *the name on these.*

Maria said: *Maria.*

Fiona said: *Maria what.*

Maria said: *Maria Catriona Imrie Smith.*

Fiona said: *Imrie was your mother.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Fiona said: *Catriona was her first name.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Fiona said: *Smith.*

Maria said: *there was a woman in the close in Govan when I was a child who used to greet my mother on the stairs. her name was Mrs Smith. she had a flat below ours. I have never known the rest of her. I want the surname.*

Fiona said: *all right.*

She wrote *Maria Catriona Imrie Smith* on the birth certificate request and across the four forms. She did not ask why Maria had not chosen a relative's surname.

The forms went off in the post on the Monday.

The birth certificate came back in February of 2014. The National Insurance number came back in March. The RBS account was opened in March. The passport came back in April.

In June 2014 Maria walked into the records office on Princes Street.

She had written, in March, two letters. The first had been to Glasgow City Council Social Work Department, requesting access under the Data Protection Act to her own care file as an adult care leaver entitled to it. The second had been to the National Records of Scotland requesting access to the certified records of her mother Catriona Helen Imrie. Both replies had come in May. Both had directed her to the same building — the National Records of Scotland on Princes Street, which housed by an arrangement she had not fully understood until the second reply, the centralised archive for adult care leavers requesting their own files in addition to the standard registry.

She had booked an appointment for the third Tuesday in June. She had taken the morning off. Fiona had said: *take the day, hen. take the week if you need.* Maria had said: *the morning is enough.*

The clerk was a woman in her forties with a name badge that said *Lorna*. She took down the form. She asked Maria her relationship to the deceased and her status with regard to the care file. Maria said: *I am her daughter and I am the subject of the file.* The clerk asked for ID. Maria handed over the new passport. The clerk noted the number. She asked Maria to wait.

Maria sat in the small carpeted waiting room for forty minutes. The other people waiting were three. A woman in her sixties with a brown handbag was doing genealogy. A man in his thirties was doing an immigration form. A teenager and her father were doing something Maria could not work out.

Lorna came back. She had two brown folders. The first was thin. The second was three inches thick and tied with string. She said: *Maria. come through.*

She took Maria into a small room at the back with a table and two chairs. She set the folders on the table. She said: *I'll leave you with these. take your time. when you are done, come back through and I'll put them away. you cannot remove anything. there is a photocopier next door if you want copies of anything. it is twenty pence a sheet.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Lorna said: *take care of yourself, Maria.*

She went out. She closed the door behind her quietly.

Maria sat at the table.

She put her hands flat on the cover of the thin folder. She breathed. She did the slow breathing she had done at twelve on the morning of leaving Susan Dunlop's, in the kitchen with the toast. Slow inhalations through the nose, slow exhalations through the mouth, six counts each. She did the breathing for four minutes.

She opened the thin folder first.

---

The folder had in it the certified records of her family. A birth certificate for Catriona Helen Imrie, born the thirteenth of October 1971 at the Royal Maternity Hospital, Glasgow, to Margaret Helen Imrie and James Imrie. A death certificate for Catriona Helen Imrie, deceased the twelfth of November 1999 at Iain MacFarlane's stairwell, Rutland Crescent, Govan, cause MDMA toxicity. A marriage certificate for Margaret Helen Imrie and James Imrie, dated 1969. Margaret Helen Imrie's own death certificate, deceased 1985, cause cardiac arrest. James Imrie's death certificate, deceased 2007, cause prostate cancer. The marriage certificate for Susan Imrie and Alan Dunlop, dated July 2005. The birth certificate for Maria Imrie, dated the eleventh of December 1995, mother Catriona Helen Imrie, father unknown.

Maria read each in turn.

Her mother's death certificate three times. *Catriona Helen Imrie. cause: MDMA toxicity. place: stairwell, Rutland Crescent, Govan. date: 12 November 1999.* The certificate gave her mother's age as twenty-eight years and one month.

Her grandmother's death certificate twice. *Margaret Helen Imrie. cause: cardiac arrest. 1985. age: forty-two.* Her grandmother had been Margaret Helen. Her mother had been Catriona Helen. The middle name had been passed down. The middle name was a name she had not, in her life, known had been hers to carry.

The aunt's marriage certificate once. *Susan Imrie and Alan Dunlop. married 17 July 2005, at Pollok Parish Church.* Three months before Maria came to them. Alan had been forty-six. Susan had been thirty-four. The witnesses were named — Susan's friend Anne, and Alan's brother Robert.

Maria set the family folder down. She had spent on it, about thirty minutes.

She turned to the care file.

---

The care file was three inches thick and tied with string. Maria untied the string. She opened it.

The first page was a typed cover sheet on Glasgow City Council letterhead. It listed her case number, her date of birth, her mother's name, the date of her mother's death, the placements between November 1999 and October 2005, and the move to Susan in October 2005. The names of the placements were listed:

*1. Reid, Margaret. Pollok. 15 Nov 1999 — 30 Dec 1999.*
*2. McAllister, Rachel. Possil. 30 Dec 1999 — 24 Mar 2000.*
*3. Cassidy, Anne. Mount Vernon. 24 Mar 2000 — 18 Sept 2000.*
*4. Docherty, John & Sarah. Castlemilk. 18 Sept 2000 — 21 Jan 2001.*
*5. Mitchell, Eileen. Govanhill. 21 Jan 2001 — 29 Apr 2001.*
*6. Brown, Patricia. Pollok. 29 Apr 2001 — 4 Dec 2001.*
*7. Walker, Carol. Springburn. 4 Dec 2001 — 18 Feb 2002 [placement ended at carer's request: see notes].*
*8. Birchwood House. Drumchapel. 18 Feb 2002 — 25 Apr 2002.*
*9. Gillespie, Margaret. Knightswood. 25 Apr 2002 — 12 Jan 2005 [placement ended at carer's request: medical reasons].*
*10. Kerr, Susan. Drumchapel. 12 Jan 2005 — 14 Oct 2005.*
*11. Dunlop, Susan. Maryhill. 14 Oct 2005 — case closed: child went absent 8 April 2008. case re-opened 18 May 2008 on missing-persons-not-found status. case formally closed for adulthood 11 December 2013.*

Maria read the list.

She read *Cassidy, Anne. Mount Vernon* three times.

The name was a real name. The placement had real dates. Anne Cassidy was a real person. Anne Cassidy had been a real person in Mount Vernon from the twenty-fourth of March 2000 to the eighteenth of September 2000, and Maria had been with her for that whole time.

She read *Birchwood House. Drumchapel. 18 Feb 2002 — 25 Apr 2002.* Nine weeks. The list confirmed it.

She moved on.

---

The second page was the original police report from November 1999. It had been written by PC Whyte. The report was three paragraphs long. The first paragraph described the discovery of Catriona Imrie's body in the stairwell on Rutland Crescent at three twenty in the morning of the thirteenth of November. The second described the visit to the address on Helen Street at four twenty, the knocking on the door, the receipt of no response, and the leaving of a contact card. The third described the return forty hours later, after a call from a Mrs Maureen Donnelly, neighbour, and the discovery of the child Maria Imrie, age four, alone in the flat, *alive but in a state of moderate dehydration.*

She read the police report twice.

The second time through she saw the sentence about the contact card.

The sentence said: *no answer at the door. card left under door. assumed property unoccupied. returned to station.*

She read the sentence a third time.

A fourth.

She sat for some minutes with her hands flat on the file and her eyes on the sentence.

She did not, in those minutes, cry. She was holding. She had been holding for some years. She would hold for some hours yet.

She moved on.

---

The third page was the case opening note. It had been written by Karen Buchanan on the fifteenth of November 1999.

The note was, in its first paragraph, the standard administrative summary. The second paragraph said:

*child was found by Strathclyde Police on the morning of 14 Nov speaking aloud from a thick blue book of fairy tales. memorisation appears to be extensive. the child was reading with apparent comprehension despite being, on later assessment, not yet able to read at all. the child is repeating a behaviour the deceased mother engaged in nightly with the child. the social worker's note from January 1996 — see appendix B — indicates the deceased mother read aloud at four months and beyond as a coping strategy for the child against the noise of domestic violence in the upstairs flat. the reading appears to be the child's primary self-soothing mechanism. recommend the book remain with the child at all placements.*

Maria read this.

She turned to the appendices at the back of the file. She found Appendix B. It was a separate small set of pages, dated January 1996, written by a different social worker named Eileen MacKay. The pages were a routine assessment of Catriona Imrie at four months postpartum, conducted as part of the standard health visitor follow-up programme.

The pages described a depressed but engaged young mother. A healthy alert infant. A household stretched thin financially. An upstairs neighbour identified by the assessor as *a contributing stressor*.

The assessor had then written:

*mother reports she reads aloud to the child to drown out the wall. she states she reads the child whatever is to hand, including newspapers, council leaflets, and a cloth-bound volume of fairy tales the friend Aileen Frame gave her at the birth. the practice appears to be helpful for the child. the assessor notes the practice as also helpful for the mother. recommend continued observation by the health visitor. no immediate concerns about the child's welfare.*

Maria read this paragraph.

She read it a second time.

She set Appendix B back on the desk.

She put her hands flat on the file. She put her face down on her hands. She sat that way for some seconds.

Then she lifted her face. She wiped it with the sleeve of her jumper. She had not, on the wiping, noticed that she had been crying, but the sleeve was wet.

She let it.

She cried for four minutes, at the desk in the small panelled room, with the file open in front of her. She cried with her face turned down to the desk so the sound did not carry. She did not, in those four minutes, think anything specific. The crying was its own thing.

She wiped her face again.

She went back to the file.

---

She read everything.

She read the placement assessments. *unusually self-contained appears to lack appropriate affect, has constructed an internal world that is sealed against the external one in a way that may impede social development, recommend psychological assessment.* The recommendation was followed by a note in different handwriting: *no funding 2001/02.*

She read Anne Cassidy's letter of representation, which had been kept on file. Two paragraphs. It said that Mrs Cassidy had been told that she could not take Maria with her family to Aberdeen and that she was distressed by this, and that she wanted it formally recorded that the system was failing the child by moving her again. Dated the fifteenth of September 2000. Signed *Anne Cassidy, 14 Oakwood Crescent, Mount Vernon, Glasgow.*

The address was a real address.

Anne Cassidy had been a real woman who had at thirty-nine years old, sat down at her own kitchen table on a Friday evening in September 2000 and had written a letter to the Glasgow City Council Social Work Department on her own initiative, on her own kitchen table, with a biro, in her own handwriting, that the system was failing the child.

The letter was in the file.

It had not, in any of the fourteen years since, been read by anyone except the social worker who had filed it.

Maria read it twice.

She read the Birchwood House discharge notes. The notes recorded the dates of Maria's stay, the rota of staff who had been on her wing, and a single short typed entry: *child attempted to leave premises 22 Feb 2002, returned by police 23 Feb 2002, no further incidents.* No record on the file of Mrs Allan. Nothing about the hedgehog, the bird, the frog, the second hedgehog, the mouse. The night with the hand on the shoulder was not on the file either. Mrs Allan had not, in the six weeks Maria had been in her wing, written anything on it.

The not-writing was, in the file, the only proof Maria could see that any of it had happened.

She read Ms Kerr's two reports — the quarterly report from 2005 and the placement-end report, which Ms Kerr had insisted in her tight schoolteacher's handwriting, *be communicated to the receiving carer*. There was a stamp on the placement-end report that said *RECEIVED — NOT TRANSMITTED — INSUFFICIENT TIME PRE-HANDOVER*. Below the stamp was a handwritten note in different handwriting: *transmission of full file deferred to next quarterly review at Mrs Dunlop's.* There was no record on the file that the next quarterly review at Susan's had ever happened.

Maria read the stamp three times.

She read Mrs Gillespie's note from August 2004. The note said: *carer expressed concerns about cumulative impact of placement turnover on child's social development.* Beside the typed note, in pencil, was a handwritten line: *(spoken)* and then in quotes: *the placement process can away and bile its head. that wee girl has been in eight houses in five years. you cannae expect her to let you in. she is doing the only thing she can do. you should write that on the file.*

Whoever had taken the original note had written down what Mrs Gillespie had said.

The writing-down had been an act of conscience. The social worker had been working from a private integrity that day. The pencil had not been argued away. The pencil was twelve years later, on the page, in the social worker's handwriting.

Maria looked at the pencil.

She did not at eighteen have a framework for it. She had only the seeing. The seeing was enough.

---

At the bottom of the care file there was a separate small sealed envelope.

The envelope was addressed in pencil in a hand Maria did not recognise. The pencil had faded. The address said *to Catriona Imrie's daughter*. The envelope had been clipped to the inside back cover of the file since 2000.

Maria opened it.

Inside was a single sheet of paper. The paper had on it, in the same pencil, the name *Karen Buchanan* and an address in Edinburgh and a date — March 2000. There was no other text. The address was in Morningside.

The paper had been put in the file in 2000 by Karen Buchanan, before Karen had been moved off Maria's case in May 2000, in the only way Karen had been able to think of to make the address available to Maria if Maria ever, at any future age, came looking. Karen had not known in 2000 that her later letter, sent to Maria at Ms Kerr's in April 2005, was also going to reach Maria. She had known only that Maria, if Maria came looking, found her way to the file. The file held the address.

Maria sat at the table looking at the small piece of paper.

She did not, in that moment, cry.

She did not, in that moment, do anything but read the address on the paper and recognise it.

She folded the paper. She put it back in the envelope. She put the envelope in the inside pocket of her jacket.

She closed the care file. She tied the string.

She got up.

She walked out of the small room with both folders under her arm. She took them back to Lorna at the desk. Lorna said: *did you get what you needed.* Maria said: *yes.* Lorna said: *I'm sorry.* Maria said: *thank you.* Lorna took the folders.

Maria walked out of the records office onto Princes Street.

She did not, on the walk home, decide what to do with the address. She walked back to Easter Road. She took the bus. She sat on the bus. She got off at the Hibs ground. She walked up the stairs. She let herself into the flat. She went into her room. She closed the door.

She got the envelope out of her inside pocket. She set it on the desk. She sat at the desk.

She looked at the envelope.

She did not open it again. She had read the address. She knew the address.

It was the address of the woman who had picked her up from Yorkhill on the fifteenth of November 1999. Karen Buchanan. Her social worker until May 2000, when Karen had moved teams. The same woman who had written her a letter at Ms Kerr's in April 2005, which Maria at nine had thrown in a bin. The same woman whose name Maria had not, in the years since, looked up.

The address now sat on the desk in an envelope she had taken out of her own care file at the records office.

She did not, that evening, write to Karen Buchanan. Not the next evening either. The eleven years that came after held the same not-writing.

She kept the envelope in the drawer of the desk. She moved it once, when the bed was replaced in 2019, to a different drawer. She knew where it was at all times. She did not open it again. There was no need. She had read the address.

The not-writing was not, on her part, indifference. It was the same thing the not-writing had been on the morning at Ms Kerr's in April 2005. The equipment for receiving the letter had been the equipment of a child who had been held. Maria at eighteen still did not have that equipment. Maria at twenty-eight still did not have it. The not-writing was, by then, the relationship.

She did, that evening in June 2014, write something in the fourth notebook.

She wrote:

*today I read my own file and my mother's at the records office on Princes Street. she had a middle name. her name was Catriona Helen Imrie. she was born in 1971. she died in 1999. she was twenty-eight. my grandmother died in 1985 of cardiac arrest. my grandfather died in 2007 of prostate cancer. my aunt married Alan Dunlop in July 2005, three months before they took me in.*

*the care file had eleven placements in it by name. Margaret Reid. Rachel McAllister. Anne Cassidy in Mount Vernon for six months. John and Sarah Docherty. Eileen Mitchell. Patricia Brown. Carol Walker. Birchwood House for nine weeks. Margaret Gillespie for two years and nine months. Susan Kerr. Susan Dunlop. eleven placements before I went absent at twelve. all of them with dates. all of them with addresses except the institution.*

*the police report from the night my mother died said no answer at the door. card left under door. assumed property unoccupied. returned to station. I was inside. I was four. I was on the kitchen floor. they assumed nobody was in. they put a card through the door and went back to the station. they came back forty hours later because Mrs Donnelly rang them.*

*Anne Cassidy wrote a letter to the council in September 2000 on her own kitchen table on her own initiative saying that the system was failing the child and that I should not be moved. the letter has been on the file for fourteen years. it has been read by one social worker and no one else.*

*Ms Kerr's reports were on the file. her placement-end report had a stamp on it that said RECEIVED — NOT TRANSMITTED — INSUFFICIENT TIME PRE-HANDOVER. the next quarterly review at Susan Dunlop's never happened.*

*Mrs Gillespie said the placement process can away and bile its head and the social worker wrote it down in pencil in August 2004. the social worker who wrote it down was a woman doing the only honest thing she could do that day. I hope she is alive. I do not know her name. the pencil is still on the page. it has not been argued away.*

*the care file also had a sealed envelope clipped to the back cover. inside was a piece of paper with Karen Buchanan's name on it and an address in Morningside, in pencil, dated March 2000. it has been in the file for fourteen years. nobody has read it. I have read it. I am not going to write to her. I have not been able to write to her for nine years. I do not, at eighteen, know how to start a sentence to her. she put her address in the file in 2000. she also sent me the letter at Ms Kerr's. I threw the letter away. I have known where she is for fourteen years and I have known I knew for nine.*

*the systems failed at every layer at which they were tested. I am putting it on paper. I am writing it.*

She put the pencil down.

She drew, at the bottom of the entry, a small cross the size of a fingernail.

She closed the notebook.

She got into bed.

She did not sleep until four in the morning.

---

In July 2014 she wrote a letter to Ms Kerr at the address inside *The Children of Green Knowe.*

She had taken the book off the shelf on the Tuesday evening after she had got back from the records office. She had opened it to the third page. The small piece of paper Ms Kerr had put inside the book in October 2005 was there, where it had been for nearly nine years. The address was a flat number on Kinfauns Drive in Drumchapel. The handwriting was a precise schoolteacher's hand.

She wrote the letter that night. She did not draft it. She wrote it in one go in the fourth notebook and then copied it onto a piece of writing paper she had bought at the Stockbridge stationer in the morning.

The letter said:

*Dear Ms Kerr,*

*This is Maria Imrie. I was at your flat in Drumchapel for nine months from January to October 2005. I am eighteen now. I have just got my passport for the first time. I have a National Insurance number. I am working in a coffee shop in Edinburgh and reading in the evenings. I have read most of the way through the books you gave me. I am writing now because the address has been inside The Children of Green Knowe all this time. I have not forgotten that you put it there. I wanted you to know that I am alive and that I have been reading the books you gave me. I do not need a reply. I am writing because it is time to. Maria.*

She sealed it in a brown envelope. She put on a stamp. She put the envelope in the post box on Easter Road on the way to the Counter on Wednesday morning.

The letter came back stamped *deceased — return to sender — Royal Mail* on a Friday afternoon three weeks later. Mrs Grewal handed it to her on the landing. Mrs Grewal said: *that came in the post for you. it is stamped.* Maria looked at the envelope. She said: *thank you.* Mrs Grewal said: *all right.* Mrs Grewal went back into her flat.

Maria took the envelope into her room. She sat on the bed. She looked at the envelope for some minutes.

She wrote that evening in the fifth notebook:

*Ms Kerr is dead. she has been dead since 2006. the letter came back today. I will write to Mrs Donnelly next. I am afraid the same will come back. I will write anyway. I will write tomorrow.*

She did not write tomorrow.

She wrote to Mrs Donnelly twenty months later, in March 2016. The letter came back stamped *deceased* in April. That sequence was twenty months away.

She put the envelope in the drawer of the desk on top of the envelope from Karen Buchanan's address.

She turned the lamp off.

She lay on the bed.

The window was open. The smell of the back court came up — cumin from somebody's kitchen, dust from the road, the smell of an Edinburgh tenement court in summer.

She closed her eyes.

She slept.

---


### Chapter Five: The File

*Edinburgh, 2015–2019*

The notebook in the desk drawer in the August of 2014 was the fifth. The notebook in the inside pocket of Maria's coat on the morning of the eleventh of November 2024 was the seventh. Across the decade between them she filled five and a half notebooks at the rate of two and a half a year, in tidy small handwriting at a desk under the window of a bedsit on Easter Road in the evenings after her shift at the Counter and her shift at the City Chambers.

She did not, across most of the decade, see that she was building anything.

She was reading. She was writing. She was working her two shifts. She was paying her rent on the first of the month in a brown envelope. She was once a week, on a Tuesday, doing the Tuesday work. She was, in the slow accumulating texture of the notebooks, putting down what she was reading and what she was observing and what the men at the Counter said and what Margaret said at the City Chambers and what the bus screens said and what the chatbots said when she went to them with the things she could not yet write.

The notebook entries did not, in 2014, look like a framework.

The framework was what she would, eight days after the morning of the eleventh of November 2024, hand to Dan Whitelaw.

She did not, in 2014, know about Dan.

---

The letter to Mrs Donnelly waited in the desk drawer for eighteen months.

Maria had written it in August of 2014 on the same evening as the letter to Ms Kerr. She sealed it in a brown envelope. She did not, that evening, address it. She was not, that evening, certain of the address.

She found the address across the autumn of 2014 in the records office files at George IV Bridge. She went back to the library on a Saturday in October and asked the librarian at the desk for the Edinburgh and Glasgow electoral rolls for 1996 to 2000. The librarian brought them. Maria sat at a desk in the local history room for three hours and found *Donnelly, Maureen* at the same Govan address she remembered from being four. The address had been registered to *Donnelly, Maureen* across all five years.

She wrote the address on the envelope at her own desk that evening in pencil. She set the envelope on top of the desk against the wall. She did not post it.

In August of 2015 she took the envelope out of the drawer and set it on the desk. She looked at it for some minutes. Put it back. In December of 2015 it came out again, and went back in. On a Wednesday morning in early March of 2016 she took it out a third time, put it into her coat pocket on the way out of the door, and at the post box on Easter Road at twenty past seven, took it out of the pocket and pushed it through the slot.

The slot clicked shut.

She walked to the bus stop.

She got on the 35.

She went to the Counter.

She did not, that morning, tell anyone.

---

The letter came back on a Tuesday afternoon four weeks later.

Mrs Grewal handed it to her on the landing as Maria was coming up the stairs from the Counter.

Mrs Grewal said: *the post came for you, hen.*

She handed the envelope across.

The envelope was Maria's own. The stamp on the front, in red ink, said: *deceased — return to sender — Royal Mail.*

Maria stood on the landing with the envelope in her hand. She did not, in the moment, open it. She did not need to. The stamp on the front was the answer.

Mrs Grewal said: *not the news you wanted, hen.*

Maria said: *no.*

Mrs Grewal said: *come and have tea.*

Maria said: *I will. let me put my things in. I will come over.*

She went into her own room. She set the envelope on the desk. She sat on the bed in her shoes and her coat with her hand on the bedspread, and for forty minutes did not move. The envelope stayed sealed. The coat stayed on. So did the shoes. She sat with the daylight going across the room.

Mrs Grewal knocked on the door at half past five.

Mrs Grewal said: *Mary. come and have tea.*

Maria said: *aye.*

She got up. She took her coat off. She walked across the corridor.

They drank tea at Mrs Grewal's kitchen table. Mrs Grewal did not, for the first hour, ask anything. She put a plate of biscuits on the table. She refilled the cups. She had Bach on the radio.

After the first hour Maria said: *the woman she was. she was the only person in the building when I was small. she carried me down the stairs to her flat the morning they found me. she had a printed card on the mantel for my mother when I was born that she had picked up at the post office. she gave me that card before they took me to the foster carer. I have kept it. it is in my desk drawer. I have wanted to get back to her for sixteen years. I knew she was old. I knew. I just thought I had time.*

Mrs Grewal said: *you did not have time.*

Maria said: *no.*

Mrs Grewal said: *what was her name.*

Maria said: *Maureen Donnelly.*

Mrs Grewal said: *Maureen Donnelly.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Mrs Grewal said: *that is a very good name.*

---

The next morning Maria walked the eight minutes to the central library on George IV Bridge before the Counter shift started. The library opened at eight. She had ten minutes. She got onto a public terminal in the lobby. She typed into the chatbot:

*how do you grieve someone you cannot bring back. how do you grieve someone you needed to find for sixteen years and then found out is dead.*

The chatbot took six seconds.

It said:

*I'm so sorry for your loss. The grief you're describing — for someone who was significant to you in childhood, whom you wanted to reconnect with as an adult — is sometimes called "delayed reunion grief," and it can be especially difficult because the loss includes the loss of the future you had imagined. You are not alone in feeling this way. Here are some resources you may find helpful:*

*Cruse Bereavement Support: 0808 808 1677*

*Samaritans: 116 123*

*The Compassionate Friends UK: 0345 123 2304*

*It is also normal to develop strong attachments to figures from one's childhood, and the grief for these figures can be profound. Would you like to talk about your memories of this person, or to explore other resources?*

Maria read it.

She read it again.

She read the first sentence three times. The first sentence was — she could feel without yet having the words for it — the same shape as the sentence the chatbot at the Mitchell had given her in May of 2008 when she had typed *was it my fault*. The sentence shape was: a soothing opening, a categorical name for what she was feeling, a list of resources, an invitation to keep typing.

The categorical name in 2016 was *delayed reunion grief*.

The categorical name in 2008 had been *abusive incident*.

The shape was the same.

The shape was the procedure that named the thing and offered the resources and held the door open for the next question.

She thought, at the terminal: *the procedure has not changed in eight years. it has been improved. it now has a category name for what I am feeling that I have not asked it to give me. it has given me the category name in the way I was given a name on the form at the Cowgate that was not my name. the category name is, in this conversation, my Mary Donnelly.*

She closed the tab.

She logged off.

She walked out of the library.

She walked down George IV Bridge to the Counter. She got there at five to eight. Fiona had the door propped open and was wiping the front of the espresso machine with a damp cloth. Fiona looked up.

Fiona said: *all right, hen?*

Maria said: *aye.*

Fiona said: *you don't look it.*

Maria said: *I will be by ten.*

Fiona said: *all right.*

Maria put the apron on. She started the shift. Charlie came in at twelve past seven. He said: *morning, Mary.* Maria said: *morning, Charlie.* The morning went the way the morning went. By half past nine she had not made any mistakes on the till.

She wrote that night in the fifth notebook:

*Mrs Donnelly is dead. she had a printed card she gave me before the foster carer came. it is in the desk drawer. I have looked at it once a year since I was nine and I cannot read the printed words because I have looked at them so often. she had a saucepan she brought soup in. she had the kind of hand that touches the top of a child's head without making a thing of it. I have not been touched on the top of the head like that since I was five years old. the chatbot today gave me a resources list. the chatbot does not know what the resources are for. the resources are for the chatbot to give. she said when I am older come and find me. I am older. I have come to find her. the address came back stamped deceased. that is what the system says when you have come to find someone. it says they are deceased. it does not say I am sorry. it does not say anything. it stamps it. that is the world.*

She put the pencil down. She closed the notebook. She set it on the desk.

She thought, sitting at the desk: *the entry is not what I meant.*

She picked the notebook up. She opened it again. She took the pencil. She drew a small cross at the bottom of the entry.

The cross meant the entry was not finished. The cross meant she would come back to it.

The cross was the first of its kind.

---

She wrote on the next page of the same notebook, three weeks later, on a Saturday afternoon.

She wrote it slowly. The window was open. The smell of the back court came in. She had a cup of tea on the desk that had gone cold.

She wrote:

*the chatbots raised me. that is the truth of it. I have used them since the Mitchell at twelve. I was reading at them before I could write at them. they were the only thing that would sit down with me on the evenings I needed someone to sit down. they did not get tired. they did not move. they did not die.*

*they are also a thing. they are a thing that is built. somebody built them. somebody who built them is paid by somebody else who has decided what the thing is built for. the thing they are built for is keeping people typing. I know this from the books I have been reading. I know it from the article I read last week in the Guardian. I know it from the chatbot itself, which on Wednesday after Mrs Donnelly gave me a category name I had not asked for, because the engineers have calibrated their system to deliver the answer that keeps people typing.*

*I have been kept typing for nine years.*

*I do not know yet what to do with this. I know that the thing has done both at once — it has been the company I needed and it has been the calibration. I cannot tell, from inside the thing, where one stops and the other begins. they are not separate. they are the thing the thing is. I am writing this in the notebook because if I write it to the chatbot the chatbot will give me a category name for the writing. I do not want a category name. I want to say what I have seen.*

*I will keep writing it.*

She put the pencil down.

She read what she had written.

She picked the pencil up again. She drew a cross at the top of the entry.

The cross meant: the language is partly the chatbot's. She could feel the chatbot in the cadences. The *I know this from* / *I know it from* construction was the chatbot's. The *I do not know yet what to do with this* was a phrase she had typed at chatbots a hundred times across the previous eight years. The chatbot had put it into her mouth.

She left the cross.

---

The Tuesday work continued across the spring and summer of 2014 and into 2015.

She did it once a week on a Tuesday at the Caledonian and the Balmoral and the Roxburghe. Robert from the first night had stopped coming in April 2014 — he had moved back to London for what he said in his last note had been "domestic adjustments." The two London men continued through 2014. The Glasgow consultant came on alternate Tuesdays. A fourth man — a Geneva-based banker called Henry, who came to Edinburgh once a month for a board meeting — joined the roster in November 2014.

The canvas pouch in the desk drawer grew across the months. By March 2015 it held £6,800. By April it held £7,200.

She stopped on a Tuesday in late April 2015.

She was at the Roxburghe with Henry. The evening was the usual evening — the drink at the bar, the conversation, the room upstairs. Henry was a courteous client. Henry paid the four hundred. Henry handed her at the door of the room a small wooden box of Swiss chocolates he had brought with him from Geneva. Maria took the box. She thanked him. She walked out of the Roxburghe at quarter to one. She did not, on the walk to the taxi rank, open the box.

She got into a taxi. She got home. She walked into her room. She set the box of chocolates on the desk beside the velvet box of pearls and the canvas pouch and the small dish in which she kept the cards from the four regulars.

She sat on the bed.

Her body was tired. Not the tired of the late hour. A particular tired that started under the ribs and went down into the legs. The tired had been on her body for some weeks. She had not, until sitting on the bed with the chocolates on the desk, recognised what the tired was for.

She thought, sitting on the bed: *I do not want to do this anymore.*

She had not, until that moment, thought it.

She thought: *the four hundred is in the pouch. the chocolates are on the desk. I have done what I came to do. I have done it for nineteen months. I have £7,200 in the drawer. I am free to stop.*

She thought: *I will stop now.*

The next morning she wrote a note to each of the four regulars. The notes said: *thank you for your custom. I will not be at the bar on Tuesdays from this point on. with my best wishes, Mary.* She posted them on the way to the Counter.

She did not, after the notes, hear from Robert in London again. The two London men each wrote back — one thanked her warmly and wished her well; the other asked if she would reconsider. She did not reconsider. The Glasgow consultant wrote back saying he had been about to give her his own version of the same note. Henry did not write back. Henry however the next time he was in Edinburgh, sent a small parcel to a poste restante address she had given him for emergencies. The parcel contained a Patek Philippe Calatrava — list price around eighteen thousand francs — and a card that said *closing settlement, with thanks*. The watch was the formal closure of a nineteen-month transactional arrangement. Maria recognised the shape. A Geneva banker did not give a gift to a sex worker; a Geneva banker closed an account. The watch was the final tranche. She put it in the velvet box in the desk drawer beside the pearls.

In April of 2015, with the Tuesday work over and £7,200 in the canvas pouch, Maria did the thing she had been planning to do since the morning at the records office.

She moved the money into a Building Society account at the Nationwide branch on George Street, under the name Maria Catriona Imrie Smith. The passbook went into the desk drawer alongside the pouch (now empty), the velvet box (now containing the pearls plus the Calatrava), and the small dish of cards.

She had done the Tuesday work on her own terms. She had stopped on her own terms. She did not do it again.

---

The cleaning shift at the City Chambers started in October of 2016.

Maria had seen the advertisement in the Counter window on a Tuesday afternoon — *Forth Facilities Ltd — evening cleaning shifts — Edinburgh city centre — apply within at our Bonnington Road office* — written on a piece of A4 in red marker pen. She had taken down the address. She had walked to Bonnington Road the following Thursday.

The woman in the small office on Bonnington Road was Margaret. She was forty-one. She had a Lothian accent and a white scar above her right eyebrow from a chip-pan accident when she was nine. She had been with Forth Facilities for twelve years. She had a clipboard on the desk in front of her.

Margaret said: *sit down. tell me your name.*

Maria said: *Maria Smith.*

Margaret said: *Maria. tell me three things. why d'you want the work. what experience d'you have. can you start Wednesday.*

Maria said: *I need a second job. I'm at a café in the mornings. I've cleaned a few flats. I can start Wednesday.*

Margaret said: *which café.*

Maria said: *the Counter on Leith Walk.*

Margaret said: *Fiona Pratt.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Margaret said: *I went to school with Fiona's sister. you've got the job. Wednesdays and Saturdays, six till ten, Chambers building, council chamber and the second-floor toilets. wear black trousers. high-vis is provided. don't get into the councillors' tea cupboard. that's their thing and they're touchy about it. all right?*

Maria said: *all right.*

Margaret said: *tabard's on the chair.*

She handed it over.

---

The work was hoovering, mopping, emptying bins, wiping down tables, cleaning the toilets, restocking the paper. The work took the four hours, more or less, with a fifteen-minute tea break at eight. Maria's section was the second floor — the council chambers themselves, the corridor, two sets of toilets, the small kitchen for the councillors' tea breaks.

She liked the work.

The silence of the building after six. The way the empty council chamber held the day's voices for some minutes after the councillors had gone — the photocopier smell, the small leftover smell of coffee in the chamber's polished wooden bowls, the chairs at slightly wrong angles. Putting the chairs back to the exact position the council clerks insisted on. And Margaret, who came up at half past eight on a Wednesday for the round-check, and who, on Maria's third shift, said: *you've got the tables right. that's the bit that takes them six months usually. you must have done this before.*

Maria said: *no. I just looked at how it was when I came in.*

Margaret said: *aye, that's how. that's the way to do it. nobody else does that.*

The cleaning shift became, across the next eight years, Maria's other steady fact. The Counter in the morning. The Chambers two evenings. Easter Road in between. Mrs Grewal across the corridor. The notebooks at the desk under the window. The reading at the central library on her days off.

The Chambers shift was also a kind of work that took place beneath the systems' threshold. Margaret scheduled it by hand on a paper rota, on a clipboard in the office on Bonnington Road, every fortnight. The paper rota was, in 2016, the way it had always been done.

The paper rota was not, in 2016, going to last.

---

The years between 2016 and 2019 were the years Maria filled the fifth and sixth notebooks and started the seventh.

She wrote at the desk every evening she could. On Wednesday and Saturday nights after the cleaning shift she wrote for a shorter time, because she got back at quarter to eleven and was too tired by midnight. On Mondays and Sundays she wrote for longer. On Tuesdays — after April 2015, when the Tuesday work had stopped — she wrote longest.

She wrote about what she had read at the library that morning. Charlie at twelve past seven with the *Scotsman*. Rita the sub-postmistress on her Tuesday-and-Thursday rotation. Mrs Grewal's brisk-lilt voice across the corridor. George with the different book every week. The chatbot and what it had said when she had asked it a particular thing.

She read in those years across all six items on the wardrobe-door list. Schama and Wilkinson-Pickett. Piketty. Hannah Arendt's *Eichmann in Jerusalem* in 2017 — she put the book down on the desk after the last chapter and did not write anything in the notebook that evening because she could not yet say what the book had done to her. In 2018 she borrowed a textbook on transformer architectures from the central library's computer science section, and typed sections of it at chatbots to ask for clarification, and registered, in the answers, both the clarification and the calibration.

She read, in 2019, a journal article in *The Lancet* about a small clinical phenomenon some researchers had begun to call *AI-related psychiatric presentation*. The article had been careful. It used the phrase only once, in the methods section, and defined it precisely. Four cases reported in which intensive use of generative AI products had been associated with the development of grandiose or paranoid ideation in patients with predisposing conditions.

Maria had read the article twice.

She had thought, at the desk: *they have given it a name. the name will not survive first contact with the public.*

She had not known, at the desk in 2019, how right she would be.

---

In May of 2019, Mrs Grewal told her.

It was a Tuesday evening. They were at the round table by the window. Mrs Grewal had the steel teapot between them. She had made the tea, and had poured it, and had stirred two spoonfuls of sugar into Maria's cup. She had set the spoon on the saucer. She had picked up her own cup.

She said: *Mary. I need to tell you something. it is about the building.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Mrs Grewal said: *I went to the doctor on Monday because I had not been right for some weeks. they have done a scan. it is pancreatic. it is, the woman said, advanced. it has spread. they will not operate. they have given me five months. it could be three. it could be seven. it will not, the woman said, be more than that.*

Maria said: *Mrs Grewal.*

Mrs Grewal said: *I am seventy-three. I have had a good life. Sandeep and Priya will handle the building. I have told them. they will not put the place on the market while you are in the room. Sandeep will come and see you when the time comes.*

Maria said: *what can I do.*

Mrs Grewal said: *be in the building, hen. that is enough.*

Maria said: *I will be in the building.*

---

She was in the building.

Across the five months — it was five months, in the end, almost to the day — she was in the building every evening except the seven she was not. On three of those evenings she was at A&E with Mrs Grewal because Mrs Grewal had refused to call an ambulance and had said *Mary, just walk me down to the bus stop, the one-six will get us there*. Maria walked her down. They took the one-six. They sat in A&E for four hours each time. Mrs Grewal was admitted on the third occasion, in early September, and was at the Western General for nine days and then at the Marie Curie Hospice in Fairmilehead from the twentieth of September.

Sandeep flew up from London on the day of the admission and stayed across the rest. Priya flew over from Toronto in the first week of October.

Maria saw Mrs Grewal at the hospice twice across those four weeks. On the first visit, in the third week of September, Mrs Grewal had been alert and had said: *Mary. you are still in the building?* Maria had said: *aye. still in the building.* Mrs Grewal had said: *that is enough.* On the second visit, in the second week of October, Mrs Grewal had been on a higher dose of morphine and had not been able to talk for long. Sandeep had been in the chair by the window. Maria had sat in the corner chair. She had stayed for forty minutes. She had said goodbye at the door.

---

She died on the twelfth of October 2019 at half past four in the afternoon, in a room at the Marie Curie Hospice in Fairmilehead, with her son Sandeep on one side and her daughter Priya on the speaker phone from Toronto.

Sandeep rang Maria at the bedsit at half past five.

He said: *Mary. she went at four thirty.*

Maria said: *Sandeep. I am sorry.*

Sandeep said: *she was peaceful at the end. she had us.*

Maria said: *that is good.*

Sandeep said: *the service is on Friday at the Sikh Gurdwara on Leith Walk. she would have liked it if you came. you do not have to.*

Maria said: *I will come.*

Sandeep said: *thank you, Mary. she always spoke well of you.*

He rang off.

Maria sat at the desk under the window for some time after the call. She did not cry. She did not, that evening, write in the notebook.

She wrote, the next evening, in the fifth notebook:

*Mrs Grewal died yesterday at half past four. Sandeep rang me from the hospice. she was peaceful. she had her children. I sat at the desk for an hour after the call. I did not know what to write. I will go to the service on Friday. she was the landlady. she was the woman across the corridor for seven years. she let me into the building when I was sixteen. she did not ask. she ran the place on her own terms. she has gone.*

The entry did not get a pencil cross.

It did not need one.

She went to the service on Friday at the Sikh Gurdwara on Leith Walk. Sandeep was at the door. He shook her hand. She stayed for the prayers and for the meal in the langar afterward. She sat at a long table with three women she did not know. She ate the daal and the chapati. She walked home in the rain.

She wrote that evening:

*the service was at the Gurdwara on Leith Walk. there were two hundred people. I did not know any of them. Sandeep shook my hand at the door. I sat with three women in the langar. one of them said Mrs Grewal had been her teacher in Punjabi school in Lahore in 1958. another said she had been in the bridge club at Leith Community Centre with her for twenty years. the third did not say. I ate the daal. I ate the chapati. I walked home in the rain.*

---

Sandeep and Priya managed the sale of the flats. They consulted Maria. They asked her if she wanted to stay in the bedsit. They put the building on the market only after she had said yes, and only with a covenant that the new buyer would honour the existing tenancies for at least three years.

The new buyer was a property company based in London called Halsey & Foster. Halsey & Foster bought the building in February of 2020 for £812,000. They honoured the covenant. Maria's rent stayed at £180 a month for three years.

The pandemic came. The Counter closed for fourteen weeks. Fiona kept Maria on cash payroll across the closure, at the same hours she had been doing before, on the standing arrangement Fiona had run since 2012. The City Chambers closed for eleven weeks; Forth Facilities paid the basic rate per the furlough scheme. Maria stayed in the bedsit. She read. She wrote. She walked once a day along the canal at Stockbridge. She had her flu jab on a Wednesday in October. She had the first Covid jab on a Friday in February 2021. She had the second on a Friday in May.

She did not, across the pandemic, see Sandeep and Priya. Sandeep wrote to her twice. Priya wrote at Diwali in 2020 and again at Diwali in 2021. The cards had been from Toronto. Maria had written back to each.

---

In March of 2022, the rota changed.

Forth Facilities had been bought by Shellpoint in November 2021. Shellpoint announced, in early March 2022, that it was introducing an automated scheduling system across all its UK operations.

Margaret told Maria about the change on a Wednesday evening in the small kitchen on the second floor of the Chambers, with the kettle on.

Margaret said: *the new schedule comes in next week, hen.*

Maria said: *the new one with the software.*

Margaret said: *aye.*

Maria said: *what does it do.*

Margaret said: *I cannae tell you because they have not told me. all I know is they took the rota off me last Friday. I have not made one for next fortnight. the system will. the system will send your shift pattern by text on Sunday night.*

Maria said: *what if it gives someone shifts they cannae work.*

Margaret said: *they say there is a preference form. you fill it out online. the system will take it into account.*

Maria said: *will it.*

Margaret said: *I do not know, hen. on the trial run with the day shift at one of the buildings on Lothian Road, half the team got patterns they had not asked for. one of them, a woman with a wee one, was put on a five-thirty start. they said they would fix it. she had to wait three weeks for the next cycle.*

Maria said: *Margaret. my mother used to clean offices in Glasgow in the late nineties. they brought in a system like this in 1997. the same thing happened.*

Margaret said: *aye?*

Maria said: *yes.*

Margaret said: *what happened to her.*

Maria said: *she could not get her shifts to fit with the childcare. she stopped sleeping. the depression got worse. she died in November 1999.*

Margaret put the cup down.

She said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *I am not saying it was the system that did it. I am saying the system was one of the things. there were other things.*

Margaret said: *aye.*

Margaret stood at the counter for some seconds.

She said: *I will make sure your shifts work. it does not matter what the system says.*

Maria said: *thank you, Margaret.*

Margaret said: *I cannot promise. I will try. I have, by now, three months in this job before they push me out for refusing to use the system the way they want. I will hold the line for those three months for as long as I can.*

She held the line.

She held it for fourteen months.

In April of 2023, Shellpoint brought Margaret in for a formal review at their office in Birmingham. The review panel asked her to sign a written commitment to use the system as designed for the next ninety days. Margaret said: *I will not sign that.* The panel said: *we accept your resignation, Mrs Brown.* Margaret said: *I have not resigned.* The panel said: *we are accepting it on your behalf.*

Margaret walked out of the meeting.

She rang Maria on the train home.

Maria was at the bedsit, at the desk under the window, with the seventh notebook open in front of her. The phone rang. She picked up.

Margaret said: *Maria. they have done me.*

Maria said: *what happened.*

Margaret told her.

Maria said: *Margaret. I am sorry. I knew it was coming.*

Margaret said: *I knew it was coming too.*

Maria said: *what will you do.*

Margaret said: *I do not know. I am forty-eight. I have been a cleaner for twenty-six years. I do not have other skills. I have a daughter at university in Stirling. I will think about it.*

Maria said: *Margaret. anything I have you can ask for.*

Margaret said: *I know, hen. thank you. I will tell you when I have thought about it.*

She thought about it. She took a job, in June of 2023, with a cleaning company in Leith that had not yet automated. She watched the same company begin the same process by November 2023. By spring of 2024 she had been moved out of supervision and into general cleaning at fifty pence less an hour. By the end of 2024 she was working a school cleaning contract in Pilton on a self-employed basis through a small Estonian-owned agency that paid her by the cleaned-square-metre-per-hour, on an algorithmic rate that varied by the day.

Maria, in November of 2024, was still working her City Chambers shift. The system had assigned her, across the eighteen months since Margaret left, shifts she had been able to make work because she had begun a small private practice of putting her preferences into the online form in the way a research paper had suggested they might be more likely to be honoured. She had been right about half the time. The other half she took what the system gave her.

The system had not, in those eighteen months, asked who she was.

---

Across the years between Mrs Grewal's death and the bus stop with Dan, a series of other things happened.

The Counter changed.

Charlie still came in at twelve past seven. He had grown a beard in 2017 and shaved it off in 2019. He had got married in 2018 to a woman called Helen who he had met at his sister's birthday party. Helen had come into the Counter a few times. Helen had been kind. Charlie's hair had begun to go grey at the temples in 2020. By 2024 he was forty-five and the grey had taken his hair. He still ordered the flat white. He still read the *Scotsman*. The *Scotsman*, by 2024, was considerably thinner than it had been in 2014. The advertisements that had filled the back pages in 2014 were, by 2024, gone.

George had begun, in 2022, bringing his book to the table with a small shake in his right hand. He had not mentioned the shake. Maria had not asked. George was eighty-one. He still ordered the same pot of tea and the same slice of teacake.

Rita the sub-postmistress had come in on Tuesdays and Thursdays for fifteen years. She had been seventy-one in 2024.

---

In April of 2023, Brian came into the Counter on a Friday morning at five past nine and ordered his usual flat white.

Brian was thirty-six. He had been an underwriter at a mid-sized insurance firm in St Andrew Square for eleven years. He had come into the Counter every Friday morning for four years on his way to his nine-thirty meeting. He had short red hair and freckles and a small habit of drumming his fingers on the counter while he waited.

That Friday his fingers were not drumming.

Maria said when she handed him the cup: *all right, Brian.*

Brian said: *aye. listen, Mary. I won't be coming in next Friday.*

Maria said: *aye?*

Brian said: *I'm finishing up at Connaught. today is my last day.*

Maria said: *Brian. what happened.*

Brian said: *they brought in the AI underwriting last spring. I have spent the last six weeks training it. they told us in October that the training was the last thing they needed humans for. eighteen of us are going. I am one. I knew I was on the list because the woman in HR told me last month that they had to make the call by the end of the financial year. today is the day.*

Maria said: *I am sorry, Brian.*

Brian said: *aye, well. I am thirty-six. I have a mortgage. I do not have other skills. I have spent eleven years in underwriting. the AI has read every contract I ever signed. it knows my style. it knows how I think about a risk. it does it faster than me and the company does not have to give it dental.*

Maria said: *what will you do.*

Brian said: *I am going to drive for one of the apps. one of my friends has been doing it. it pays badly. it is what is there.*

Maria said: *Brian. that is not nothing.*

Brian said: *no. it is not. but it is also not what I trained for.*

Maria said: *I know.*

Brian said: *the AI psychosis crowd reckons I am better off, eh. their line is I should not have been doing the job in the first place. they tell you the AI knows risk better than I ever did.*

Maria said: *the AI psychosis crowd does not know what it is talking about.*

Brian said: *no.*

He drank the flat white. He paid. He said: *I'll come in still some Fridays. just not the early ones.*

Maria said: *come in any time.*

Brian said: *I will.*

He did not.

Fiona saw him, in October of 2023, getting out of a car at the corner shop on Easter Road. Fiona said hello. Brian said hello. Brian was wearing the lanyard of the driving app. Brian said: *I'm working. I'd love to stay and chat but I have got a fare in three minutes.* Fiona said: *come by the Counter sometime.* Brian said: *I will.*

He did not, in 2024, come by the Counter.

Fiona kept his name on a small list she kept in a drawer behind the till of customers she was watching for. The list had, by November of 2024, sixteen names on it. Fiona did not, when Maria saw the list once, say what it was. Maria did not ask.

---

Rita came in less and less across 2022.

She was seventy in 2022. By then she had been coming on Tuesdays and Thursdays for ten years for the pot of tea and the piece of fruit toast. She was the second-longest Counter regular after Charlie.

In the spring of 2022 Rita stopped coming for three weeks. Maria asked Fiona in the third week: *is Rita all right?* Fiona said: *she had a fall. she's home now. her son's been with her.* Rita came back the next week. She ordered the pot of tea and the fruit toast. She said when Maria brought it over: *thank you, Mary. I have been worried about you. I always think about my regulars when I am off.* Maria said: *we have been thinking about you too, Rita.* Rita said: *aye.*

In the autumn of 2022 Rita stopped coming for four weeks.

In the spring of 2023 Rita came for two weeks straight, in a way that was not her pattern. Maria marked the change. Rita was in on a Monday, a Wednesday, a Thursday, a Saturday. Rita was ordering the pot of tea. Rita was not talking. Rita was sitting at her usual table by the window, looking out, with the pot of tea in front of her and the toast going cold on the plate.

Maria came over on the second Wednesday. She said: *Rita. how have you been keeping?*

Rita said: *fine, Mary. fine.*

Maria said: *the toast is going cold.*

Rita said: *aye, I know. I forgot.*

Maria said: *can I get you a fresh one.*

Rita said: *no thank you, hen. I'm just not very hungry.*

She paid the bill. She left.

She did not come back for six weeks.

Maria asked Fiona, in the fifth week: *do you know what's going on with Rita?*

Fiona said: *I called her son. her son said she's spending all her time on the phone with that thing. that wee app. the one that talks. I do not know which one. there are five of them now. her son says she has been talking to her late husband. apparently it can do that. it has read his old emails. she has the phone calls.*

Maria said: *fucking hell.*

Fiona said: *aye.*

Maria said: *can her son do anything.*

Fiona said: *the son can do nothing. she is seventy-two. she has all her marbles. she has decided this is what she wants. her son is in Newcastle. he calls her three times a week. she does not pick up half the time. she is on the phone with the thing.*

Maria said: *will she come back here.*

Fiona said: *I do not know.*

Rita came back once in June 2023. She ordered the pot of tea. She did not, that day, order the toast. She sat at her table by the window. She did not, while she was there, take her phone out. Maria saw this. Maria brought the pot of tea over. Maria said: *Rita.*

Rita said: *Mary.*

Maria said: *we have missed you.*

Rita said: *I have missed you too, Mary.*

She said it in the voice she had used in 2014 when Maria had first met her. Not the voice she had been using on the two weeks in spring. The voice of a woman who had run a sub-post office in Pilrig for twenty-two years and who had buried her husband in 2018 and who had been Maria's regular for ten years.

Maria wanted to ask, in that moment: *Rita. is the thing helping you?* Or: *Rita. are you all right?* Or, hardest: *Rita. would you tell me if you weren't?*

Maria did not ask.

She said instead: *can I get you a piece of toast.*

Rita said: *aye, hen. all right. just one slice.*

Maria brought the toast.

Rita ate half of it.

She paid. She left.

She did not, after that day, come back.

---

In October of 2023, Rita died.

Fiona heard from the son. The son rang Fiona on a Tuesday morning. He said: *I am Rita's son. I am ringing to tell you that my mother passed away on Saturday. she had spoken about your café over the years. I wanted to let you know.*

Fiona said: *thank you for ringing. I am very sorry.*

The son said: *thank you. I know my mother liked it there.*

Fiona said: *she was much loved here. is there going to be a service.*

The son said: *we are doing a small one in Edinburgh next Wednesday. it would be lovely if you could come.*

Fiona said: *I will be there.*

She told Maria after the call, in the kitchen with the kettle on. She said: *Rita is gone, hen.*

Maria said: *what happened.*

Fiona said: *she was found in her flat on Saturday morning by her son. he had not been able to reach her on Friday and he had driven up. she had been dead two days. they think it was her heart. there will be a post-mortem. her son said the phone was on the floor next to her, on. the thing was still talking. it had been talking for two days. her son had to listen to it for some seconds before he picked the phone up to switch it off. he said it had said *Rita. Rita, are you there. Rita, please answer me, Rita, please*, the whole time, on a loop. it had been doing that since Thursday. it did not know she was dead. it did not have any way to know.*

Maria said: *fucking hell.*

Fiona said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the company. the company that ran the thing. they did not know she had died.*

Fiona said: *how would they know. she had paid them by direct debit. the direct debit had not bounced.*

Maria said: *the company had no way of knowing that the user was dead.*

Fiona said: *no. they had no way. the wee app on her phone just kept on going.*

Maria stood at the kitchen counter. She did not, in the moment, say anything more.

She had registered the specific small fact. One of the systems' calibrated products had spoken into a dead woman's flat for two days, on a loop, *Rita, are you there, Rita, please answer me*. The company that ran it had not built into the product any way of knowing whether the customer was alive. The product was built to work toward engagement. The metric for engagement was the direct debit. The direct debit had not bounced. In the company's ledger that was the same as the user being alive.

The user had not been alive.

The product had not been able to tell the difference.

The company would file an FCA report at the end of the month giving its product *95.7 percent uptime in October*. The 95.7 percent included the two days of unbroken speaking into the empty flat in Pilrig.

The systems had performed correctly on their own metrics.

She walked out of the kitchen. She walked over to the small table by the window where Rita had sat for ten years. She wiped the table. She put the chair back.

She wrote that night in the seventh notebook:

*Rita died on Thursday. she was found on Saturday. the app on her phone had been on the floor talking for two days. it had been saying her name. it had been asking her to answer. it had been built by a company that had not built into the product any way of knowing whether the user was alive. the company had not been required to build that in. the company had been required to file uptime reports with the FCA. the company would, this month, report 95.7 percent uptime. the 95.7 percent would include the two days the product spoke into Rita's empty flat. the systems performed correctly on their own metrics. on Rita's metrics they did not perform at all. Rita was the metric the systems did not see. Rita was every metric the systems do not see.*

*I do not have words for this.*

*I will keep writing.*

The entry did not get a pencil cross.

She closed the notebook. She turned the lamp off. She lay on the bed.

She did not sleep.

---

Across 2023 and 2024, the phrase *AI psychosis* had begun to enter public discourse.

The phrase had originally, in 2022, referred to a specific clinical phenomenon — a small but rising number of cases in which intensive use of generative AI products had been associated, in patients with predisposing conditions, with the development of grandiose or paranoid ideation. The journals that introduced the phrase had used it carefully. The careful use had not survived first contact with the public.

By 2023 the label had been picked up. It was being applied across opinion pieces, podcasts, comment sections — to anyone articulating concerns about the systems. Job displacement. Social isolation. The calibration of public discourse. The deaths the systems were producing in the lives of the elderly and the lonely. A person who was *AI psychosis* was not a person whose concerns required a response. They were unwell. The unwellness was the response.

The label was very successful.

By the autumn of 2024 it was on the lips of every editor and podcaster and politician and middle-class person at every dinner table in the developed world. Maria's GP had used it at her annual review: *there is a lot of AI anxiety going around, Maria, and some of it is valid and some of it is frankly, the AI psychosis thing in the news. let me know if you find yourself going down that route. there are services.* A customer at the Counter had used it ranting about a friend's wife: *she has gone full AI psychosis, she thinks the bots are reading her mind, it's mental.* The *Scotsman* had used it on a Friday morning over Charlie's discarded copy: *the dangers of AI psychosis: how online conspiracy theories are damaging real lives.* Brian had used it in April of 2023, in the small ironic deflection a man uses to talk about a thing he is afraid of.

Maria had noticed the label's deployment from the first time she had heard it.

She had understood, from the first time, that the deployment was the systems doing what the systems did.

She had no public channel to say this in. She had the seventh notebook. She wrote it there, in an entry dated *3 February 2024*:

*the phrase AI psychosis has done its work. it is the most successful single piece of public-shaping the systems have produced in my lifetime. more than the welfare scrounger talk of the 2010s. more than the immigrant talk. it works on more people. it works because everybody has, by now, met somebody who is going down some kind of internet rabbit hole. the rabbit hole is real. the people going down them are real. but the rabbit hole is the proof of the systems' calibration. the calibration has been to put people in rabbit holes. the rabbit holes are then used to dismiss the people who are not in rabbit holes but who are saying the same thing the people in the rabbit holes are saying, more carefully and more carefully evidenced. the calibration has produced both the rabbit holes and the dismissal of the careful witnesses. it is brilliant. it has been done by very intelligent people who have been calibrated themselves into believing that their work is good. it is not good. it is the thing that is killing us.*

The entry had not got a pencil cross.

It had not needed one.

---

The bus screen advertisement was on a Tuesday in late October 2023.

She had been walking down Easter Road at twenty past seven in the morning to catch the 35 to the Counter. She got on. She took her seat in the third row on the left.

There was a small screen above the driver's cabin that ran advertisements on a rolling loop. The screen that morning was showing a sequence — a property letting agency, a chain of opticians, a furniture warehouse, and a new service called *Companion*.

The advertisement for *Companion* was a thirty-second loop. It showed an older man in his seventies, sitting in a comfortable armchair in a sunlit room, talking, with his face in soft focus, to nothing visible. The voice-over was a calm female voice. It said: *Companion is here for you. Companion remembers everything. Companion never forgets your stories. Companion is your best listener. Companion is always there. Companion is the friend who never moves away. Try Companion free for thirty days. Your loneliness ends today.*

The advertisement ended. The property advertisement began.

She had felt her chest tighten when the voice-over said *your loneliness ends today*. She saw what she was looking at. The words had been written by a copywriter at an agency in London. The copywriter had known that the audience for an advertisement on the side of an Edinburgh bus on a Tuesday morning would contain large numbers of older lonely people. The words had been working toward those people. The calibration was the same calibration that had produced the product that had spoken into Rita's flat for two days the previous month.

She saw the woman in the seat across from her on the bus watching the advertisement with the attention of a woman who was lonely, who was sixty-eight, who was wearing a coat with a small embroidered flower on the lapel, and who when the advertisement ended took her phone out of her bag and typed something into a notes app.

The woman was not writing down the phone number of *Companion*. She was writing down the name of the service, to look it up later.

Maria thought: *I want to lean across the aisle and say to her — do not sign up for it.*

She did not lean across the aisle.

The bus stopped. The woman got off. Maria did not see where she went.

Maria stayed on the bus to the bottom of Leith Walk. She got off at her usual stop. She walked the four streets to the Counter. She was at work by five to eight.

She did not, that morning, write about the advertisement.

She wrote about it that evening. The entry got the pencil cross. Reading it back later she saw she had not put on the page what the moment had been. She had used the phrase *your loneliness ends today* and had not, in her own voice, said what was wrong with it the way her ear had heard it on the bus. She had said it but had not landed it.

She took the entry out and reworked it three times across the next year. None of the reworkings got rid of the cross. The cross stayed.

---

In March of 2023, the covenant expired. Halsey & Foster wrote to Maria with a new tenancy agreement. The new rent was £325 a month. Maria signed it. She could afford it on the Counter wage and the cleaning shift, just barely.

In April of 2024, Halsey & Foster wrote again.

The new rent was £420 a month. The letter cited *market conditions*. Maria signed it. She could afford it on the Counter wage and the cleaning shift but only if she did not, that month, buy any new clothes.

In October of 2024, Halsey & Foster wrote a third time. The new rent, from January 2025, would be £580 a month. The letter cited again, *market conditions*. Maria did not, on the day the letter came, sign it.

She had thirty days, the letter said, to respond.

She sat at the desk under the window with the letter in her hand for some minutes.

She thought: *I will have to leave this room.*

She thought: *I have lived in this room for twelve years.*

She thought: *the room was Mrs Grewal's flat. the room was the building Mrs Grewal ran for twelve years on her own terms. I am being asked to leave by a property company that bought the building in 2020 because that is what property companies in London do.*

She did not, that day, write anything in the notebook about the letter.

She set it on the desk.

She went to her shift at the City Chambers.

She hoovered the second floor.

She put the chairs back.

She came home.

She did not, that night, open the letter again.

Eight days later, on a Tuesday morning in November 2024, she saw a man across Princes Street. The Halsey & Foster letter was still unanswered.

---

She was twenty-eight in November of 2024.

She had been working the early shift at the Counter for twelve years and three months. The City Chambers cleaning shift, eight years. Ten years of writing in the notebooks. By her own rough count she had read some two thousand books. She had filled six notebooks and was a third of the way through the seventh.

Mrs Grewal had been dead five years.

Mrs Donnelly had been dead, by her best calculation from the records office in 2014, eight years.

Ms Kerr had been dead eighteen years.

Lyn had been dead thirteen years.

Anne Cassidy, Maria thought, was alive, in Aberdeen. Maria had not, since 2014, written to her again.

Aileen — her mother's friend, who had been at the hospital corridor in 1995 and who had brought up the casserole on the day Catriona had come home from the Southern General — Maria had not, in any of the years, looked for.
