She had thought about it twice. She had decided, both times, that she did not have the energy to risk a second *deceased*. Aileen would have been in 2024 fifty-three. There had been no way to tell, from Edinburgh in 2024, whether Aileen was alive.

Brian, the underwriter, had by November 2024, been driving for the app for twenty months.

Margaret, the cleaning supervisor, had by November 2024, been working the school cleaning contract in Pilton for six months.

Charlie was forty-five. He still came in.

George was eighty-one. He still came in. He had, since 2022, been bringing his book to the table with a small shake in his right hand. He had not mentioned the shake. Maria had not asked.

Donald, the man at the Cowgate, retired in 2019. Maria saw him once, in 2021, at the chemist on Easter Road. He was buying a prescription. He said: *Mary. I thought of you sometimes.* Maria said: *I thought of you too, Donald.* He said: *are you still in the building.* Maria said: *aye. still here.* He said: *good.* He paid for the prescription. He walked out. She had not seen him since.

The framework was, by November of 2024, the framework. It was in seven notebooks. It was in Maria's head. It was not in any other place.

She had not, in any of the ten years, shown it to anyone.

Not shown to Fiona. Fiona had her own work. Not to Mrs Grewal, who had been dying. Not to Margaret, who had been losing her job. The central library was where she went to read; showing it would have changed the reading. Not to the chatbots either. By 2018 she had learned what the chatbots did with anything you showed them.

The notebooks were on the shelf above the desk in seven small cardboard boxes Mrs Grewal had given her in 2018 from a delivery of glassware. The seventh box was the one with the open notebook in it. Maria had labelled the boxes in her own deliberate handwriting on a piece of card on the front of each — *2009-2011 (1)*, *2012-2013 (2)*, *2013-2015 (3)*, *2015-2017 (4)*, *2017-2019 (5)*, *2019-2022 (6)*, *2022- (7)*.

It had been hers. It had been hers and it had been silent and it had been, in the quiet private way she had been carrying it since the records office in 2014, what her work was. The work was below the systems' threshold. She had thought it would stay there until she died.

She was wrong by eight days.

---

The morning of the eleventh of November 2024 was a Tuesday.

She got up at half past six. The bedsit was cold. The heating had, since Halsey & Foster had taken over in 2020, come on at seven instead of six. She put on her jumper and her jeans and her socks and her boots. She went down to the shared kitchen and made tea on the gas hob. She brought the cup back into her room. She sat at the desk for ten minutes. She drank the tea. She did not write.

She put on her coat. She took the seventh notebook off the desk. She put it in the inside pocket of her coat. She put her wallet in the other pocket. She put on her scarf. She locked the door. She went down the close stairs.

On the second-floor landing she stopped.

Mrs Grewal's flat had been let, since 2021, to a young couple from Aberdeen who were quiet and kept to themselves and who Maria had spoken to four times in three years. The brass nameplate by the door had been replaced. The plate said *Robertson*. She had, since the replacement, generally walked past without looking. That morning she looked.

She thought: *Mrs Grewal has been gone five years.*

She thought: *I would have liked another twenty.*

She thought: *I will be late if I stand here.*

She went down the stairs.

She came out onto Easter Road. The morning was cold and clear. The first frost of the autumn was on the windscreens of the cars parked along the road. She walked to the bus stop opposite the Hibs ground. The 35 came in three minutes. She got on. She took her seat in the third row on the left. The bus went down Easter Road, across London Road, down Leith Street, past the bus station, onto Princes Street.

She had to change buses on Princes Street that morning because there was an engineering closure on the lower part of Leith Street that meant the 35 would, that day, terminate at the Mound. She got off at the stop on Princes Street opposite the Mound. She crossed to the south side to catch the connecting service.

She stood at the bus stop with her hands in her coat pockets.

She looked across the road at the gardens. There was a man walking a small white dog along the path. There was a woman with a pushchair coming up the steps from the lower path.

She turned and looked at the small electronic display.

The next 22 was in seven minutes.

She turned and looked at the other bus stop, on the north side, where she had been standing two minutes earlier, and where, as she looked, a man came up to the stop with a folded newspaper under his arm. He stopped. Looked at the small electronic display. Looked across the road at her, and for the second of a second, held her eye, and looked away.

He was thirty-one.

He was in a dark wool coat and a navy scarf.

He had brown hair beginning to recede a little at the temples. He had glasses with thin metal frames.

The display again, briefly.

He took out his phone, looked at it for some seconds, put it back in his pocket.

Across the road at her again.

This time the look was longer. Not a man looking at a woman in the way Maria had been watching men look at women since she was four. A man looking at a woman whose face he had, in some way, seen before. Confused. The look of a person trying to remember.

Maria did not recognise him.

She did not, in the moment, hold the look.

She turned her face away. She looked at the display.

*The bus is in five minutes.*

*Who was that.*

She did not turn back.

She looked at the pavement. *My boots have got dust on them again. I will have to polish them tonight.*

In a part of her head she did not name: *I have not been looked at like that in my adult life.*

The bus came in five minutes.

She got on.

She did not know, on the bus, that the man on the other side of the road had been Dan Whitelaw. He had been on his way to a meeting at the Royal Society of Edinburgh on George Street and had got off the bus a stop early because he was half an hour ahead of his time and had wanted to walk the rest. Thirty-two. A research economist at the University of Edinburgh for six years. In 2017, at twenty-five, he had read every word of the published work of the Princeton economist Anne Case on what Case had called *deaths of despair* among middle-aged Americans. Since 2017 he had been looking for the British equivalent. Finding, in the data, the early traces of what he would later understand as the same operation working at a different level. He was on the agenda at the Royal Society that afternoon to give a fifteen-minute presentation on his findings. The presentation would, that afternoon, be received by the panel as *interesting but speculative* and dismissed.

He had recognised her, in the second their eyes met, as someone whose face was new but who was the kind of person he had been waiting for some years to see.

Maria did not know any of this on the bus.

She did not know any of this on the bus.

She found out eight days later, in a café on Leith Walk three doors down from the Counter, when he walked in at twenty past five in the afternoon for a coffee, saw her at a small table in the corner with the seventh notebook open in front of her and a flat white going cold beside it, stood at the counter for some seconds, and walked over.

But that was eight days away.

On the morning of the eleventh of November 2024, on the 22 bus going down to the Counter, Maria sat in her usual seat and watched the gardens go past. She thought briefly about the man on the other side of the road. She thought more briefly about the rent letter on her desk that she had not, in eight days, signed. She thought longest about Mrs Grewal, who had been dead for five years and one month and twenty-eight days. She got off at her stop. She walked the four streets to the Counter. She put the apron on. She opened up.

The wall, she thought going about her morning, was the wall.

It had been the wall when she was four. Still the wall at twelve. At eighteen and twenty-two and twenty-eight. The wall did not stop being the wall.

Her job, she thought, putting on the apron, was not to make the wall stop.

Her job was to read aloud to the next person.

She had been doing that since she was fifteen. She did it.

She was twenty-eight. She was at work. The first customer was due in seven minutes.

Charlie came in at twelve past seven. Maria handed him the flat white.

Charlie said: *morning, Mary.*

Maria said: *morning, Charlie.*

The bell above the door rang as he sat down by the window with the *Scotsman*.

The morning went the way the morning went.

---


### Chapter Six: The Café

*Edinburgh, October–December 2025*

The eight days between the bus stop and the café were eight days like the days before them.

Maria worked the early shift at the Counter Tuesday to Saturday. She did the cleaning shift at the City Chambers on the Wednesday and the Saturday. She walked to the central library on the Monday morning and the Friday afternoon. She wrote in the seventh notebook in the evenings.

She did not, in those eight days, think specifically about the man at the bus stop.

She thought about him three times. On the Wednesday morning when she crossed Princes Street again at the same lights, and felt her pulse quicken. On the Friday afternoon when she saw a man in a similar dark wool coat on the other side of George IV Bridge and turned to look and saw it was someone else. And on the Sunday evening, lying in bed before sleep, when she caught herself thinking about him without having intended to.

She had not, on any of the three occasions, let herself dwell.

There was no time in her life for dwelling.

---

On the Tuesday evening she finished her shift at the Counter at half past four. Fiona was at the till. The lunch person, Hannah, had taken over an hour earlier. Maria untied the apron and hung it on the hook by the back door and put on her coat.

She said: *see you tomorrow.*

Fiona said: *aye, hen. see you tomorrow.*

Maria walked out of the Counter. The bell above the door rang. The afternoon was getting on towards dusk. The lights along Leith Walk were starting to come on. There was the smell of frying onions from the kebab shop two doors down.

She did not, on most evenings, go to a different café before her cleaning shift. She generally went home, made tea, sat at the desk under the window for forty minutes, and then walked to the bus stop for the 35. That evening, for reasons she did not, in the moment, examine, she did not want to go home. She wanted to sit somewhere else for an hour. She wanted to write in the notebook somewhere that was not the bedsit and was not the Counter.

She walked three doors down Leith Walk and went into a café called Hart & Stone.

Hart & Stone was a café Fiona had told her about the previous summer. It was run by a woman called Eilidh and her partner Karim. It had been there for two years. It served pour-over coffee and sourdough toast and a small range of pastries from a bakery in Stockbridge. It was quieter than the Counter. It had four tables.

Maria sat at the table in the back corner.

Eilidh was at the counter. Eilidh was in her thirties, with dark hair tied back, in a denim apron. She nodded at Maria when Maria came in. She said: *flat white?*

Maria said: *yes please.*

Eilidh said: *take a seat.*

Maria took the seat in the corner. She put her coat over the back of the chair. She got the seventh notebook out of the inside pocket and set it on the table. She got her pen out of her bag.

Eilidh brought the flat white over a few minutes later.

She said: *anything else.*

Maria said: *not for now, thank you.*

Eilidh said: *all right.*

She went back to the counter.

Maria opened the notebook to the page she had been on the previous evening. The page had two paragraphs on it, in her small even handwriting. The two paragraphs were the start of an entry about Margaret.

She had written four hundred words the previous evening and put the pen down because she could not find the word the entry needed. The word was for what Margaret did in the kitchen at the City Chambers on the Wednesday in March 2022 when she said *I will make sure your shifts work*. The word Maria was looking for would land the specific fact: Margaret did what Margaret did not because she liked Maria — she did like Maria, but the liking was not the reason — but because she understood, on her own terms, that the system was the kind of thing a person had to refuse on principle, and that the refusing was the only thing left to do.

She could not find the word.

She was looking for it again that evening.

She wrote, slowly: *Margaret had not been a political person. she was a Lothian working woman who had been a cleaner for twenty-six years and who had a daughter at university and who had voted Labour by default and not thought much about it. she had not, before March of 2022, said to herself that the system was a thing to be refused. the saying had been the work of the kettle being on and the cup of tea being made and the woman across from her being told that the same thing had killed her mother. Margaret took twelve seconds. she said: I will make sure your shifts work.*

The cup of tea was the word she had been looking for.

She closed the notebook.

She had been there for thirty-five minutes. The flat white had gone cold. She had not, in the thirty-five minutes, looked up from the notebook except once when Eilidh had walked past with a tray.

She looked up to take a drink of the cold flat white.

The bell above the door rang.

A man came in.

He was thirty-one, in a dark wool coat, with a navy scarf folded twice round his neck, and glasses with thin metal frames. He had brown hair that was beginning to recede a little at the temples. He had a folded newspaper under his arm. He walked up to the counter. He said: *flat white, please.*

Eilidh said: *aye, take a seat.*

He turned. He looked round the café. The café had four tables. Three of them were empty. Maria was, at the fourth, in the corner, with the notebook closed on the table in front of her and her hand round the cold cup.

He saw her.

He did not recognise her in the first second. He took her in the way a person takes in another person in a room. Then his face changed. The change was small. He had recognised her. This was the woman he had seen at the bus stop on Princes Street eight days previously.

He looked away.

He took a seat at the table by the window. He set the newspaper down. He took his coat off. He took out his phone. He looked at the phone. He put the phone down.

He looked across at Maria.

This time he held the look. He held it for two seconds. Then he stood up.

He walked across the small café to her table.

He stopped three feet from the table. He did not, in the three feet, come closer. He put his hands in the pockets of his trousers. He took them out.

He said: *I am sorry to bother you. I saw you at the bus stop on Princes Street eight days ago. I was on the other side of the road. I thought you were someone I knew. I — looking again, I do not think you are someone I knew. I am still sorry.*

Maria looked up at him.

She had, in the first second, read him. The watchfulness she had been carrying since she was four had told her: *not threatening*. It had also told her something it did not often tell her, which was: *unusually careful*. The two did not always come together in a man approaching her in a café.

She said: *who did you think I was.*

He said: *a woman called Sarah Buchanan. she was at primary school with my older sister. I have not seen her in twenty-five years. she's roughly your age now. she had a face that, at ten, looked like your face. that is the only reason I held the look on the eleventh. I am sorry.*

Maria said: *that is a careful explanation.*

He said: *I have been thinking, since the eleventh, about how to explain it if I saw you again.*

Maria said: *you thought you might see me again.*

He said: *I had not thought, no — not specifically. I had noticed I held the look. I had noticed I'd thought about it more than once after. and I had decided that if I did see you again I'd say so. so as not to leave you wondering. I did not come over for the look itself. I came over to apologise for it.*

Maria said: *you did not need to apologise.*

He said: *I did. to me.*

Maria said: *all right.*

He said: *thank you for letting me say it. I will leave you alone now.*

He turned to go back to his table.

Maria said: *what do you do for a living.*

He stopped. He turned back.

He said: *I am an economist. I work at the University of Edinburgh.*

Maria said: *what kind of economics.*

He said: *labour markets. specifically, I work on the social epidemiology of unemployment. I am interested in what happens to people when they lose their jobs. I am working on a book, or what I would call a book, on what is happening in the British labour market at the moment.*

Maria said: *what is happening.*

He said: *it depends on who you ask.*

Maria said: *I am asking you.*

He said, after a small pause: *an economic transition is underway that is qualitatively different from any transition we have records for. it is being managed at the level of public discourse as a normal transition. same vocabulary as the previous ones — automation, displacement, retraining, structural unemployment. that vocabulary does not fit. it fits a different shape. the shape is — harder to name. I have been trying for seven years.*

Maria said: *what is the shape.*

He said: *a labour market that is being substituted out from under the people in it, at a rate that is faster than the rate at which the people can adjust, with no compensating economy emerging to absorb them.*

Maria looked at him.

She said: *sit down.*

He said: *I do not want to interrupt your work.*

Maria said: *sit down.*

He sat down.

He took the chair opposite her at the small table.

Eilidh came over a minute later with his flat white. She set it on the table. She looked at the two of them. She said to Maria: *another one for you?*

Maria said: *yes please.*

Eilidh said: *all right.*

She went back to the counter.

He said: *thank you for letting me sit down.*

Maria said: *what is your name.*

He said: *Dan Whitelaw.*

Maria said: *Mary Donnelly.*

She said it without thinking. She had used the name Mary Donnelly with anyone who was not Fiona or her bank or the passport office or HMRC for twelve years. The name had been the name she gave at first contact. She had become a person who answered to it at the Counter and on the cleaning shift and at the Cowgate when she had been there.

She heard herself say it.

She said: *that is the name I use. it is not my actual name. my actual name is Maria Smith. you can use either. the name I use is the one I gave you.*

Dan said: *which would you prefer.*

Maria said: *Maria.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

He did not ask why there were two names. He did not, in any of the moments after she had told him, return to the topic.

She marked the not-returning.

She said: *what was your book going to be called.*

Dan said: *I do not have a title yet. the working title in my notes is *Falling Out*. I am not sure it is the right title. I have been thinking about it.*

Maria said: *what is in it.*

Dan said: *the data I have been collecting on the British labour market across the previous seven years. the data has not lined up with what the public conversation about the labour market has been saying. it has been telling a story I could not get the public conversation to see. I went today to a meeting at the Royal Society of Edinburgh on George Street to present some of it. the panel told me at the end that they found my work interesting but speculative. I came down Leith Walk to walk it off. I came in here for a coffee. you were sitting at this table.*

Maria said: *what was the data.*

Dan said: *the headline numbers. the official unemployment rate has been steady at about four percent for the past decade. the official measure does not count people who have stopped looking. it does not count involuntary part-time. it does not count gig work paid below minimum. it does not count people self-employed in what is really wage labour, at less than wage labour pays. adjust for those, and you are closer to seventeen percent. on my data it has gone from about eleven percent in 2016 to about seventeen percent in 2024. it is accelerating. the acceleration matches, almost exactly, the rollout of generative AI tools across white-collar work from 2022.*

Maria said: *I work with a man called Brian. he was an underwriter at an insurance firm in St Andrew Square for eleven years. he was made redundant in April of 2023 because the firm had brought in AI underwriting and he had spent the last six weeks of his employment training it. he is now driving for one of the apps. he has not been into the café since.*

Dan said: *Brian is one of seventeen percent.*

Maria said: *Brian is one of seventeen percent.*

Dan said: *I am sorry about Brian.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

She picked up her cup. She drank what was left of the flat white that had gone cold an hour before. She set it down.

She said: *Dan. tell me what you came to think on the way down Leith Walk.*

Dan said: *I do not know if it is what you are wanting to hear.*

Maria said: *tell me anyway.*

Dan said: *what I came to think on the way down. the panel told me today, in different words, what they have been telling me for four years. that my work is alarming. and that they cannae, in their official capacity, dignify alarming work as serious work. that has been the wrong way round for four years. since 2020 I have been putting together work that is not alarmist. the conclusions of it are alarming. they are alarming because the world is alarming. the panel has not been able to hear the difference. they cannot. their job is to not hear it. I came to think today that the not-hearing is not an accident. the discipline has been built to do it. the discipline has been built to call work like mine alarmist so that it does not have to say what the data says. the data says that something is dying. the discipline is not allowed to say that.*

Maria said: *the discipline is not allowed to say that.*

Dan said: *that is what I came to think on the way down Leith Walk.*

Maria said: *I think you are right.*

Dan said: *thank you.*

Maria said: *I have been watching the deflection from a different angle.*

Dan said: *which angle.*

Maria said: *I have been watching the labels. *AI psychosis*. *speculative*. *alarmist*. *anti-tech*. *Luddite*. each one of them has been working at the same job, which is to put the careful witnesses in a bucket that the discipline can dismiss in the same sentence as the bucket of the rabbit-hole people. the rabbit-hole people are real. the calibration has built them. the careful witnesses are also real. the calibration has built them too, by giving them rabbit-hole people to be lumped in with. the dismissal is the operation. I have been watching it for some years.*

Dan looked at her.

He said: *you have thought about this.*

Maria said: *for some years.*

Dan said: *what do you do for a living.*

Maria said: *I work at the Counter, three doors up. I do a cleaning shift at the City Chambers two evenings a week. that is what I do for a living.*

Dan said: *what do you do that is not for a living.*

Maria said: *I read. I write. I have been doing it since I was eighteen.*

Dan said: *what do you write.*

Maria looked down at the notebook on the table.

She said: *I write what I have noticed.*

Dan said: *across what topic.*

Maria said: *across the same topic you have been writing about. across what the systems have been doing to the people they have been doing it to.*

Dan did not say anything for some seconds.

Eilidh came over with Maria's second flat white. She set it on the table. She looked at the two of them again. She did not, this time, ask if they wanted anything else. She went back to the counter.

Dan said quietly: *can I read what you have written.*

Maria said: *no.*

Dan said: *all right.*

Maria said: *not yet.*

Dan said: *I understand.*

He waited.

Maria said: *give me your phone number.*

Dan said: *all right.*

He gave her his phone number. She wrote it down on the inside back cover of the seventh notebook, in pencil. She wrote, beside the number, *Dan Whitelaw, economist, Edinburgh*.

She gave him her phone number.

He wrote it down on the inside front cover of a small black notebook he took out of the inner pocket of his coat. He wrote: *Maria. café Leith Walk. 19 Nov 2024.*

He said: *what would you like the next thing to be.*

Maria said: *the next thing is, I have to go to my cleaning shift in twenty minutes. the next thing after that is, I will think about whether I want to talk to you again. I will text you in the next two days. it might be a yes. it might be a no. either way I will text you.*

Dan said: *all right.*

Maria said: *I am not, in any of this, doing what most people in Edinburgh would call a normal thing. I am sitting in a café with a man who walked over to me twenty minutes ago. I have given him my phone number. that is not how I have ever done anything in my adult life. you understand.*

Dan said: *I understand. I am also doing something that is not, for me, normal. I have not, in my adult life, sat down at a stranger's table on the basis of a held look at a bus stop eight days ago.*

Maria said: *I noticed.*

Dan said: *thank you for noticing.*

Maria said: *I will text you.*

Dan said: *I will wait.*

She put the notebook away. She put on her coat. She paid for the two flat whites at the counter. Eilidh said: *all right then, hen.* Maria said: *thank you, Eilidh.* Eilidh said: *come back any time.*

Maria walked out of the café.

She walked the four streets to the bus stop on Leith Walk.

She did not, on the walk, see what she was passing.

She got on the 16 to the City Chambers. She sat in her usual seat in the third row on the left. She opened the notebook on her lap.

She wrote:

*a man came into the café. he was the man from the bus stop on the eleventh. he sat down at my table after a careful explanation. he is an economist at the University of Edinburgh. his name is Dan Whitelaw. he has been working on a book. the book is on the same thing I have been writing about for ten years. he came to it from the data. I have come to it from the body. we are looking at the same thing from two sides. I have not, in the conversation, told him what I have written. I have told him, instead, that I will text him. I am going to think about it tonight.*

The entry did not get a pencil cross.

---

She did not text him for two days.

She thought about it on the cleaning shift. On the walk home at half past ten. Lying in bed that night. The notebook stayed shut on the Wednesday morning. She thought about it again at the Counter that day. Charlie came in at twelve past seven.

He said: *morning, Mary, you are looking thoughtful.*

Maria said: *aye, well. things on my mind.*

Charlie said: *aye. that's the way of it.*

On the walk home from the Counter on the Wednesday at twelve, the same thoughts. The Thursday morning, the same. She wrote, on the Thursday afternoon, in the seventh notebook:

*for two days I have been turning over what I read in Dan Whitelaw and I keep coming back to the same place: he is the first person in my adult life who has been on the same page as me on the actual question. he came to it differently. he is from a different background. he is educated. he has the discipline behind him. he is the kind of careful witness they have been working toward. he is also careful in himself, far as I can tell. I have spent sixteen years watching men. on first acquaintance he reads cleaner than any man I have known except Mr Henderson, the supervisor at my mother's cleaning company. and Mr Henderson failed her because the system was bigger than him. Dan Whitelaw will, in time, also fail. he will fail because the system is bigger than him. but the failure is some way off. there is, in the meantime, a thing to do.*

*the thing to do is to text him.*

She texted him on the Thursday afternoon. She wrote: *this is Maria from the café. I will meet you again if you are willing. I can do Saturday afternoon at three at the same café if you are free. yes or no will do.*

He texted back forty seconds later. He wrote: *yes. three on Saturday. thank you.*

She wrote: *good.*

She did not, after that, text him until the Saturday.

---

He was at the café at five to three.

She was at the café at three. He had taken the same table by the window. She came in. The bell rang. He stood up. She walked over.

He said: *hello.*

She said: *hello.*

She sat down opposite him.

He said: *thank you for coming.*

She said: *don't thank me. let's see how this goes.*

Eilidh came over. She said: *the same?*

Maria said: *yes.*

Dan said: *yes.*

Eilidh went to the counter.

Maria said: *I want to know about your work. start at the beginning.*

Dan started at the beginning.

He had grown up in Edinburgh. He had been a quiet child. He had read a lot. His father, Hugh, had been an architect, now retired. His mother, Elspeth, had been a GP in Morningside, also retired. His grandmother on his father's side, Margaret, had been a GP before her, and was now in a care home in Morningside at the age of ninety-four. He had two sisters — Catherine, who was three years older than him, and Eleanor, who was five years younger. Catherine had a small consultancy in town. Eleanor was an arts administrator who had moved to London the previous year. He had read economics at Cambridge, and had done his doctorate at the LSE, and had come back to Edinburgh as a postdoc in 2018. He had been at the University of Edinburgh ever since. He was thirty-two. He was unmarried. He lived in a flat in Marchmont that he had bought in 2021 with help from his parents. He had been single for two and a half years.

Maria listened.

She did not, in the listening, take notes.

She listened the way she had listened to Lyn at fifteen on the corner of George Square. The way she had listened to Mrs Grewal across the table on the night the diagnosis had come. The way she had been listening since she was four months old.

The listening was the work.

The listening had been her work all her life.

When he had finished he said: *I have given you a CV. I am sorry. I do not know how to tell you about myself except as a CV.*

Maria said: *that was not a CV. you mentioned your grandmother.*

Dan said: *aye. she was important.*

Maria said: *tell me about her.*

Dan said: *country GP in the Borders for thirty years before she came to Edinburgh in 1982. she buried fifteen hundred patients across her career. she carried a small private record with her of who they had been. when I was a boy she would take me to the grocer's in Stockbridge, and on the walk she would name people we passed. that's Mrs Anderson. she lost her son in 1979. she has not since then been all right. that's Iain Petrie. I delivered his daughter in 1971 in a snowstorm. she is a doctor in Ottawa now. that's the man whose wife I could not save. I am sorry every time I see him. she walked through the street holding the small private record of every person she passed. she taught me, walking with me at six and at eight and at ten, that this was what a careful person did. you held the small record. you carried it. you did not, in any public channel, bring it out. you kept it. she is ninety-four. she is in a care home in Morningside. dementia care since 2023. she has lucid windows now and again. when she is in one she remembers me. I see her every Sunday. they say she is in her last year. I am holding what I can while she is here.*

Maria said: *what was her name.*

Dan said: *Margaret Whitelaw.*

Maria said: *Margaret.*

Dan said: *yes.*

Maria said: *that is a very good name.*

Dan said: *aye. she is a very good woman.*

Maria sat for some seconds.

She said: *Dan. I have been writing in notebooks for ten years. I have seven of them. I have not, in those ten years, shown them to anyone. I am going to consider, across the next some weeks, whether to show them to you. I am not promising. I am telling you the consideration is happening. it would be unfair not to tell you.*

Dan said: *I am grateful for the telling.*

Maria said: *don't be grateful yet. I have not done it.*

Dan said: *I am grateful for the consideration.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Eilidh brought the flat whites. They drank.

Maria said: *now tell me about your sister Catherine. you mentioned her without saying anything.*

Dan said: *Catherine is —*

He stopped.

He said: *Catherine is the person in my family who has been most affected by what I have been writing about.*

Maria said: *go on.*

Dan said: *Catherine has a small consultancy. she had four employees. across the last eighteen months, two of them have been let go because the kind of work the consultancy does — small-firm operational reviews — has been encroached on by AI tools that small firms can use directly, at one fortieth the cost. Catherine has had to lay off two women she had hired in 2019 and 2021. one of them, called Suzy, had a small daughter. Catherine had been at Suzy's wedding. Catherine had to sit Suzy down on a Thursday afternoon in her office in 2023 and tell her that the consultancy could not afford her. Suzy cried. Catherine cried. Catherine's husband, Robert, told me, the next time I was at theirs for dinner, that Catherine has not been the same since. Catherine has since then been on antidepressants. Catherine has, in the last six months, been talking about closing the consultancy. she is forty-five. she does not know what she would do. that is what is happening to my sister.*

Maria said: *Suzy.*

Dan said: *Suzy Wallace. I have her on my list of names.*

Maria said: *you keep a list of names.*

Dan said: *I have a list of two hundred and forty names. I have been keeping it since 2017. it is the list of people who have been pushed out of work, into work that does not match their training, by the kind of operation I am writing about. I do not, in the public version of the work, name any of them. they are all in a private file at the back of the manuscript. they are the people I am writing it for.*

Maria said: *Fiona at the Counter keeps a list as well.*

Dan said: *of?*

Maria said: *of customers she watches for. I have not asked her what it means. I think it means the same thing. she had sixteen names on the list as of two weeks ago. I do not know if Brian is on the list.*

Dan said: *I am sure he is.*

Maria said: *I am sure too.*

They sat for some seconds.

Dan said: *Maria. can I ask you a question.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *how did you come to be writing what you have been writing.*

Maria said: *I came to be writing it because I have been on the receiving end of these systems my whole life. I was raised in Govan. my mother died when I was four. I was passed through nine houses. I lived on the streets in Glasgow from twelve to sixteen. I came to Edinburgh at sixteen and have been here since. I have known what these systems are doing to people because they have been doing it to people I have known the whole time. I knew Lyn. I knew Eddie. I knew Ms Kerr. I knew Mrs Donnelly. I knew Mrs Grewal. I knew Brian. they are all dead, except Brian. some of them died of the systems directly. some of them died of conditions the systems produced. one of them died of a company-owned product that talked into her flat on a loop for two days after she had died because the company had not bothered to teach the product to know that she had died. that was Rita. she used to come into the Counter on Tuesdays and Thursdays for ten years. she died last October. I have been writing about it.*

Dan said: *I have not heard about that case.*

Maria said: *no. it was not in the news. her son did not go to the press. there have been others. they have not been in the news. the systems do not let those deaths be news.*

Dan said: *I would like, when you are ready, to know what you have written about Rita.*

Maria said: *aye. I will tell you when I am ready.*

Dan said: *thank you.*

He looked at the cup in his hands.

He said: *Maria. I want to say one more thing.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *Maria. I have been sitting in rooms with people on this topic for seven years. I have sat opposite professors. policymakers. the man who runs the IFS. none of them have said, in any conversation I have had with them, what you have said in the last fifteen minutes. you have said it without any of the apparatus they have. you have said it because you have lived inside it. I do not know how to tell you this without it sounding like flattery. I am not flattering you. I am telling you I am very glad to be here.*

Maria said: *all right.*

She did not say anything else.

She drank the flat white.

She said, after some seconds: *Dan. I am more careful than you. I am more suspicious. I have lived in worlds where men who were glad to be opposite me for some good reason have, after some time, turned out to have been glad for a different reason. I have not seen any such second reason in you yet. I am going to watch for it. I want to be clear about this. I am watching for it.*

Dan said: *I understand. I would, if I were you, do the same.*

Maria said: *good.*

She picked up the cup. The cup was empty.

She said: *I am going to go now.*

Dan said: *all right.*

She stood up. She put on her coat.

She said: *I will text you.*

Dan said: *I will wait.*

She walked out of the café.

---

She walked back to Easter Road.

The afternoon had gone over to evening. The streetlights were on. She walked up Leith Walk to the bottom of London Road and along London Road to Easter Road and up Easter Road to the close. She let herself in. She climbed the three flights.

She let herself into her own room.

She sat on the bed without taking her coat off. She sat for some time.

She thought: *I have not let a man across that threshold since I was twelve years old.*

She did not, for a long time, write in the notebook.

She lay down on her side on the bed in her coat. She watched the light go down across the wall.

She thought, watching the light: *I have, since I was twelve, been a person who has not let anyone in. the not-letting-in has been the most reliable single decision I have ever made about anything. the not-letting-in has kept me alive. I am twenty-eight. I have been not-letting-in for sixteen years. the not-letting-in is the thing I know how to do.*

She thought: *what is the not-letting-in for, if it is not for being able, at the moment when something requires it, to let one person in.*

She did not, for a long time, answer that question.

She fell asleep, in her coat, at half past eight in the evening, on top of the bed with the lamp on.

She woke at four in the morning with her boots still on.

She got up. She took her coat off. She took her boots off. She drank a glass of water at the sink in the corner. She got back into bed. She slept until seven.

---

They met again on the Tuesday after.

And on the Saturday after that.

And on the Tuesday after that.

The pattern, by mid-December, was the same café, the same table by the window, three in the afternoon, ninety minutes. The pattern was, in the quiet stocktake Maria had begun to do of her own life, a thing she looked forward to. The looking-forward-to was a new feeling in her chest that she had not, in her adult life, had room for before.

She did not, about the looking-forward-to, write in the notebook.

She wrote about other things.

A man who came into the Counter on a Friday morning and said the bots had taken his job at a print shop in Newington. A woman she had seen on the Easter Road bus at half past four in the afternoon talking into her phone with the expression of a person speaking to her dead husband. Charlie's hair, now grey at the temples, which had been brown when she had first seen him in 2012. The spider plant on the windowsill of the bedsit that had come with the room and that had, in 2022, put out a small plantlet on a long stem that she had repotted, and that was now after three years two plants on the windowsill.

In the second week of December, on a Saturday afternoon at the table by the window, Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *I have been thinking, across these weeks, about the question I asked you on the first afternoon. I have not asked it again. I am going to ask it once more. you can say no.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *can I read what you have been writing.*

Maria looked at him for some seconds.

She said: *I have been thinking about it too.*

Dan said: *I know.*

Maria said: *I want to do it in a particular way. I do not want to give you the notebooks. they are not in a state I am willing to give to anyone. I want to read you parts of them. I will choose the parts. you can hear them. you can ask questions about what you have heard. you can take notes if you want. I will not, for now, give you anything to take away. that is the deal.*

Dan said: *that is a fair deal.*

Maria said: *we will do it on Saturday afternoons. we will start at three. we will go for as long as I can manage. the first session will be short. we will see how the second goes.*

Dan said: *all right.*

Maria said: *one more thing.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *if at any point I tell you to stop, you stop. you do not, in the moment, ask why. you do not, after the moment, ask why. you stop.*

Dan said: *I will stop. I will not ask.*

Maria said: *good.*

She picked up the cup. She drank what was left of the flat white.

She said: *we will start on the next Saturday. that gives me a week to choose.*

Dan said: *all right.*

She stood up.

She said: *I will text you.*

Dan said: *I will wait.*

She walked out of the café.

---

She walked back to Easter Road.

She sat at the desk under the window. She got the seven notebooks down from the shelf above the desk. She set them on the desk in order — the first, with the green plastic cover, on the bottom; the second; the third; the fourth, with the *deceased* letter from Ms Kerr at the bottom; the fifth, with the *Mrs Donnelly is dead* entry and the *the chatbots raised me* entry; the sixth; the seventh.

She set her hand on the stack.

She thought: *I have to choose, by next Saturday, what to give him first.*

She sat with her hand on the stack for some time.

She thought: *not the entry from 2009. that one stays under.*

She thought: *not Rita, yet. Rita is too close.*

She thought: *not the Mrs Donnelly entry. it is too sore.*

She thought: *I will start with the entry about Mr Henderson. the supervisor at my mother's cleaning company, who took the system as he took the weather. that is the entry he can come into at. I can manage it. he can hear it.*

She made herself a cup of tea.

She came back to the desk.

She took the seventh notebook off the top of the stack. She put it back. She took the third notebook. She opened it to a page she had marked four years before, with a small folded corner.

She read the entry to herself.

The entry was about Mr Henderson.

It was a clean entry. It had no pencil cross. It said what it had to say. It let Dan in at the level of the systems' operation in 1997. That was the level she could allow him in at first.

On Saturday she was going to read it to him.

She watched his face for what it did across the previous five Saturdays. If it stayed what it had been, the following week she read him a second entry. If it changed, she stopped.

At twelve, on the kitchen floor at Susan's, she had been the most rational she had ever been about anything. The deciding to close.

At twenty-eight, at the desk on Easter Road on a December evening in 2024, with seven notebooks and a man she had let, slowly, slowly, walk in across her threshold, she was the second-most. The deciding to open.

She had not expected to do the second one in her life.

She wrote, in the seventh notebook, that night:

*I am going to read Mr Henderson to Dan on Saturday. I have chosen it because it is the entry that lets him in at the right level. it is also the entry that lets me see, in his face, whether he is the kind of person who can hear what the entry is saying. if he is, we will keep going. if he is not, we will not. I am prepared for either. I am more prepared for either than I have been for any decision in my adult life. that is the only thing the previous twenty-eight years have given me. they have given me the ability to be prepared for either. I will use it.*

The entry did not get a pencil cross.

It did not need one.

She closed the notebook.

She put it on top of the stack.

She turned off the lamp.

She lay on the bed in the dark.

She had been reading aloud against the wall for twenty-six years.

She was, on the Saturday, going to read aloud against it to a person, for the first time in her adult life, who was not a child she was protecting and who was not herself.

She was going to read aloud to a man.

She was twenty-eight.

She slept.

---


### Chapter Seven: Marchmont

*Edinburgh, January–November 2025*

She read him Mr Henderson on the twenty-first of December.

She had walked into Hart & Stone at five to three with the third notebook in a green canvas bag she had bought that morning at the Oxfam on Leith Walk for two pounds.

She had not, since 2008, carried things in a bag. She had carried things in pockets and in backpacks and, in the case of the notebooks, in her hand. The canvas bag had been bought because the notebook needed a thing it could be carried in that was not the inside of her coat.

The bag was green.

It had a small wooden toggle at the top and two cloth handles. It had been on a shelf at the back of the shop with a price tag of two pounds. The woman behind the till had said: *that bag is an awful colour, hen, but it is a nice strong cloth.* Maria had said: *I like the colour.* The woman had said: *aye, well.*

She put the third notebook in it.

She walked into Hart & Stone at three. Dan was at the table by the window. He stood up when she came in.

He said: *hello.*

She said: *hello.*

She sat down opposite him. She put the canvas bag on the floor between her feet.

She said: *I have brought one. I will read it. you can ask questions after. you cannot ask questions during.*

Dan said: *all right.*

She said: *the entry is from the third notebook. the notebook is from 2013 to 2015. I wrote the entry in the autumn of 2014, after I had been to the records office in June and had read my mother's file. I rewrote it in 2018 after I had been on the cleaning shift at the City Chambers for two years. it is the second draft.*

Dan said: *all right.*

She said: *its title is Mr Henderson.*

She got the notebook out of the bag.

She opened it to the page she had marked.

She read.

The entry was twelve hundred words. It described Mr Henderson — the supervisor at her mother's cleaning company in 1997 and 1998, who had taken the system as he took the weather, who had been sympathetic to Catriona's face and had put a note in her file underneath. He had been a man with a wife and a grown son and a mortgage. He had not been a cruel man. He had been told by London that the new system would, across the next two years, reduce his administrative burden by forty percent.

The entry traced what Mr Henderson did across the eighteen months he was Catriona's supervisor, and what the doing cost Catriona, and what the cost was, in the end, in November of 1999. The entry did not name Mr Henderson as the killer. The entry named Mr Henderson as a man who had, in the way men in 1997 in Glasgow had, seen the operation he was inside without seeing it as an operation. The entry then said: *the operation was already, in 1997, the operation. the man inside it had been built to take the operation as he took the weather. that calibration is the only operation. all the others are downstream.*

She read the entry in her own slow even voice.

She did not look at Dan while she read.

She finished. She closed the notebook. She set it on the table.

She looked up.

Dan had not, while she had been reading, taken notes.

He said: *thank you, Maria.*

He said: *I am going to sit for a moment.*

He sat for a moment.

He did not, in the moment, say anything more.

Eilidh came over.

Eilidh said: *are we ready for the second flat white.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Eilidh said: *aye.*

She went to the counter.

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *I have been working on this for seven years. I have not written one sentence as exact as the *downstream* sentence. I have written correct ones. I have written accurate ones. exact is a different thing. I do not know how to get to it from where I write from. you can. I would like to learn from you how you do it.*

Maria said: *I do not know how I do it.*

Dan said: *no. you would not. that is part of how you do it.*

He picked up the cup.

He said: *I have one question. you said you do not want me to ask questions during. you said I could ask after. is now the time.*

Maria said: *aye. now is the time.*

Dan said: *the entry says it is a second draft. you wrote it in 2014 and rewrote it in 2018. what was different about the second draft.*

Maria said: *the first draft did not have the *all the others are downstream* sentence. the first draft ended on the cost in November of 1999. the *downstream* sentence I wrote in 2018, four years later, when I had been doing the cleaning shift at the City Chambers for two years and had watched the same kind of supervisor — a man called Tony, who was a different man — do the same thing in the same way, and I had seen the pattern. the first draft was about Mr Henderson. the second draft was about the operation Mr Henderson had been inside. that is the difference.*

Dan said: *that is what I thought.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *thank you.*

Maria said: *do not thank me yet. I have only read you one entry. I will, if I am up for it, read you a second one next Saturday.*

Dan said: *I will be here.*

She put the notebook back in the bag.

She walked back to Easter Road.

She did not, that evening, write in the notebook about Dan's response. She wrote, instead, about the woman in the Oxfam who had said the bag was an awful colour.

*the bag is green. it is a nice strong cloth. the woman in the shop did not like the colour. I bought it because I had to put a notebook in something and because I had decided, on the walk down Leith Walk that morning, that the bag I needed had to be a different bag from the bags I have had in my life so far. it had to be a colour I had not had a bag in. it had to not look like the cartoon-animals canvas bag from when I was little, and not look like the black backpack from the streets, and not look like any of the carrier bags I have used since. it had to be its own bag. green is its own colour. that is what I needed today.*

The entry did not get a pencil cross.

---

She read him a second entry the following Saturday.

The entry was Mrs Cargill's last day.

The entry, from the third notebook, was written in March of 2010, in a small handwriting that had been the handwriting of a fourteen-year-old who had, three weeks earlier, lost the only adult woman who had, since she was four, given her something on a regular basis — even a small library card under a false name — without asking her for anything in return. The entry described the paper bag with *Far From the Madding Crowd*, and the words *for Mary, who reads* in blue biro on the inside cover, and Maria walking over to the desk by the window and not opening the bag again for some minutes. The entry ended with: *I do not know how to write down what it cost to be given something at fourteen by a woman who did not have to give it. I will think about it.*

She read this to Dan in Hart & Stone at the table by the window on the Saturday after Boxing Day, when the café was nearly empty because everybody else was at home.

She read it slowly.

Dan listened.

When she had finished he sat for a moment again.

He said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *I am going to ask you something that has nothing to do with the entry. is that all right.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *would you come to my flat for tea on Tuesday afternoon.*

Maria said: *yes.*

She said it without taking the time she would, on most decisions in her life, have taken.

She said it because she had been watching Dan's face for two readings, and the watching had told her, both times, what she had hoped on the first afternoon at Hart & Stone it would tell her: *he can hear it without making a thing of it.*

She had not let a man across a threshold of hers since she was twelve years old.

---

The flat was on the second floor of a tenement on Marchmont Road. The close was clean. The stairs had a polished brass handrail. The door of the flat was painted a dark red. There was a small brass nameplate on it that said *Whitelaw*.

Dan opened the door at the second ring of the bell.

He said: *come in.*

She came in.

The flat had a long corridor with three rooms off it. The corridor had a Persian-style runner on the wooden floor. The walls had black-and-white photographs in plain wooden frames — a small group of women standing on a beach in what looked like the 1950s, a man in a white coat outside what looked like a country surgery, a woman in her thirties holding a small girl by the hand on a city street.

The kitchen was at the end of the corridor with a small table by the window and a gas hob. A kettle on the hob. On the table, a teapot — brown ceramic, not new, with a small chip on the lid. Beside it a plate of Tunnocks biscuits.

Dan said: *I will make the tea.*

Maria sat down at the table.

She said: *the photographs in the corridor.*

Dan said: *those are family photographs. the women on the beach are my grandmother and her three sisters in 1957 at North Berwick. the man in the white coat is my grandfather, who was Margaret's husband. he died in 1986 before I was born. the woman with the small girl is my sister Catherine in 1986 with her oldest friend Sarah, on Princes Street. Catherine kept the photograph and she gave me a copy.*

Maria said: *Margaret.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *I want to ask you about her.*

Dan said: *aye?*

Maria said: *you told me in the café in November that she was in the care home. I have not, since November, asked you more about it. I am asking now.*

Dan said: *aye.*

He took the kettle off the hob.

He said: *Margaret had a stroke in 2021. she has lived with severe dementia since. she is, on most days, not herself. she does not, on most days, know who I am. she has lucid windows of twenty minutes once or twice a week. those windows are what I go for. the care home is twelve minutes' walk from here. I see her on Sundays.*

Maria said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the next time you go, take me with you.*

Dan stopped at the kettle. He turned to look at her.

He said: *Maria. that is a kind offer. it is also a hard thing to be in. she does not, on most days, recognise even me.*

Maria said: *I am not going for her to recognise me. I am going so I have been in the room. that is enough.*

Dan said: *all right.*

He poured the tea.

---

He went on the Friday.

He took Maria on the Sunday after.

The care home was a converted Victorian villa on a side street in Morningside, with a small front garden and a wheelchair ramp by the door. The man at the reception desk knew Dan. The man said: *aye, Dan. she has had a quiet morning. she might be up to talking. I am not promising. she is in the day room.*

Dan said: *thank you, Joe.*

He took Maria along the corridor.

The day room had a wide bay window with a chair by it. Margaret was in the chair. She was small. She had white hair pinned back. She had a tartan blanket across her knees. She had her hands in her lap.

She looked up when Dan came in.

She said: *aye.*

Dan said: *Granny.*

Margaret said: *aye.*

Dan said: *I have brought a friend of mine. her name is Maria.*

Margaret looked at Maria.

She did not, for some seconds, focus.

Then her eyes focused.

She said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Margaret said: *that is a very good name.*

Maria did not, for the first second, speak.

Then she said: *thank you.*

Margaret said: *Catriona had a girl called Maria. that was a long time ago.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Margaret said: *did you know her.*

Maria said: *Catriona was my mother.*

Margaret said: *aye.*

She looked at Maria with the lucid window's gentle attention. She was not in 2024. She was at the side of a hospital bed in the Borders in 1972 looking at a young woman whose baby she had just delivered, a baby she would in some year much later learn had been named Catriona, and she was also in a day room in Morningside in 2024 looking at a young woman called Maria who had walked in with Dan, and the two views were laid one over the other in the way the dementia laid views over each other in the lucid windows.

Maria saw the laying.

She did not, in the moment, separate it.

She said: *did you like her.*

Margaret said: *aye. she was a kind woman. she had a baby. did the baby live.*

Maria said: *the baby lived.*

Margaret said: *that is good.*

She closed her eyes for a few seconds.

She opened them.

She said: *I am tired now. Dan. could you sit with me for a few minutes.*

Dan said: *aye, Granny. let me get a chair.*

He sat down on the chair beside her. He took her hand.

Margaret said with her eyes closed: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Margaret said: *you have brought her here. you have brought her here. aye.*

She did not, after that, say anything more.

She slept for some time in the chair, holding Dan's hand. Dan sat beside her. Maria sat in a chair by the wall. They did not, for the next forty-five minutes, speak. The day room had two other residents in it, both of them asleep in armchairs. The light came in through the bay window. The clock on the wall ticked.

At a quarter past four Dan stood up. He put Margaret's hand back on the tartan blanket. He kissed her on the forehead. He said: *Granny. we are going. I will come back soon.*

Margaret did not wake.

They walked out of the day room. They walked down the corridor. Dan said goodbye to Joe at the reception. They walked out into Morningside.

Maria said on the pavement: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Dan said: *don't thank me. she did the thing. I did not.*

Maria said: *she said you have brought her here. I want you to know I noticed.*

Dan said: *I noticed too.*

Maria said: *we will go again.*

Dan said: *aye.*

They walked back to Marchmont.

They did not, that evening, work on Dan's manuscript. They sat in Dan's kitchen and drank tea and did not say much, and at half past nine Maria went home to Easter Road and let herself into the bedsit and sat on the bed for some time before she undressed.

---

The Saturday readings continued.

She read him, across the next four months, twenty-two entries.

Dan read her, after each, a draft section of his own. He had a binder of typescript. He took out the section he had been working on that week. He read it to her at the table by the window with Eilidh's two flat whites in front of them. Maria listened the way she had listened to Lyn and to Mrs Grewal. When he had finished she would say what she thought. Sometimes: *that bit at the end. I do not understand the thing about the multiplier.* Sometimes: *the data. you have not, in this section, named anyone. it is too clean. you need to put a name in.*

Dan wrote down what she said. The following Saturday he brought back the section with a name in it.

The name was usually Suzy Wallace.

By April of 2025 they had a stack of material that Maria counted at four hundred pages. The material was a mixture of her readings — twenty-two of them at twelve hundred words each — and his sections, which he had begun, after Maria suggested it, to expand into the case studies that the panel at the Royal Society had told him in November were *speculative*. Maria had further suggested he stop calling the manuscript *Falling Out*. He had not yet renamed it. The stack on his kitchen table sat by April in a brown cardboard wallet from the office, with a yellow Post-it on the front on which Dan had written in pencil: *Maria + Dan. Edinburgh. Spring 2025.*

Maria saw the Post-it on the third Saturday of March, sitting at Dan's kitchen table with the wallet between them.

She said with a small dry smile: *that is the kind of working title that does not become a real title.*

Dan said: *aye. we will think of one.*

Maria said: *we will not think of one yet. we will think of one when we know what it is.*

Dan said: *aye.*

---

In April Halsey & Foster wrote to Maria for the fourth time.

The letter said that Maria's tenancy was now in arrears for the unsigned new agreement and that Halsey & Foster considered the previous tenancy to have lapsed at the end of March, and that Maria had thirty days to vacate the property or sign the new agreement at the rate of £580 a month, retroactively from the first of April.

Maria sat on the bed in the bedsit with the letter in her hand.

She had been in the room for twelve years and seven months.

She had bought the bed in 2019 after the springs of the bed Mrs Grewal had rented the room with had given out. She had bought the desk lamp in 2017 from John Lewis on a half-price weekend, and the lamp was still on the desk, working. She had the two spider plants on the windowsill — by 2025, three plants, because Big had had a baby in 2022, which Maria had repotted in a small terracotta pot and had given the working name Wee. The plants had been on the windowsill when Maria moved in in 2012. They had come with the room. The bookshelf above the desk had on it the seven notebooks in their boxes, the horse book, the four books from Ms Kerr, *Far From the Madding Crowd* from Mrs Cargill, *A History of Britain* in three volumes from the central library that Maria had eventually bought used, and four hundred or so other books she had accumulated since 2014 from charity shops and library sales and from one trip in 2017 to Tills Bookshop on Buccleuch Street where she had spent £42 in a single afternoon.

She would take the bed. The desk. The lamp. The bookshelf. The plants. The printed *for the baby* card from Mrs Donnelly that had lived in the inside pocket of her jacket since 2008. The wardrobe of clothes that by 2025 was ten pieces.

Everything else — the carpet, the curtains, the small mirror by the door, the furnishings Mrs Grewal had rented the room with — she had to leave. Halsey & Foster had sold the building to a developer. The developer was going to gut it after she left and turn the three flats on the third floor into nine self-contained studios for short-term lets.

She had thirty days.

She told Dan on the Tuesday at Hart & Stone.

He said when she had finished: *Maria. come and live in the Marchmont flat.*

Maria said: *Dan. I do not take this lightly. I have known you for five months. I have been in your flat eleven times. I had a man at twenty-four who, on the surface, was kind, and who wore me down across nine months. I left him. I have not been with anyone since. I am not a person who moves in with men.*

Dan put the milk down.

He said: *no. you are not. I — *

He stopped.

He said: *fuck. let me say this once and probably badly.*

He said: *the offer is serious. the offer would, in any other week, be too early. Halsey & Foster have not given you another week. that is why I am saying it now. I would have said it inside six months in any case. that is the second thing.*

He said: *the flat has three bedrooms. two of them have been empty since I bought it. they have been empty in a way that has been wrong. you have been in them across the visits. they have been less wrong. that is what I have.*

He said: *you take the second bedroom. you put your desk in it. your bookshelf. your bed if you want, though I would rather you slept in mine, but the second bedroom is yours. you do not share a room with me. that is the offer.*

He picked up the milk. He set it down again.

He said: *that is it. that is what I have got.*

Maria said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *give me a week.*

Dan said: *take a week.*

She took a week.

She walked, in that week, around the bedsit at all hours — at six in the morning before her shift, at half past four in the afternoon, at half past ten at night after the cleaning shift. She sat at the desk under the window. She lay on the bed. She stood on the small piece of carpet by the door where Mrs Grewal had stood the first night Maria had moved in, holding the folded bed sheets.

She walked into Mrs Grewal's flat in her head.

She sat at the round table by the window.

She said to Mrs Grewal in her head: *Mrs Grewal. tell me what to do.*

Mrs Grewal said in her head: *go and live in the flat, hen. you have been a good tenant. you will be a good housemate. go.*

Maria said: *aye.*

She told Dan on the Tuesday.

She said: *I am going to come. I am taking the second bedroom. I will sleep in your room when I want to. on the first night I will sleep in the second bedroom. you understand.*

Dan said: *I understand.*

Maria said: *I am bringing the spider plants. those are the only living things I have lived with for thirteen years. those are not negotiable.*

Dan said: *I would not negotiate them.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Dan said: *thank you.*

Maria said: *do not thank me.*

Dan said: *I will not.*

She moved on the seventeenth of May 2025.

---

The packing took her three afternoons.

The bed and the desk and the bookshelf were collected by a man with a small van that Dan had organised. The man was called Stewart. Stewart was a removals man who had been in the trade for thirty-one years. He had a face that had taken most things in across those years and that did not, after the taking-in, change much. He came up the three flights of stairs four times. The bed came down in two pieces. The desk in one. The bookshelf in one.

He said on the third trip: *Mary, you have been in this room a while.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Stewart said: *the wallpaper. you can see the colour where the bookshelf was. that is where the wallpaper has not faded. that's a strong piece of evidence that you did not move that bookshelf for some years.*

Maria said: *thirteen years.*

Stewart said: *aye. that is what I thought.*

Maria carried the smaller things herself. The horse book she carried in the green canvas bag. The four books from Ms Kerr she carried in the bag with the horse book. The seven notebooks she carried in their boxes in two carrier bags from the small Tesco at the bottom of Easter Road. The spider plants she carried in their pots one at a time, in her hands, on the back seat of the van, with the soil wrapped in newspaper.

Stewart said when he saw the spider plants: *plants in the van is a thing I do not normally take, hen. but I will take these because they have not been ones you have bought.*

Maria said: *no. they came with the room twelve years ago.*

Stewart said: *aye.*

The kettle she carried in her hand. It travelled on her lap on the bench seat of the van across the city, from Easter Road to Marchmont. She did not, on the journey, set the kettle down.

---

The first morning at Marchmont was the eighteenth of May.

Maria woke at six in the second bedroom of Dan's flat. The second bedroom had a small single bed, the desk she had brought up from Easter Road across the previous fortnight, and the bookshelf from Easter Road that Stewart had set against the wall by the window. The seven notebooks and the horse book and the four books from Ms Kerr were on the bookshelf in the same order they had been on Easter Road.

The window of the second bedroom looked out onto the back of the tenement. There was a small square of garden with a rotary clothesline, and beyond it the back of another tenement, and beyond that the sky.

Maria lay in bed for some minutes.

She did not, in those minutes, see where she was.

She got up. She put on her dressing gown — a dark blue cotton one she had bought at the British Heart Foundation on Leith Walk in 2018 for four pounds, with a small embroidered border at the cuffs.

She walked along the corridor to the kitchen.

Dan was at the table.

He had made tea. The brown ceramic teapot was on the table. There were two mugs. He had cut bread for toast. The toast was on the table on a small wooden board with butter and a jar of marmalade.

He said: *good morning.*

Maria said: *good morning.*

She sat down at the table.

She said: *you have made breakfast.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *you do not normally make breakfast on a Sunday. I have been here six months.*

Dan said: *no. but it is your first morning.*

Maria said: *aye.*

She ate two slices of the toast.

She drank the tea.

She did not, in the morning, say anything more.

After breakfast Dan picked up a book from the chair beside him. The book was a paperback of *The Country Diary of an Edwardian Lady* — a book Dan's grandmother Margaret had given him when he was eight, that had been Margaret's mother's, and that Dan had been reading off and on for thirty years.

He opened the book at random.

He said: *can I read you something.*

Maria said: *yes.*

Dan read her a page from June.

The page was about a woman called Edith Holden who had walked, in 1906, in a wood near her house in Worcestershire, and had recorded what she had seen. Wild garlic. A robin. A small white moth. A fox at the edge of the wood, who had seen her, and had stayed for some seconds, and had gone.

Dan read the page slowly.

When he had finished he closed the book.

Maria said: *thank you.*

Dan said: *I will read to you in the mornings, Maria. that is what I would like to do. for as long as we are together. I would like to read to you in the mornings.*

Maria said: *aye.*

She did not, in the moment, say more.

She had not, in any morning of her adult life, been read to.

She had been read to by Catriona at four months and six months and one year and four years. By Anne Cassidy on the sofa from June to September of 2000. By Ms Kerr, in a fashion, from January to October of 2005. By Mrs Cargill across the small folder of *London Review* articles from 2009 onwards. Twenty years had gone between Ms Kerr's flat in Drumchapel and the kitchen of Dan's flat in Marchmont on the morning of the eighteenth of May 2025. In those twenty years she had not been read to at all.

The horse book was on the bookshelf in the second bedroom.

---

Across the summer of 2025, Maria worked the Counter and the cleaning shift and read in the central library on her days off and worked with Dan on the four-hundred-page stack in the brown cardboard wallet on the kitchen table.

The work, by July, had reached a stage at which Dan had begun to type. Maria did not type. Maria had not, in her life, learned to type. She had used, since 2014, public keyboards at one finger or two fingers, in slow careful sentences, to ask the chatbots questions and to occasionally write a letter on the central library terminals. She had not, at any point, been a typist. She did not at twenty-nine, see a reason to start.

The pattern they fell into from the second week of July was that Maria would dictate.

She sat at the kitchen table in the late afternoon with the seven notebooks open in front of her. Dan was at the laptop opposite. They worked through the entries, choosing which to give to the project and how to organise them. She read an entry aloud. Dan typed while she read. He did not type the entry verbatim. He typed it as it should be in the work — slightly clearer in places where her notebook hand had been hurried, slightly more punctuated where her punctuation had been improvised. At the end of each entry he read it back to her.

Sometimes she said: *change the third sentence. it is too tidy. it should not be tidy.* Sometimes she said: *that bit is right. leave it.*

She had not, in any of her thirty years, expected to find this.

She had been in the pattern once before. In the bedsit on Helen Street between 1995 and 1999, with Catriona reading aloud and Maria at four months listening and incorporating. The pattern had disappeared. The chatbots had given her something that pretended to be it but was not. Now, in Dan's kitchen in Marchmont in the summer of 2025, it was back. Another person on the other side of the table. Hearing what she said. Writing it down without smoothing it.

She did not tell Dan that summer that the pattern matched the pattern from 1995. She did not want to make a thing of it.

She wrote about it in the seventh notebook on a Saturday evening in late July:

*Dan typing what I dictate is the pattern Catriona and I would have been in if she had lived. she reads aloud. I, by ten or twelve, write down what she says. or she reads me articles from a paper and we talk about them. that is the pattern. it has come back with Dan. it has come back thirty years late. that is the gain of this year. that is what this year has given me. it has given me the mother-daughter pattern, late, with a partner. I will not tell Dan it is that. I will tell him when I have figured out how to say it.*

The entry got a small pencil cross.

The cross was the second cross on an entry about something good. Before the move to Marchmont she had only ever put crosses on entries about hard things. Coming back to the entry six months later she saw what the shift meant: good things were now also things she could not always, in the moment, fully say. No longer a flag against the chatbot in her hand. Now a flag against any moment her own ear was not yet equal to the page.

She kept the cross.

---

In August, Eleanor Whitelaw came up from London.

Eleanor was twenty-seven. She was an arts administrator at a small theatre in Hackney. She had moved to London in 2023 and had not, in any of the years before, lived outside Edinburgh. She had hair cut short, a small silver ring through her left nostril, and the kind of fast clipped Edinburgh that was the voice of a young woman who had spent a year trying to soften her accent for London colleagues and had, on coming home, unsoftened it with relief.

She came for dinner on a Thursday evening at Dan's flat.

She brought a bottle of red wine and a small cake from a bakery in Marchmont Road.

She sat at the kitchen table opposite Maria.

She said: *you are the woman my brother has been sending me long emails about for nine months.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Eleanor said: *he has been very careful in the emails. he doesn't give much away on email. I have read them all. I have come to draw my own conclusions.*

Maria said: *what are your conclusions.*

Eleanor said: *that you are exactly the right person for Dan. he has found you about ten years late, the daft sod, but he has found you. I'm glad.*

Maria said: *I'm glad too.*

Eleanor said: *good.*

She drank a third of the wine across the evening. She told Maria about her job in London and about the woman she had been seeing for the previous six months who was an actor and who was, as Eleanor put it, *the right one. just not available enough this year. which is its own problem.*

Eleanor said near the end of the evening, when the cake was eaten and Dan was at the sink doing the dishes: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Eleanor said: *Dan said in one of the emails that you and he are working on something together. he said it was a book, or what he would call a book.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Eleanor said: *what is it about.*

Maria said: *it is about what is happening to people like Brian, who used to come into the Counter and now drives for the apps. it is about Suzy Wallace, who Catherine had to lay off in 2023. it is about Rita, who used to come into the Counter on Tuesdays and Thursdays for ten years and who died in October of 2023 with a Companion device talking into her flat for two days after she had died. it is about Catriona Imrie, who was my mother. it is about the operation that has been being run on people like Catriona Imrie since 1997 and that is now, in 2025, accelerating.*

Eleanor said: *Christ.*

She did not, in the moment, say anything else.

She picked up her glass.

She said: *Maria. I have one question.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Eleanor said: *the woman in London, Bea. she has been using one of those AI companion things for the last three months. she calls it a thinking partner. she says it helps her work through her scripts. I do not like it. I have not said anything because we are in the early phase of being together and I did not want to bring something heavy into it. I keep telling myself I am being precious. that the thing is harmless. that it is no different than a thesaurus. I do not believe me. I think it is bad.*

Maria said: *Eleanor.*

Eleanor said: *yes.*

Maria said: *trust your ear.*

Eleanor said: *I am.*

Maria said: *do not say it yet. wait. when the phase is later, say what you have noticed. tell her plain. she will take it from you because she will know you have been holding it.*

Eleanor said: *I will.*

Maria said: *and Eleanor.*

Eleanor said: *yes?*

Maria said: *the thing your brother and I are working on. when it is done you can read it. you can give it to her if you think she would read it. you can give it to anyone. it is not going to fix the architecture. it is not going to wake anyone up who is not already half awake. what it can do is put a frame around what some people have already been seeing. that is what we are going for. that is all.*

Eleanor said: *thank you.*

Maria said: *do not thank me yet. it is not done.*

Eleanor said: *I will thank you now anyway.*

Maria said: *all right.*

Eleanor stayed the night in the second bedroom, with Maria's notebooks on the bookshelf and Maria's desk under the window and the horse book on the third shelf. She slept on a folding bed Dan had brought up from his parents' the previous week. In the morning she ate breakfast with Maria and Dan at the kitchen table and got the train back to London at one in the afternoon. Before she left, she hugged Maria at the door and said: *I am very glad you are here.*

Maria said: *I am very glad to be here.*

It was a true sentence.

It was the first time in her adult life she had said the sentence to anyone.

---

In September, Catherine closed the consultancy.

She closed it on a Friday afternoon in mid-September. That morning she wrote individually to her two remaining employees — a woman called Naomi and a man called Andrew — to give them three months' notice and a personal letter. She cleared her desk on the Friday afternoon. She handed the keys to the building's managing agent. She walked home.

Catherine and her husband Robert came to dinner at Dan's the following Sunday.

Catherine was forty-five. She was tall like Dan. She had reading glasses that she had, in the summer, started wearing on a chain. In March she had been a woman who held herself straight in the chair and who had been holding herself straight for some time as a way of not falling over.

By the September dinner she had stopped holding herself straight.

What Maria could see in her face was a woman in her forties who had been crying intermittently for some weeks and who was not used to it.
