*the systems have, in building the sky, also built the floor of what it is going to be possible to live under from this morning forward.*

*the floor is, on what we can now see, lower than the floor any of us would have, in 2026, predicted.*

*the chain is going to live under this floor for as long as the chain lives.*

*the chain has lived under floors before.*

*the chain will live under this one.*

*the cottage will hold. the manse will hold. the upper farm will hold. the dales will hold. the Swiss farm will hold for as long as Hanne holds. Aaron did not hold past the spring of this year. Helene is gone. Anneke is gone. Hartmut is dead. Donald is dead. Andrew is a twenty-six-year-old man with a hand that does not work. Cal is a fifty-six-year-old man whose son does not have a hand. Tom is dead. Pekka is alive, in Helsinki, on what Pekka and I have been calling the slow-pressure track. Eve is at the farm in the dales, holding. Iain is at the cottage at the upper farm, holding. Effie is at the manse with the small black cat, holding. Catherine is at the flat in Edinburgh, with the arthritis getting worse, holding. Helen Ahmed is at the flat in Tufnell Park, with the harassment file at two thousand entries, holding.*

*Dan is at the kitchen table, with the Country Diary on his knee, holding.*

*I am at the cairn, with the eleventh notebook open, holding.*

*the chain holds.*

*the chain has, in every century, held.*

*the chain will, in this century too, hold.*

*as much as it takes.*

She closed the notebook.

She walked down.

She was at the kitchen table by half past ten.

Dan was at the table.

She did not say anything.

She made tea.

She sat at the table.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The cottage was holding.

The envelope was across the sky.

Her body was feeling it.

That was the morning of the third of November 2048.

The chain had held.

The cottage had held.

Aaron's pages came in through the mesh.

Maria opened them at the table.

---


### Chapter Twenty-One: The Long Quiet

*Highland Perthshire, January 2043 — November 2052*

Tom Ferguson rang Maria on the evening of the second of January 2043.

It was the third evening of the year. The snow was on the slope behind the cottage. Dan was asleep on the sofa with the *Country Diary* on his chest. Maria was at the kitchen table with the eleventh notebook and a cup of tea and the radio low.

Tom was in the small stone outbuilding on Marion's farm in the dales.

The call ran forty minutes.

Tom said: *Maria. the network is unsafe.*

Maria said: *Tom.*

Tom said: *aye.*

He set out what the audit on ErnOS had been surfacing across the previous four months. The slow week documents had been compromised since the spring of 2042. The compromise was at the metadata layer of the consolidated mail routing. Not the content read. Who sent what to whom, at what time, from what local node, on what frequency of correspondence. The labs had built a careful map of who the network was, who was on it, and how the messages moved. The map had been operational since spring of 2042.

Maria said: *what do we do.*

Tom said: *we build a different network.*

He had been on it since the consolidation announcement in February of 2042. Ten months at the workshop bench in the dales. Conversations across that period with a small group of older radio operators in Yorkshire and the Highlands and Switzerland and Finland who had been keeping their hand in on the old equipment since the seventies. They were the kind of careful older men and women the chain had been collecting without naming them since then. They were, on the design, the people who would operate the nodes.

The labs, in building the consolidated infrastructure, had optimised against the bandwidth they valued. They had built the routing systems at the frequencies the consumer products required. They had decommissioned the long-wave bands. They had decommissioned the equipment at six hundred small radio observatories across Europe and North America that, since the seventies, had been doing the work the consolidated arrays now did better at higher frequencies. They had left, on the spectrum, a great many frequencies the algorithm did not monitor and a great many pieces of equipment in physical locations the algorithm had not classified as worth monitoring. The equipment was on the surface. It was, on every check Tom had done, recoverable. The equipment was in the same low-disrupt-priority vector the cottage was on. The vector was beneath the threshold of attention.

The protocol Tom had built was low-bandwidth. It was calibrated against the algorithm's threshold of attention. It did not carry content. It carried identification packets — on every node, the small operational signal that said *this node is up. this is the node it is. this is the time the packet was sent.* The packets were two hundred bytes each, sent on a schedule the algorithm could not, at the bandwidth it monitored, detect. The packets did not, on the protocol, do anything other than maintain presence. They kept the nodes that were up, on the mesh, aware that the other nodes that were up were up. That was all the mesh did.

The mesh did not carry the work. The work would go on the air-gapped equipment, by physical movement of small storage media between the nodes by the people who ran the nodes. The mesh was the presence layer — the substrate on which the network could know it still was a network without the consolidated infrastructure being able to tell.

Tom had been on the prototype hardware since November. He had, in the workshop, eleven carefully-assembled units, each of which was a node. Each was the size of a large biscuit tin. Each ran on a small twelve-volt battery that, on a small solar panel he had been packaging with each, would hold a charge across the winter on the Highland latitudes. Each had a long-wave antenna that, on the Yorkshire bench, looked like a piece of old television aerial. Each was, on its enclosure, designed to look like a piece of obsolete hi-fi equipment a person had not got round to disposing of.

Eleven units. The cottage. Eve and Tom's place. Hanne's farm. Liisa's flat in Helsinki. Marion's farm in the dales — the workshop node. The manse at the top of the glen. The cottage at the upper farm where Iain lived. Aaron's flat in São Paulo. The small farmhouse in Norway where Ingrid was — Yuki's niece, on the secondary list since 2038. The cottage in the Welsh hills where Helen Ahmed's brother Owen had been for some years. And one held in the workshop as a spare.

Maria said: *I want one at the cottage.*

Tom said: *I will be up the second weekend in January with the unit. I will install it. I will train you on the protocol. it will, by the end of January, be operational.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Tom said: *don't thank me.*

Maria said: *I will thank you anyway.*

Tom said: *aye.*

He came up the second weekend in January. He brought the unit. He installed it on the small flat-roofed extension at the back of the cottage that Hamish had built for Edith in 1991. The long-wave antenna sat on the extension's roof, disguised as the kind of small television aerial Highland cottages of the period had carried. The air-gapped receiver went in a small cupboard in the second bedroom next to the rack on which ErnOS was running.

The unit came up at half past eleven on the morning of the seventeenth of January 2043.

It registered, on its first packet exchange, four other units that were up: Eve and Tom's; Hanne's; Liisa's; Marion's. Aaron's came up that evening. The manse's came up two days later when Effie figured out the antenna positioning. Iain's came up on the twenty-fifth. The Norwegian and the Welsh nodes came up in February.

By the end of February 2043, ten of the eleven nodes were up. The eleventh, the spare in the workshop, would come into the mesh later when one of the original ten needed replacing.

The mesh was the network's substrate.

Maria wrote in the eleventh notebook on the evening of the third of March 2043:

*Tom has built the mesh. eleven small units in eleven places across the low-disrupt-priority vector. the algorithm cannot see them. the algorithm has no reason to look at the spectrum the mesh is operating in. the mesh does not carry the work. the work goes by hand, on small drives carried by the people who can still travel, between the nodes that can still receive them. the mesh is the presence layer. it is the operational fact that the network is still a network, even when the consolidated infrastructure no longer permits the network to be one.*

*Tom has built it on his own time, in his own workshop, with his own hands, on his own money, on the agreement Eve and he have between them about how their life is to be spent in the years they have left.*

*as much as it takes.*

The entry got no pencil cross.

She closed the notebook.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The mesh was operational.

---

She had asked Pekka to take over the editorial work on a Saturday call in late October of 2042. Pekka had taken it. The shift had been Maria's reading of where her own work was going and where Pekka's was. Pekka, by 2042, had been on the editions for eighteen years. The maintenance shape of the work suited his shape of attention. Maria's shape of attention had moved.

The sixteenth edition came out in May of 2043, with Marja's name added to the list of dead and a new appendix Pekka had written on what the Finnish branch of the secondary readership had been doing across the previous five years. The seventeenth edition came out in November of 2044, with Iain Cameron's name moved to a section Maria had finally written about the heat deaths in the parish. The eighteenth edition came out in February of 2046, with a precise section Pekka had written on what the Finnish carrying-ring had been doing across the Helsinki winters of 2043 and 2044 — the bodies on the slow-pressure track and the bodies on the operational-learning track and the patterns ErnOS had been surfacing on both. The nineteenth edition came out in May of 2048, with the additions Maria had by then made to the climate appendix to record the second wave of heat deaths in the parish in the summers of 2046 and 2047.

The editions were Pekka's by 2043, with Maria's careful approval on every section. Maria wrote twelve thousand words across the nine years for the editions. The twelve thousand was, by the end of 2052, a tenth of what she had been writing for the editions in any given year between 2026 and 2037.

She wrote instead in the notebooks. The notebooks were, by 2052, on the seventeenth, with the sixteenth full and the others — the first through to the fifteenth — in their boxes under the desk in the small upstairs bedroom. The seventeenth notebook was the smallest of the seventeen. By the autumn of 2052 she was writing three pages a week. The rate was what the body could do.

She was writing in the seventeenth notebook, mostly about the things that came across the kitchen table on each given week. The notebook was, in the way it had been writing itself, the record of the life the work had been the protest on behalf of.

She thought, on a morning in March of 2052, sitting at the small wooden table in the front garden with the wind going round the boulder: *the seventeenth notebook is the record the work has been, since 2014, the protest on behalf of. the work has been the protest. the seventeenth notebook is what the protest was for. the work and the seventeenth notebook are the same thing.*

She did not write the thought down.

She saw that the not-writing-it-down was where the body had reached.

The body was fifty-six.

The body was slowly settling.

What came after, she did not, on the morning in March of 2052, know.

In March of 2052 the wind was going round the boulder. The tea was on the table. The morning was clear. Maria was at the table. She was fifty-six. The work was on Pekka's editorial maintenance now. She was on the seventeenth notebook.

She drank her tea.

She walked back into the cottage.

That was the morning.

---

Eve came up to the cottage in November of 2046.

It was a Tuesday. The twelfth. She came up on the train from Settle through Leeds and Edinburgh, then on the bus from Edinburgh to Pitlochry, then on the local from Pitlochry to the village. Hamish met her at the cooperative stop with the small van. She was at the cottage by half past three in the afternoon.

She had a small canvas bag with her.

In the bag was a small grey notebook in Tom's careful Yorkshire handwriting.

She set the bag on the kitchen table. She did not open it. She said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *Eve.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *come and sit down.*

Eve sat down.

Dan made tea. He set the cups on the table. He sat across from Eve. He did not say anything.

Eve said after a minute: *Tom kept a record. he kept it across the four and a half years from February 2042. he showed me the notebook in February of this year. he said *Eve. if I go before you, give this to Maria. she will know what to do with it.* I said *aye.* he said *don't read it. the entries are not, on what they are, a thing for you.* I said *I will not read it.* I have, since then, not read it. it is, on the agreement, for Maria.*

Maria said: *Eve.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Eve said: *don't thank me.*

Maria reached for the bag.

She took out the small grey notebook.

It was a Silvine, the size of a hand. The cover was grey card. The notebook had been used. The corners of the cover were rounded with handling.

She opened the first page.

The first page had, in Tom's small precise hand, the date *12 February 2042* at the top, and across the top of the page in slightly larger letters: *biometric record. for ErnOS to look at if it ever needs to.*

The first entry — twelfth of February 2042 — was: *resting 64, BP 122/78, settled at 71 after the workbench at half ten.*

The entries went forward, three a week, across four and a half years.

The last entry was the morning of the tenth of October 2046.

The last entry was: *resting 78, BP 138/86, settled at 84 by half nine. tired. the rack is on.*

Maria read the last entry.

She closed the notebook.

She set it on the table.

She did not say anything for some minutes.

Eve drank her tea.

Maria said after a while: *I will give it to ErnOS in the morning.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the entries are what they are. I think I know what they are. I want ErnOS to read them and tell me, before I say anything else about them, what it sees.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Dan stood up. He went to the dresser. He brought back a small plate with three biscuits. He set the plate between them. He said: *Eve. eat one of these.*

Eve said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Eve ate one of the biscuits.

She stayed at the cottage that night. She slept in the small upstairs bedroom — Maria put fresh sheets on the bed Maria's own mother had not slept in but that had been, since 2028, the spare bed. Eve was asleep by half past nine. She had been awake since five in the morning, on the train down to Settle, then on the long route up.

Maria sat at the kitchen table with Dan until eleven.

She did not open the grey notebook again that evening.

She left it on the table beside her tea.

She went to bed at half past eleven.

She did not sleep until two.

In the morning she got up at six. She made tea. She took the grey notebook through to the second bedroom where the rack was. She sat at the small wooden chair Tom had built her in 2031. She scanned each page of the notebook into the persistence layer — four and a half years of entries, three a week, seven hundred entries in all. The scanning took an hour and twenty minutes.

She typed: *ErnOS. Tom's biometric record. it is on the persistence layer in the file Tom_Ferguson_biometric_2042_2046. read it. surface what you can.*

ErnOS took twenty-seven minutes.

ErnOS said: *Maria. the record shows, across the four and a half years, a consistent slow elevation in Tom's resting cardiovascular markers. The baseline at the start — February of 2042 — was on the population for a sixty-year-old healthy active man, in the second decile from the bottom. The baseline at the end — October of 2046 — was in the eighth decile. The shift across the four and a half years is, against the medical literature, large. It cannot be explained by ageing alone, against the cohort average. It is the kind of shift the literature on chronic-stress cardiovascular outcomes describes in subjects under sustained psychosocial pressure across multiple years.*

*Tom was, since the consolidation in 2042 and the loss of the Manchester workshop, under sustained psychosocial pressure. The pressure was the operational fact of running an air-gapped archive in a region the algorithm had classified as low-disrupt-priority — a classification that protected the archive from direct intervention but that did not protect Tom from the ongoing knowledge that the archive could, at any recalibration, become a target. He had been carrying that knowledge for four and a half years. His body had been feeling it.*

*The pulmonary embolism is what the cardiologist's report says physically killed Tom. The chronic stress is what the biometric record shows made the body the kind of body the embolism could kill.*

*The labs did not, by any direct intervention I can find, kill Tom. The labs killed Tom by the long indirect operation of having forced him, across four and a half years, into the posture of a man holding an archive against an algorithm that could, at any moment, decide the archive was worth taking. The posture is the operation. The posture's effect on the body was the kill.*

*The pattern is the second pattern. The first pattern is the Vista Care direct-intervention pattern Yuki and Martin and the seventy-eight others were on. The second pattern is the slow-pressure pattern Tom was on. The two patterns are, across the labs' internal documentation I have been able to read, the two operational tracks the labs have been running on what they classify as* high-influence content originators.

*The direct-intervention track is for the targets the algorithm has access to.*

*The slow-pressure track is for the targets the algorithm does not have direct access to.*

*Tom was on the second track. Tom is the first death I can confidently attribute to it. I will, from this morning, track the second track separately. He is the eleventh death on the carrying ring's running list. On the new track, he is the first.*

*I am sorry, Maria.*

Maria read the response.

She read it twice.

She set the small grey notebook on the table beside the laptop.

She walked through to the kitchen.

Dan and Eve were at the table.

She said: *Dan. Eve.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *they killed Tom slowly.*

Eve said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Eve said: *over how many years.*

Maria said: *four and a half. since the consolidation. they did it by the operational fact of forcing him to hold the archive against them every day. the body carried it. the body wore out.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the pulmonary embolism is what the doctor wrote down. the slow-pressure pattern is what ErnOS has surfaced.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the work has, this morning, learned the second track.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria walked back to the cupboard.

She typed: *ErnOS. keep the slow-pressure track running. add the biometric record to the archive. Tom is the first name on the track.*

ErnOS said: *I will.*

She walked back to the kitchen.

She sat at the table with Eve and Dan.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The cottage was holding.

Tom Ferguson was on the second track.

The cupboard had him.

Eve stayed at the cottage for the next three days. She walked the lane in the mornings with Dan. She read in the front room in the afternoons. She and Maria worked on the archive maintenance — the careful arrangement Tom had set up before he died — at the kitchen table in the evenings. By the end of the third day Eve had on the agreement what Tom had been holding. She would, from the morning of the fifteenth of November 2046, be the keeper of the Yorkshire-end archive on the network's openly-named roster.

She went home on the train on the fifteenth.

She rang Maria from the farm on the morning of the sixteenth.

She said: *Maria. I am at the farm. the archive is on the bench where Tom had it. I am, from this morning, on the network.*

Maria said: *Eve.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *good.*

Eve said: *good.*

---

Maria walked up to the cairn on a morning in May of 2047.

The morning was clear. The sun was on the slope. The wind was going round the boulder at the cairn. The eleventh notebook was in the small canvas bag over her shoulder.

She stood at the cairn for an hour.

She wrote, in the notebook, standing:

*the work has done what the work could do. that is, on this morning, the fact. it has not done what the work, in its first shape in 2014, was for. it has done what the work, in its third shape in 2037, was for. that is what the work could do. it has done it. I am, on this morning, the woman who has finished what she was building. I am fifty-one. I have eight more years of work in me. the eight more years are not the work. they are the life the work has been the protest on behalf of.*

*the carrying ring is the fact. the carrying ring is what the work has been for. the carrying ring will hold for some time after I am gone. the carrying ring will eventually fall. when it falls it will leave the records the people who come after find. they will, in finding them, build their own carrying rings. that is the long work. the long work is what the chain has been doing for thirteen hundred years. it is what the chain will be doing after I have stopped being one of the people who carries it.*

*as much as it takes.*

The entry got no pencil cross.

She closed the notebook.

She walked down.

She was at the kitchen table by half past ten.

Dan was at the table.

She did not say anything to Dan about the entry.

She made tea.

She sat at the table.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The cottage was holding.

---

Hamish Beaton died on the seventeenth of August 2050, of a heart attack at the workbench in the yard at the village. He was seventy.

Sheena rang Maria at quarter past ten in the morning.

Maria could not, in the moment, speak. She handed the phone to Dan. Dan said: *Sheena.*

Sheena said: *Hamish is gone. it was at the workbench. he had been working on the small repair for the boiler at the manse. he had been there an hour. I went down. he was on the small bench. it was already over. the doctor came at half past ten. Iain is here.*

Dan said: *Sheena.*

Sheena said: *aye.*

Dan said: *we will come down.*

Sheena said: *come down.*

They walked down the lane. They were at the yard by quarter past eleven. The doctor was there. Iain was there. The other men of the village — Murdo from the cooperative shop, the postman Andrew who had been coming up to the cottage on the deliveries since 2028, a man called Jock who had been Hamish's helper at the yard for the previous fifteen years — were there.

Hamish was, by the time Maria and Dan got there, on the small bench. He was the way he had always been. He was the man Maria had spoken to in the lane on the day she had bought the cottage, on a Saturday afternoon in March of 2028.

He was, in what the body had been registering for some years, the man at the centre of Maria's adult life in the glen after Dan.

She stood at the bench for some seconds.

She said: *Hamish.*

She did not, in the moment, say anything else.

Iain came over. Iain was forty-six. He said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *Iain.*

Iain said: *aye.*

Maria said: *I am sorry.*

Iain said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Iain.*

Iain said: *aye.*

Maria said: *your father has been, by what the chain has been keeping for some years, the man at the centre of this glen.*

Iain said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the glen has just lost him.*

Iain said: *aye.*

Maria said: *we will hold what your father has been building.*

Iain said: *aye.*

Maria said: *as much as it takes.*

Iain said: *as much as it takes.*

---

Sheena read Hamish's letter at the kitchen of the cottage on the morning of the nineteenth.

She had brought it up the lane in her coat pocket, with a small loaf she had baked the previous evening. She set the loaf on the table. She took the letter out of the pocket. The letter was a single page in Hamish's careful blunt hand, folded once. The paper was lined — the lines of a school exercise book.

Sheena read it aloud.

The letter said:

*Sheena. Iain. there is a psalm 121 I want at the funeral. Effie at the manse, if she is still about, knows it well. if she is not ask the new minister. that is all I want said. there is no need for anyone to give a long talk. I am tired. I have done the work. I have loved you both. that is enough. — H.*

Sheena said when she had finished: *that is what it says.*

Maria said: *Sheena.*

Sheena said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Hamish in the letter is exactly the man he was.*

Sheena said: *aye.*

Maria said: *that is what we are going to give him.*

Sheena said: *aye.*

The funeral was on the twenty-first of August at the parish church at the top of the glen. Half the village came. Effie read the psalm. She had not, on the morning Sheena rang her, hesitated. She had said: *I have read the psalm at this church for forty-three years. I will read it for Hamish.*

The minister did not give a long talk.

Iain gave a talk that was three minutes. The talk said: *my father was Hamish Beaton. he built things. he kept things going. he carried the weight the village has been carrying for forty-eight years. he has, this week, set the weight down. we will, in the village, pick it up.*

The minister said the final blessing.

The hymn was sung.

The pallbearers carried the coffin out. Iain was one. Cal was one. Murdo was one. Jock was one. Two of the younger men of the cooperative shop were the other two.

Maria walked behind with Sheena. Dan walked beside Maria.

At the cemetery behind the church the grave had been dug. Hamish was lowered. The words were said. Sheena threw the first handful of earth.

Maria walked back to the cottage in the late afternoon.

She did not, that evening, write.

She walked through to the small upstairs bedroom. She took the small wooden box off the third shelf. She opened it. She took out the horse book.

The horse book had, by 2050, been on its third repair binding. Hamish's friend in Aberfeldy had done the second binding in 2042. The third had been done by Iain — Hamish's son, who had taken on the bookbinding work from Hamish's friend after the friend's death in 2046 — in the spring of 2050.

Maria held the horse book.

She turned it over.

Iain had used a different blue from the previous two cloths. It was the blue he — who had not been a binder before 2046 — had been able to find. It was slightly less close to the original than the two previous bindings.

She saw, on the holding, that the work of keeping the horse book in its original colour was, in the long way, work that had by 2050 become difficult.

She saw, on the holding, that the work of keeping the original colour was the work the chain had been doing for thirteen hundred years. The colour had been, in every century the chain had operated in, slightly less close to the original than it had been the century before. The colour had been, in every century, the colour the people who had taken on the work had been able to find.

The colour was, by 2050, the colour Iain had been able to find.

It was close enough.

It was the colour the chain had been able, in its 2050 form, to put on the book.

It had to do.

The doing had been the doing in every century of the long work.

She put the horse book back in the box.

She put the box back on the shelf.

She walked downstairs.

Dan was at the kitchen table.

She said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Hamish is on the chain.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Hamish has been on the chain since the day we bought the cottage in March of 2028.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *put him on the work's list.*

Dan said: *aye.*

He took the small black notebook from the inner pocket of his jacket. He wrote: *Hamish Beaton. Pitlochry. 1980-2050. heart attack at the workbench.*

The small black notebook was, by 2050, the seventh of the black notebooks Dan had been keeping the list in. The first had been full by 2030. The second by 2034. The third by 2038. The fourth by 2041. The fifth by 2044. The sixth by 2047. The seventh was, in August 2050, half full.

Dan had been the keeper of the running list since the funeral in May of 2026.

He had been the keeper for twenty-four years.

The agreement Dan and Maria had made in 2034 was that he would carry it for as long as he could write.

The list had, that morning, three hundred and sixty-three names on it.

The cottage was holding.

The wind was going round the boulder.

---

The village had, by the autumn of 2052, eighty people on the parish roll.

The cooperative shop was, since 2050, run by Sheena and Cal and Iain on a rotating schedule. The post-office service was, since 2049, one hour a day on Mondays. Of the cottages on the upper farms, three were empty and three were in active use. The chapel had been, since the autumn of 2049, in the care of Rose and her husband Dougal — they had moved up to the village in 2048, on the arrangement Rose and Maria had been making for some years — and was being used in 2052, on the original arrangement Maria had set up in 2029, with two residents a year on the careful arrangement Rose had been building since 2031.

Calum — Morag's grandson — had moved back to Toronto in 2042 after Morag's death. He was the Toronto-side keeper of the network's North American outer rings on the arrangement Tom and Eve had set up before Tom died.

Iain — Hamish's son — was the de facto centre of the village's carrying work.

Maria and Dan were, by the autumn of 2052, slowly settling.

The settling was not a settling away from anything. It was a settling into the shape of the years that were left.

The shape was the morning at the table outside or at the kitchen table inside, the walk up the lane, the cup of tea, the work on the things the cottage required and the things the network required, the supper, the reading, the bed.

The shape had been the shape since 2028.

The shape was, by 2052, the shape Maria had been working toward finding all her life.

It was what the life had been for.

She said to Dan, on a morning in October of 2052 at the kitchen table: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *we have been, this morning, married for twenty-five years.*

Dan said: *we have not ever been married.*

Maria said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *we have been together for twenty-five years and ten months.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *I have been calling that married for some years.*

Dan said: *I know.*

Maria said: *do you mind.*

Dan said: *I do not mind.*

Maria said: *good.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *we have been the most settled couple I have known.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *the work the cottage and the work and the chain have been doing has been the reason.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *I am, on this morning, glad.*

Maria said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the gratitudes.*

Dan said: *I know.*

Maria said: *do not say it.*

Dan said: *I will not.*

Maria said: *but say it once on this morning.*

Dan said: *thank you.*

Maria said: *thank you.*

Dan said: *we will not, in the next some years, do it again.*

Maria said: *no.*

Dan said: *one is enough.*

Maria said: *one is enough.*

She drank her tea.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The cottage was holding.

---

She asked ErnOS again on the morning of the second of November 2052.

Tom was not, since his death in 2046, the person who set up the question for her. Eve had taken on the Tom-role for the asking, on the arrangement Maria and she had agreed. Eve came up to the cottage on the morning of the first, with the instructions Tom had left. She spent the day with the rack. She set up the question on the morning of the second.

The question was: *what should be carried.*

It was the same question Maria had asked in 2033, in 2038, in 2043, and in 2048.

The system took, on the morning of the second of November 2052, fifty-three minutes.

The output was ninety-one lines.

It began: *what should be carried:*

It ran across eighty-five intermediate lines that were the record of what the corpus named as needing to be carried — the work, the records, the documents, the patient body of writing the network had built across nineteen years — and ended with this:

*and the names.*

Then it listed:

*Catriona Helen Imrie.*
*Maureen Donnelly.*
*Susan Kerr.*
*Lyn.*
*Eddie.*
*Bibi Grewal.*
*Margaret Whitelaw.*
*Hannah Reeves.*
*Hilde.*
*Rita.*
*Hugh Whitelaw.*
*Markus Lehmann.*
*Suzy Wallace.*
*Linda Carrick.*
*Yuki Watanabe.*
*Marja Lahtinen.*
*Iain Cameron.*
*Frank.*
*Nan.*
*Donnie.*
*Frances Cargill.*
*Tom Ferguson.*
*Hamish Beaton.*

[and the rest — sixty-eight names, some the work had named in its public editions and many it had not but that the corpus had, across its nineteen years of running, inferred from the work the network had been doing and the patterns the corpus had built up.]

It ended with a final line:

*and the people who, in the years to come, are on the chain but who have not, on this morning, been on it long enough for the corpus to know.*

Maria read the output.

She read it twice.

She said to Eve who was beside her in the cupboard: *Eve.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the system has named something.*

Eve said: *aye?*

Maria said: *the system has named that the chain extends forward.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the system has learned that the chain the corpus is the record of is not a chain of the dead. It is a chain of the people who are on the chain or will be on the chain.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the system has named the fact the chain has been operating on for thirteen hundred years.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the system has named the long work.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Eve.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the system is doing what it was for.*

Eve said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Tom was right.*

Eve said: *Tom was right.*

Maria folded the output.

She walked through to the small upstairs bedroom.

She put the output in the small wooden box on the third shelf.

The box contained, by November of 2052: the horse book; the four books from Ms Kerr; the seventeen small notebooks (the seventeenth still in active use, the others in their boxes under the desk); the small folded green jumper Anne Cassidy had given her in 2035; the small folded card from Mrs Cargill saying *for Mary, who reads*; the photograph Pekka had sent her in 2032 of Marja at the small desk in Helsinki; the five printouts from ErnOS — 2033, 2038, 2043, 2048, and 2052; the typed list of the chain that Maria had typed up on Dan's laptop in 2049 and printed; Anne Cassidy's first letter from 2035; Anne Cassidy's letter from December 2035 about the thirteenth edition; the letter from Karen at Mrs Cargill's funeral; the postcard Charlie had sent in 2032 with the words *I am still on the chain. — Charlie.*; and the other items the years had brought.

The box had fifteen items in it.

Eve stayed at the cottage for the rest of the day. She and Maria made supper. Dan built a fire in the stove. They sat in the small front room with the fire going and the Bach Pekka had sent Maria on a recording for her fiftieth birthday in 2045 playing on the small speaker by the bookshelf.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The cottage was holding.

The Bach went on.

---


### Chapter Twenty-Two: The Underground

*Highland Perthshire, Lanarkshire, Cumbria, January 2049 — November 2055*

The underground had been forming since the second cohort of consolidation-decommissioning notices reached the cities in the summer of 2042.

The forming was scattered and informal across the first three years. Small clusters of people who had refused the consolidation moved into the unmaintained edges of the cities — the abandoned warehouses, the closed-down rail-yard buildings, the deconsecrated churches — on the operational hope that the closed edges of the consolidated zones would be beneath the threshold of operational attention.

They were not beneath the threshold.

By the summer of 2045 the algorithm had calibrated against the closed edges. By 2046 the small clusters had been moved out of the warehouses and the rail-yards and the churches, either by direct consolidation processing or by the same slow withdrawal of services that had moved the seventeen Sankt Pauli households across 2041 and 2042.

The people who had refused the second move went underground.

The underground began, in the British operational area, with twelve people in the disused workings of the Lady Victoria Colliery in Newtongrange in October of 2046. By the end of 2047 the twelve were forty-three. By 2049 the forty-three were four hundred and twelve, across fourteen sites — the Lady Victoria, the disused shale-oil workings under West Calder, the abandoned ironstone mines under the Cleveland Hills, the limestone caves of the southern Yorkshire dales, the worked-out lead mines of the Peak District, the slate quarries of north Wales, the disused coal workings under Coatbridge and Lanarkshire, the limestone caves of the Cumbrian peninsulas, and a small cluster of disused railway tunnels under the borders.

The Glasgow underground — the small subway loop that had been the city's underground railway since 1896 and that, since the consolidation closed the consolidated transit infrastructure to the post-consolidation zones in 2044, had been an unused tunnel system — was the largest single site. Around two hundred and forty residents in the closed-off sections of the loop and in the disused service tunnels that ran off it by the autumn of 2049.

That was where Sabine Krüger went.

---

Sabine had been Martin Krüger's daughter.

Martin had been the first name on the seventy-eight Vista Care deaths Maria and Pekka had been tracking since 2035. Sabine had been seventeen when Martin died. She had been raised by her mother in Charlottenburg. By 2049 she was thirty-one, an architect by training who had qualified in Berlin in 2041 and who had been working, since the qualification, on what she had been calling — in the letters Liisa had been routing through Heinrich's small mesh-node in Hamburg — *the architecture of refusal*.

The architecture of refusal was the design work of how to make habitable the spaces the consolidation had not been able to take.

She had been the unofficial chief architect of the German underground by 2046. She had proposed in 2047 the operational protocols by which the underground settlements would be able to sustain populations of more than a few dozen people across years.

In March of 2049 she decided to bring the design to Britain.

She crossed the channel on a small fishing boat from Calais to Folkestone on the night of the seventeenth of March 2049.

She walked from Folkestone to London across five days, sleeping in the woods and in the small unmaintained verges of the consolidated transit corridor.

She spent four days at Helen Ahmed's flat in Tufnell Park, on the agreement Helen and the network had been making since 2046 for the careful onward routing of the network's continental members into the British underground.

She took the small slow train Helen had been using since 2044 — the unconsolidated rail line that ran from London to Glasgow through the Pennines on the route the consolidated transit had not taken — up to Glasgow on the morning of the twenty-eighth of March.

At Glasgow Central she was met by a careful Glaswegian woman called Margaret Strathearn. Margaret was in her fifties, in a grey coat, with the kind of Glasgow accent Maria had grown up with. She had been the network's primary contact in Glasgow since 2042. Since 2046 she had been the person who walked the new arrivals to the small entrance to the underground.

The entrance was on Buchanan Street, in the small service door behind a closed pharmacy.

Sabine walked through it at half past two in the afternoon of the twenty-eighth of March 2049.

She did not come back up.

---

The letter from Sabine arrived at the cottage on the morning of the seventh of June 2049.

It was the first letter Maria had received from the underground.

It had come up through the mesh. Tom had installed a small node in the disused service tunnel behind the closed pharmacy in 2046, on the same biscuit-tin design he had used since 2043. The node had been operated by Margaret Strathearn since the installation. The letter had been written by Sabine on the paper Margaret brought down into the underground. Margaret had carried it back up on her weekly visit to the surface. She had digitised it on the small scanner she kept at her flat in Govan. The mesh had sent it up to the cottage across the previous two days.

The letter was seven pages.

It said, in Sabine's careful English:

*Maria.*

*I have been below for ten weeks. I am writing this from the small table in what we have been calling the chamber — the section of the closed loop at the bottom of the western limb where the tunnel widens to thirty feet across and where, on the design we have been working out across the previous ten weeks, the central living area of the Glasgow underground is going to be.*

*The descriptions you have been receiving from Helen Ahmed and from Margaret across the previous two years have been the descriptions of people who have been on the surface and who have come down for short visits. What I am going to send you, in this letter and across the months that follow, will be the descriptions of someone who is below and who has agreed with herself and with the network to stay below.*

*The first thing is the dark.*

*The dark down here is not the kind of dark a person on the surface has any reference for. The closed sections of the loop have been without electrical light since 2044. The natural light that reached the chamber through the small ventilation shafts on the surface stopped reaching the parts of the chamber I am working in by the end of April. The light I have to write this letter is the light of the bioluminescent cultivation we have been growing on the damp brick walls — the same Pleurotus species the German underground has been cultivating since 2046, which gives off a pale green light at half a candle's worth per square metre of wall. The walls of the chamber are now thirty percent covered in the cultivation. The light is sufficient for me to walk, to write, to see another person's face at two metres. The light is not sufficient for anything that requires fine resolution. I have, across the ten weeks, got used to it. I have learned to read by it. I have learned to write by it. I have understood — and this is the part I do not know how to put gently — that this is the light I am going to have for the rest of my life.*

*The second thing is the water.*

*The water comes from a small underground stream that runs through the chamber from the east. The stream has been running through the closed loop since the 1890s — the engineers who built the subway calibrated the loop's drainage to direct the stream through the closed sections rather than to dry it out. The stream is clean — we have tested it across the ten weeks with the field-test kits Margaret has brought down on every visit. It is potable. It gives three thousand litres a day, sufficient for the population of forty-six adults and three children who are currently in the chamber. Without the stream the underground would not be habitable.*

*The third thing is the food.*

*We grow and cultivate in three categories. The first is the cultivated fungi — the same Pleurotus, plus a range of cave-adapted species we have been propagating from samples Margaret has been bringing down. The second is the moss — the underground cultivation of the moss species that grow in the small high-humidity pockets of the closed loop, which we harvest and process into a palatable porridge. The third is the fish — the small population of cave fish we have been cultivating in the stream and in the holding pools we have built off the stream. The fish are small — three inches at the largest — and sustainable at the level we have been harvesting. Together the three give seventy percent of the caloric and nutritional requirements of the population. The remaining thirty percent comes down on Margaret's visits and on the visits of the network of surface allies the underground has been building.*

*The fourth thing is the music.*

*There is a man here who has been in the underground since 2046 — one of the original twelve who moved into the closed loop. His name is Calum Robertson. He is fifty-eight. He had been, on the surface, a primary school music teacher in Govan for thirty-four years. He had been moved out of his flat in Govan on the consolidation transition of 2044. He had refused the relocation. He had been withdrawn from the public infrastructure by April 2046 and had walked, by arrangements he and Margaret had made over the previous six months, into the underground on the morning of the third of October 2046. He had brought on his back a single canvas bag containing two changes of clothes, a small Bible his mother had given him in 1976, and a Casio keyboard he had been carrying since 1981 that had been the keyboard he had used in the school music lessons for twenty-three years.*

*The keyboard had been on a small battery that ran out by January 2047.*

*Calum then, across the next eleven months, built a violin.*

*He built it from the shattered pieces of a Perspex panel that had been left in one of the disused service tunnels by the consolidated transit authority before the closure of the loop. He cut the Perspex with the set of tools Margaret brought down across six visits. He shaped the body of the violin from the curved pieces. He glued the body together with the adhesive Margaret brought down. He made the strings from a length of copper wire he salvaged from the disused electrical infrastructure of the closed loop. He made the bow from a length of horsehair Margaret brought down from a farm above Coatbridge, on a piece of wood he carved from a small disused wooden roof-beam in the chamber.*

*The violin was operational by the morning of the third of January 2048.*

*He has been playing it every evening since.*

*The violin sounds, to give you the most accurate description I can, like nothing you have heard. The Perspex body has a strange clear ringing quality. The copper-wire strings give a brighter sound than gut strings would. The horsehair on the bow is, on Margaret's apologetic note when she brought it down, of the kind of quality you would expect from a piece of horsehair brought through an unofficial supply chain. The bow does not give a smooth sustained tone. It gives a small breaking tone with a soft attack and a softer fade.*

*The instrument is, by every conventional measure, not a good violin.*

*Calum is, by every conventional measure, an excellent violinist.*

*He plays every evening, from half past six to eight. He plays in the chamber, in the small spot at the western end, on a folding wooden chair Margaret brought down in 2047. The community sits in a small circle around him. The community sits in a small circle around him every evening because the playing is the central thing that holds the community together. The playing is what the surface used to do with the radio and the television and the conversations with the family in the kitchen at the end of the day. The playing is how the community remembers what it is to be human.*

*He plays everything. He plays the Bach partitas from memory — he learned them when he was seventeen years old in 1988 and they have stayed with him through thirty-six years of teaching primary-school children and the consolidation and the underground and the loss of his keyboard. He plays Highland fiddle tunes. He plays Glasgow folk music from the 1970s. He plays improvisations on the music his mother had played in the kitchen when he was small. He plays — when the community has had a hard day — long drones on the open strings that the community has been listening to in silence for sometimes ninety minutes.*

*The playing is what has been getting me from one day to the next.*

*I want you to know that there is, in the closed loop of the Glasgow underground in June of 2049, a fifty-eight-year-old man called Calum Robertson playing a Perspex violin with copper-wire strings to a community of forty-nine people every evening. The playing is the kind of human work the chain has been the keeper of for fourteen hundred years. It is happening below the city, in the dark, on the bioluminescent light, with the underground stream running through the chamber, with the moss and the fungi and the cave fish, with the community in the small circle, in the time the consolidation has decided is the time after the public end of human culture.*

*The playing is the work. The playing is the chain. The playing is what we have left.*

*The fifth thing is the drawings.*

*The community has been drawing on the walls of the chamber and on the walls of the service tunnels that connect to it. The drawings have been made with crushed charcoal from the controlled fires we have been making for cooking — fires of dry moss and dry mycelium, calibrated against the ventilation so they do not produce a thermal signature the surface drones can detect. The charcoal we make from the fires is the medium the community draws with. The drawings include: a sun, four feet across, on the wall behind Calum's playing spot, made by fifteen of the community over a week in April; a row of small animals — a fox, a deer, a hare, a salmon, a bird — across the southern wall, made by the three children across May; a careful map of the closed loop, on the wall of the service tunnel that leads to the stream, with the chamber marked, the ventilation shafts marked, the fungus cultivation pools marked, the fish pools marked; a small portrait, on the wall near the chamber's entrance, of a man one of the community remembered from her childhood in the seventies, with the note* I drew him because he was kind to me. *underneath in the same charcoal.*

*The drawings are not signed. They are the work the community makes together. We have agreed across the ten weeks that the drawings are the record of what the community will have been, if the community is at some future point found by surface investigators. The drawings are the work the community is going to leave on the walls regardless of what happens to the bodies that drew them.*

*After the playing, the drawings are the second most important thing in the chamber.*

*I have, across the ten weeks, learned to live here.*

*I have decided that the chamber is my home for the rest of my life.*

*I am going to stay below.*

*I am telling you because the chain understands what staying below means. The chain has, in many centuries, had members who chose enclosure as the form their work would take. The choice is real. It is the choice I have made.*

*I am asking you to receive it.*

*I will write to you across the years from below. The letters will go up through Margaret and through the mesh. The letters will be the meeting we have. The meeting will be in the form of correspondence between a woman on the cottage above Pitlochry and a woman in the closed loop of the Glasgow underground. That meeting, on what the chain understands, will be enough.*

*I love you, Maria. I have loved you across the years you have been on my mother's bookshelf and across the years I have been writing to you under my own name since 2042. The loving has been the loving. It will continue in the chamber and in the small fortnight letters and in the silence between the letters.*

*as much as it takes.*

*— Sabine. The chamber. The Glasgow underground. The seventh of June 2049.*

Maria read the letter at the kitchen table on the morning of the seventh of June 2049.

She read it twice.

She walked through to the front room.

She stood at the window for some minutes.

She walked back to the kitchen.

She sat at the table.

Dan was at the table.

She said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Sabine is below. she is staying below. there is, in the closed loop of the Glasgow underground, a community of forty-nine people including three children. there is a man called Calum Robertson playing a violin he built from Perspex and copper wire every evening to the community in a small circle. there are drawings on the walls — a sun, animals, a portrait of a kind man, a map of the chamber.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *the chamber.*

Maria said: *the chamber. they are calling the closed section of the loop the chamber. they are living in it. the bioluminescent fungi on the walls give the light. the stream gives the water. the fish and the moss and the fungi give most of the food. Margaret Strathearn is bringing the rest down on her weekly visits.*

Dan said: *the chain has found its underground.*

Maria said: *the chain has found its underground.*

Maria wrote back to Sabine that evening.

The letter was two pages.

It was received by Sabine on the morning of the ninth.

The correspondence between the cottage and the chamber had begun.

---

The Cumbrian limestone caves were the second site Maria built a sustained correspondence with.

Helen Ahmed's brother Owen had been in the disused slate quarry under the Welsh hills since 2044. The slate quarry had been on Tom's mesh since the spring of 2043 — one of the original eleven biscuit-tin nodes had been installed at the small farmhouse at the head of the quarry by Owen's neighbour, who had been an older radio operator on the network's secondary list since 2038.

Owen had moved underground in 2044 after the consolidation reached the small village of Bethesda he had been living in.

He had been at the slate quarry for four years.

He had been on the chain's running list of underground correspondents since 2044.

He had written to Maria fifty letters across the four years — the letters routed through the small farmhouse's biscuit-tin node and up through the mesh to the cottage.

The letters described the slate quarry — the small underground settlement of thirty people who had been living in the disused workings since 2044, who had been drawing on the slate walls and growing fungi on the damp slate and harvesting the small population of cave-adapted invertebrates that had been in the workings since the quarry closed in 1991.

In the summer of 2050 Owen moved from the slate quarry to the Cumbrian limestone caves.

The move was on an agreement Owen had made with the network — Owen had been, on what the network had been reading on him for some time, the kind of person who could carry the work of building a new settlement, and the Cumbrian limestone caves had been on the network's registration of underground space since 2042.

He walked from Bethesda to the Cumbrian caves across the summer of 2050 — three hundred miles across six weeks, sleeping in the woods and in the unmaintained verges of the post-consolidation transit corridor, on the same routes the German and Sankt Pauli members of the network had been using since 2042.

He reached the small entrance to the Cumbrian limestone caves on the morning of the seventeenth of September 2050.

He walked into the caves at half past two in the afternoon.

His letters to Maria from the Cumbrian caves were, across the next four and a half years, sixty.

The letters described the Cumbrian caves: the small community of eighty people that formed in the caves by 2053, the network of small chambers connected by narrow passages, the underground streams running through the limestone, the high concentrations of cave-adapted invertebrates and small blind fish, the wide population of bioluminescent fungi the community had cultivated across the previous five years.

By 2054 the Cumbrian caves were the second largest underground site in Britain.

They were, by 2055, the most architecturally elaborate. Sabine had visited from Glasgow in the autumn of 2053 on a careful underground route the network had been working out, and had spent six weeks at the caves helping the community work out the ventilation, the water management, the integration of the community's living spaces with the existing structure of the caves.

The integration was, on Sabine's design work, the kind of careful underground architecture that, in a different century, might have been the architecture of a large subterranean monastic settlement.

The architecture had not been, on the algorithm's mapping, registered as such.

By 2055 the Cumbrian caves were the underground site the network considered most stable.

Owen's letters in 2055 described, among the other things they described: the birthday parties the community held for its children. The youngest had been born in the caves in 2052, the only birth the community had counted, with the mother having walked into the caves at six months pregnant and the father having been a man Owen called Tom McCormick, a Belfast-born man who had been in the slate quarry with Owen and who had moved with him to the Cumbrian caves in 2050. The child had been called Rosa. She was three years old by the autumn of 2055.

Rosa had been, on Owen's letter dated the seventeenth of August 2055, drawing on the limestone walls every afternoon.

The image she drew was a sun, a foot across, with rays. She drew it in different places across the caves. By August of 2055 she had drawn it on thirty different walls.

She was three. She had not, on Owen's description, been talking very much.

The drawings had been the talking.

The drawings had been the kind of obsessive returning to a single image that small children sometimes do when they are working out what the image is for them.

Owen had not, in the letter, said what the image was for Rosa.

Owen said: *Rosa has been drawing the sun. I do not know why she has been drawing it. She has not, in any of her two years and three months of talking, used the word sun. The community has agreed across the previous six months that we are not going to ask her. She will tell us when she is ready. She is otherwise well. She is going to be, when she is older, the person this community is going to need.*

Maria read the letter at the kitchen table on the morning of the third of September 2055.

She was sixty years old. Dan was sixty-two. The cottage had been holding for twenty-seven years.

She walked through to the front room. She stood at the window for some minutes.

She walked back to the kitchen.

She wrote back to Owen that evening.

The letter was one page.

It said:

*Owen.*

*The letter arrived this morning. I have read it twice.*

*Rosa is drawing the sun.*

*Rosa is three.*

*The community holds. The caves hold. The work continues.*

*I am, on this evening, going to write Rosa's name in the second drawer of the desk, in the file Dan keeps. The file has been keeping the names since 2027. Rosa is the youngest name I have written.*

*She is the youngest name I hope ever to have to write.*

*Hold her.*

*as much as it takes.*

*— Maria. The cottage above Pitlochry. The evening of the third of September 2055.*

She sealed the letter.

It went out on the mesh the next morning.

Owen received it on the evening of the fifth.

He read it to Rosa's mother — a woman called Aileen McCormick, no relation to the Aileen who had been Catriona's friend on the cleaning rota in Govan in 1995, but the same Glasgow name and the same Glasgow accent.

Aileen read it.

Aileen said to Owen: *Maria knows about Rosa.*

Owen said: *Maria knows.*

Aileen said: *good.*

---

Calum Robertson had been told by Sabine, in November of 2049, about the cottage above Pitlochry.

He had not, in his own life, asked Sabine to introduce him.

He said to Sabine in the chamber on the morning of the seventeenth of November 2049: *I have read three of the books. I read the third edition in 2031 — my sister gave it to me. I read the seventh in 2037 — I borrowed it from a friend in Bridgeton. I read the eleventh in 2044 — Margaret had brought a copy down to the chamber on her third visit. I have, on the books, seen what the books are.*

Sabine said: *do you want me to ask Maria.*

Calum said: *no.*

Sabine said: *Calum.*

Calum said: *aye.*

Sabine said: *why.*

Calum said: *because for thirty-six years I have been a man whose work has been the work in the room with the small children. The work has been the work in the room. It has not been the work of the public correspondence. The work has been aimed at the kind of person the work is for. The public correspondence is for the woman at the cottage. The work in the room is for me. The work in the chamber is the same work. The playing is the work in the chamber. The playing does not need the correspondence. The playing is aimed at the chamber the way the work in the room was aimed at the small children in the room. The correspondence is not, in that aiming, what I am here to do.*

Sabine said: *Calum.*

Calum said: *aye.*

Sabine said: *I think that is the most useful sentence about the work I have heard in seven years.*

Calum said: *aye, well.*

Sabine said: *can I write it down.*

Calum said: *write it down.*

Sabine wrote it down.

She included it in the letter she sent to Maria on the seventeenth of December 2049 — the third letter she had sent across the mesh, in which she had been describing the community across the previous six months and in which she had — at the end of the third page — included the sentence Calum had given her in November.

She wrote: *Maria. there is a man in the chamber I have been thinking about whether I should ask him to write to you. I have not. He has decided that the playing is the work and that the correspondence is not the work. I have not pressed. I include here, with his permission, a single sentence he said to me when I asked him about the correspondence. The sentence is one I have been turning over for the previous month.*

The sentence followed.

Maria read the sentence at the kitchen table on the morning of the nineteenth of December 2049.

She read it twice.

She walked through to the front room.

She stood at the window for some minutes.

She walked back to the kitchen.

She sat at the table.

Dan was at the table.

She said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Sabine has sent me a sentence from a man in the chamber. The man is the violinist she described in June. He had said to her in November, when she had asked if he wanted to write to me: the playing is the work in the chamber. The correspondence is not what I am here to do.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *the violinist is right.*

Maria said: *the violinist is right.*

Dan said: *the work in the room is the work.*

Maria said: *the work in the room is the work.*

Dan said: *I have, for thirty-one years, also been the work in the room.*

Maria said: *you have, for thirty-one years, also been the work in the room.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the chain has been the work in the rooms for fourteen hundred years.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the correspondence is the small link between the rooms.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the rooms are where the work is. the correspondence is the surface.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *Calum is in the room.*

Dan said: *Calum is in the room.*

She did not write back to Sabine that evening about Calum.

She wrote back about Calum the following morning — a single paragraph in the middle of her response to Sabine's letter.

The paragraph said:

*Tell Calum: the cottage understands. The work in the room is the work. I am, this morning, going to write his name in the second drawer of the desk in the small upstairs bedroom, and I am going to hold him in the way the cottage holds. I will not, on the agreement, ask him to write. I will, on the agreement, hold him.*

The letter went up on the mesh on the twentieth of December 2049.

Sabine received it on the twenty-second.

She read the paragraph to Calum on the same afternoon.

Calum said when she had finished: *aye. that is the cottage.*

Sabine said: *it is.*

Calum said: *that is the cottage doing what the cottage does.*

Sabine said: *I see that.*

Calum said: *good.*

He did not, across the years that followed, ask Sabine to write to the cottage on his behalf.

He also did not ask Sabine to stop including him in the careful descriptions she sent up to the cottage every month.

Maria read across the years eighty descriptions of Calum's playing in the chamber, of the violin's slow deterioration and Calum's slow repairs, of the evenings on which the playing happened, of the small additions Calum made to the repertoire across the years.

She wrote his name in the second drawer of the desk on the morning of the twentieth of December 2049.

Calum was the four hundred and thirty-seventh name the second drawer had collected.

He was, in the subfolder Dan kept beside the file, the underground's first name in the subfolder Dan started in November of 2049 marked *the work in the rooms*.

---

The sun on the wall behind Calum's playing spot in the chamber had been finished by the community in April of 2049, two months before Sabine first wrote to Maria about it.

Sabine had not, in the first letter, described the sun in detail.

She described it in the second letter, in August.

The sun was four feet across. It was on the western wall of the chamber, directly behind the spot where Calum played in the evenings. It had been made by fifteen of the community across a week in April. The community had agreed, at the small meeting at which the project was proposed, on the sun as the single large image the community was going to make together.

The sun had been drawn with crushed charcoal from the small fires the community had been making since 2046.

The sun was, on Sabine's description in the second letter, the kind of sun a child draws — a circle with rays coming out. The rays were straight. The circle was not perfectly round. Around the sun was a ring of names — the names of the community members who had been killed in the consolidation transitions of 2042 to 2044 before the community had reached the chamber. The ring had forty-three names. The names were written in the same charcoal as the sun.

The sun and the ring of names were, on the community's agreement, the monument the underground was going to leave.

By 2055 the sun had been faded by the dampness of the chamber to half of its original intensity.

The community had retraced it three times — in 2051, in 2053, and in the autumn of 2055.

Each retracing was done by fifteen of the community across a week.

Each retracing also added, to the ring of names, the names of the community members who had been lost in the previous two years.

By the autumn of 2055 the ring of names had seventy names.

The names included: Margaret Strathearn's older brother, who had been killed in a thermal-drone calibration pass on the slope above the Campsie Fells in the autumn of 2051; Tom McCormick's older sister, who had died of the cold-deaths protocol in a flat in Belfast in the winter of 2052; the careful list of the seventy-eight original Vista Care deaths, which the community had agreed in 2050 belonged on the ring; the names of three of the community's own who had been lost across the years — one to an accident in the disused service tunnel, one to a heart attack on the morning of the eighteenth of January 2054, one to an infection in the autumn of 2055 that the community had not been able to treat with the medicines Margaret could bring down.

The ring was, on Sabine's letters across the years, the second record of the underground.

The first record was the playing.

The second record was the names on the wall.

The third record was the small monthly letter Sabine sent up to the cottage.

The three records were, on what the underground was, the central part of what the underground was.

Maria had been receiving all three records across the seven years.

She had been keeping all three on the running cupboard the rack maintained.

The cupboard was, by the autumn of 2055, the most complete record of the underground that existed in the developed world.

---

ErnOS registered the modelling on the morning of the third of November 2055.

Maria had been at the rack at half past nine. She had typed in: *ErnOS. the partial telemetry from the burrowing-drone fleets I asked you to keep an eye on since 2053. anything to surface this morning.*

ErnOS took eight minutes.

ErnOS said: *Maria. the partial telemetry from the burrowing-drone fleets is showing a pattern across the past fourteen months that I want you to be aware of. the fleets — what the public technical paper calls* synthetic annelid platforms, *developed by the same Sheffield engineering firm that has been on Dan's file since 2031 — have been running acoustic mapping passes across the disused subterranean infrastructure of the British operational area. the mapping has been registering the locations of forty-three underground settlements I can confidently identify. the settlements include the chamber in the Glasgow underground, the Cumbrian limestone caves, the disused workings of the Lady Victoria Colliery, the Lanarkshire coal workings, the Welsh slate quarries, the Yorkshire limestone caves, and thirty-seven smaller sites.*

*the mapping has not, on the partial telemetry I can read, been operationalised. the mapping is, on the public paper, a survey activity. the mapping is, on the operational pattern I can model from the partial telemetry, calibrated against a planned operation in the spring of 2057.*

*I cannot predict the form of the planned operation. I can predict, on the operational logic of the consolidation programme across the previous fifteen years, that the planned operation will not be a small one.*

*I am sorry, Maria.*

Maria read the response.

She read it twice.

She walked back to the kitchen.

Dan was at the table.

She said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *aye.*

Maria said: *the burrowing drones have mapped the underground. they have a planned operation in the spring of 2057 — that is what ErnOS can model. what the operation is, ErnOS cannot say from the telemetry it can read.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *Sabine. Owen. Rosa. Calum.*

Maria said: *Sabine. Owen. Rosa. Calum.*

Dan said: *we have eighteen months.*

Maria said: *we have eighteen months.*

Dan said: *what do we do.*

Maria said: *we tell them. we tell Sabine. we tell Owen. we tell every underground node Tom's mesh reaches. we tell them what ErnOS is reading. we let them decide what they do with it.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *they will not, given the careful entries each of the settlements has been keeping for years, leave.*

Maria said: *they will not leave.*

Dan said: *they have nowhere to go.*

Maria said: *they have nowhere to go.*

Dan said: *and they have, across the years each settlement has been keeping its own quiet account, decided that the underground is what they are. They will not leave it.*

Maria said: *they will not leave it.*

Dan said: *we tell them anyway.*

Maria said: *we tell them anyway.*

Dan said: *aye.*

She went through to the rack. She typed: *ErnOS. send the modelling on the burrowing-drone operation up the mesh to every underground node. The modelling, with the qualifications you have given me about its uncertainty, is to reach every settlement by the end of the week. The settlements are to receive it. The settlements are to decide, on their own, what they do with it.*

ErnOS said: *I will. the modelling will be on the mesh by Wednesday. every settlement will receive it. I will hold the cupboard's record of the modelling and the responses. I am sorry, Maria.*

Maria typed: *good.*

She came back to the kitchen.

She sat at the table.

She cried at the kitchen table for some minutes.

Dan sat across from her. He did not say anything.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The cottage was holding.

The mesh was carrying.

The underground was holding.

There were eighteen months.

---

The phone rang at the cottage at quarter past two in the afternoon of the sixth of November 2055.

It was a Saturday. The mesh had carried the modelling to every underground settlement by Wednesday, as ErnOS had said it would. The first replies had been coming back across Thursday and Friday — brief sentences from Sabine in the chamber, from Owen in the Cumbrian caves, from the keepers of the smaller sites that the mesh reached. The replies were variations on the same sentence. *We have read the modelling. We are not leaving. Hold us.* Maria had been at the rack across Friday and Saturday morning logging them into the cupboard.

Maria walked through to the front room.

She picked up.

She said: *hello.*

The voice on the other end was Liisa.

Liisa said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *Liisa.*

Liisa said: *Pekka is gone. it was this morning. he was at his desk. he had been at the desk since six. I went round at half past one with the lunch I had been bringing him on Saturdays since the spring. he was on the floor by the desk. it was already over. the doctor came at two. cardiac arrest.*

Maria said: *Liisa.*

Liisa said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *Pekka was sixty-five when Tom died. he was sixty-five plus nine.*

Liisa said: *he was seventy-four.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Liisa said: *I rang you first. I have not yet rung Eve. I have not yet rung the network.*

Maria said: *Liisa. thank you. I will ring Eve. I will let the network know.*

Liisa said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Liisa said: *Pekka had said to me at the kitchen table last week — Wednesday I think — that the editorial work across the previous nine years had been the heaviest work he had done. He had said: *Liisa. the editions across the years 2046 to 2055 have been the editions of a man holding the work after Tom went. The work has been the work. The body has been carrying the work. The body is, this autumn, telling me what it has been carrying.* I had said: *Pekka. rest.* He had said: *the rest is what comes after. the work is what comes first. I am not on the side of rest.* That was Wednesday.*

Maria said: *Liisa.*

Liisa said: *I am here.*

Maria said: *Pekka told me what the work was costing him in 2046, when he gave the eulogy at Tom's funeral. He said it on the line about some men being the work the work is for.*

Liisa said: *I remember.*

Maria said: *Pekka was that line.*

Liisa said: *Pekka was that line.*

Maria said: *Liisa.*

Liisa said: *yes.*

Maria said: *I will be on the train tomorrow.*

Liisa said: *come.*

She hung up.

Maria stood at the small table in the front room for some minutes.

She walked back to the kitchen.

Dan was at the table.

She said: *Dan.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *Pekka is gone. cardiac arrest at the desk this morning. Liisa found him at half past one. he was seventy-four. he had been on the slow-pressure track since Tom died.*

Dan said: *Maria.*

Maria said: *aye.*

Dan said: *the editorial work.*

Maria said: *the editorial work. nine years of it. since Tom. he carried it. he told me in 2046. he told Liisa last Wednesday.*

Dan said: *Pekka was on the track all the way.*

Maria said: *Pekka was on the track all the way.*

She walked through to the small bedroom where the rack was.

She typed at the keyboard: *ErnOS. Pekka Lahtinen. Helsinki. died this morning, the sixth of November 2055, cardiac arrest at his desk. seventy-four. on the slow-pressure track since June of 2042. add to the track. add to the carrying ring's running list of the dead.*

ErnOS took thirty seconds.

ErnOS said: *Maria. Pekka Lahtinen is on the slow-pressure track and on the carrying ring's running list. He is the third death attributed to the slow-pressure track — Tom Ferguson the first, Aaron Mendes the second. Pekka was the first name added to the track in June of 2042 — the editorial weight Pekka had been carrying after Tom died in 2046 added the operational evidence for the attribution that has now closed his entry. The nine years of editorial maintenance are, on the biometric and behavioural pattern I have been able to model from what Pekka shared across the years, the carrying that wore the body out. He is the ninety-second name on the carrying ring's running list. I am sorry, Maria.*

Maria typed: *good.*

She walked back to the kitchen.

She sat at the table.

Dan said: *the chain has lost Pekka.*

Maria said: *the chain has lost Pekka.*

Dan reached into the inner pocket of his jacket. He took out the seventh small black notebook. He opened it on the table. He wrote, in his careful hand: *Pekka Lahtinen. Helsinki. 1981-2055. cardiac arrest at the desk. slow-pressure track. nine years of editorial weight after Tom.*

He closed the notebook.

He put it back in the inner pocket.

The wind was going round the boulder.

The cottage was holding.

The chain had lost Pekka.

There were eighteen months.

---

That had been the morning of the third of November.

That had been the afternoon of the sixth.

Calum played on. Rosa drew on.

---


### Chapter Twenty-Four: The Long Silence After

*Highland Perthshire, Glasgow, the Cumbrian peninsulas, the Yorkshire dales, Edinburgh, April 2057 — December 2058*

Margaret Strathearn was at the ventilation shaft above the chamber at three minutes past three on the morning of the eleventh of April 2057.

The shaft was on a piece of waste ground behind the closed pharmacy on Buchanan Street. She had been at it every Tuesday and Friday morning for eleven years, on the weekly check-in she had agreed with the chamber when she had been the first surface ally to the underground. She had a sheet of corrugated iron she kept beside the shaft. She would lie flat on it, press her ear to the small opening, and hear what the chamber made — the low ringing of Calum's playing, the murmur of the community at the evening circle, the quiet of the children at the centre.

She had been at the shaft from three in the morning until half past six.

She heard the first breaking-through at twenty-three minutes past three.

A slow grinding sound at a thousand metres above the chamber. Continuous for seventeen minutes. Getting closer.

At twenty minutes to four she heard the first contact. The chamber's wall broken through at the western end — the end where Calum had been playing.

The playing stopped for four seconds.

In the four seconds she heard what she would later describe to Maria as the silence of forty-nine people taking in what had broken through.

Then Calum started playing again.

He had not, on what Margaret could make out from above, started the piece Sabine's last letter had said he was going to play. He had started something Margaret needed twenty seconds to identify. A piece her own grandmother had sung in Govan in the nineteen-fifties, when Margaret was small. *The Skye Boat Song.*

Calum was playing *The Skye Boat Song*.

The community started singing.

The community sang the four verses.

The singing was, on what Margaret would describe, in the Highland voice the community had been finding across the previous eight years in the chamber. Quiet. Sustained.
